In contrast, human assembly naturally couples structural reasoning with holistic shape inference. Inspired by this intuition, we reformulate 3D assembly as a joint problem of assembly and generation. We show that these two processes are mutually reinforcing: assembly provides part-level structural priors for generation, while generation injects holistic shape context that resolves ambiguities in assembly. Unlike prior methods that cannot synthesize missing geometry, we propose CRAG, which simultaneously generates plausible complete shapes and predicts poses for input parts. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across in-the-wild objects with diverse geometries, varying part counts, and missing pieces. Our code and models will be released.

</details>


### [28] [QuadSync: Quadrifocal Tensor Synchronization via Tucker Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22639)
*Daniel Miao,Gilad Lerman,Joe Kileel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We present a framework to recover n cameras from block quadrifocal tensors via a Tucker decomposition, enabling synchronization of quadrifocal tensors and joint handling with trifocal/bifocal tensors; using ADMM and IRLS with empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadrifocal information offers richer geometric constraints than pairwise essential matrices, but practical use has been limited; a tractable framework for recovering multiple cameras from quadrifocal data is needed.

Method: Form the block quadrifocal tensor, prove it admits a Tucker decomposition whose factor matrices are the stacked camera matrices, achieving multilinear rank (4,4,4,4) independent of n; develop a synchronization algorithm using Tucker decomposition, ADMM, and IRLS; derive relations to trifocal and bifocal tensors and propose a joint synchronization algorithm.

Result: Theoretical result: multilinear rank and decomposition exist; algorithm for quadrifocal-based synchronization is developed; joint synchronization with trifocal/bifocal tensors is established; numerical experiments on modern datasets demonstrate effectiveness.

Conclusion: Higher-order information via block quadrifocal tensors can enable accurate and scalable multi-camera recovery, with practical synchronization algorithms and links to lower-order tensors, suggesting significant potential for SfM.

Abstract: In structure from motion, quadrifocal tensors capture more information than their pairwise counterparts (essential matrices), yet they have often been thought of as impractical and only of theoretical interest. In this work, we challenge such beliefs by providing a new framework to recover $n$ cameras from the corresponding collection of quadrifocal tensors. We form the block quadrifocal tensor and show that it admits a Tucker decomposition whose factor matrices are the stacked camera matrices, and which thus has a multilinear rank of (4,~4,~4,~4) independent of $n$. We develop the first synchronization algorithm for quadrifocal tensors, using Tucker decomposition, alternating direction method of multipliers, and iteratively reweighted least squares. We further establish relationships between the block quadrifocal, trifocal, and bifocal tensors, and introduce an algorithm that jointly synchronizes these three entities. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods on modern datasets, indicating the potential and importance of using higher-order information in synchronization.

</details>


### [29] [Plug, Play, and Fortify: A Low-Cost Module for Robust Multimodal Image Understanding Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22644)
*Siqi Lu,Wanying Xu,Yongbin Zheng,Wenting Luan,Peng Sun,Jianhang Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MWAM uses frequency-domain analysis to detect modality dominance with a Frequency Ratio Metric (FRM) and applies a plug-and-play Multimodal Weight Allocation Module to re-balance contributions from each modality during training, improving robustness to missing modalities across backbones and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Missing modalities cause catastrophic performance drops due to imbalanced optimization where some modalities dominate learning. A simple, efficient mechanism is needed to rebalance training contributions.

Method: Compute a Frequency Ratio Metric (FRM) to quantify modality preference in the frequency domain. Use FRM to guide a Multimodal Weight Allocation Module (MWAM) that dynamically re-weights each modality branch during training. MWAM is plug-and-play and compatible with CNN- and ViT-based backbones.

Result: MWAM provides consistent performance gains across diverse tasks and modality combinations, and enhances state-of-the-art methods tackling missing modality problems when integrated.

Conclusion: FRM-guided MWAM offers an architecture-agnostic, efficient solution to modality fragility under missing modalities, improving holistic modality utilization and robustness in multimodal learning.

Abstract: Missing modalities present a fundamental challenge in multimodal models, often causing catastrophic performance degradation. Our observations suggest that this fragility stems from an imbalanced learning process, where the model develops an implicit preference for certain modalities, leading to the under-optimization of others. We propose a simple yet efficient method to address this challenge. The central insight of our work is that the dominance relationship between modalities can be effectively discerned and quantified in the frequency domain. To leverage this principle, we first introduce a Frequency Ratio Metric (FRM) to quantify modality preference by analyzing features in the frequency domain. Guided by FRM, we then propose a Multimodal Weight Allocation Module, a plug-and-play component that dynamically re-balances the contribution of each branch during training, promoting a more holistic learning paradigm. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MWAM can be seamlessly integrated into diverse architectural backbones, such as those based on CNNs and ViTs. Furthermore, MWAM delivers consistent performance gains across a wide range of tasks and modality combinations. This advancement extends beyond merely optimizing the performance of the base model; it also manifests as further performance improvements to state-of-the-art methods addressing the missing modality problem.

</details>


### [30] [Interactive Medical-SAM2 GUI: A Napari-based semi-automatic annotation tool for medical images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22649)
*Woojae Hong,Jong Ha Hwang,Jiyong Chung,Joongyeon Choi,Hyunngun Kim,Yong Hwy Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Napari-based open-source tool for semi-automatic 3D medical image annotation using Medical-SAM2, unifying navigation, propagation, correction, and export in a local workflow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Voxel-level annotation in 3D medical imaging remains slow and expensive; existing tools often separate per-slice interaction from a cohesive cohort-oriented workflow. A local, unified pipeline is needed to annotate across multiple studies with efficient propagation and export while preserving geometry.

Method: Python Napari GUI that treats 3D volumes as slice sequences and integrates box/point prompting with SAM2-style propagation (Medical-SAM2) for 3D annotation. Features include first/last-slice initialization, object initialization via box prompts, refinement with point prompts, prompt-first correction, and single-root-folder cohort workflow. Supports DICOM/NIfTI, optional N4 bias-field correction, SimpleITK for geometry, per-object volumetry, 3D rendering, and export. Implemented for research annotation workflows; open-source at GitHub.

Result: Introduces a local-first, cohort-oriented 3D annotation workflow that leverages SAM2 propagation within Napari to enable efficient, voxel-level labeling across studies, with comprehensive export and geometry-preserving capabilities.

Conclusion: Presents a practical, research-focused tool that consolidates annotation, propagation, correction, and export in a single local pipeline, leveraging Medical-SAM2 within Napari for streamlined 3D medical image annotation.

Abstract: Interactive Medical-SAM2 GUI is an open-source desktop application for semi-automatic annotation of 2D and 3D medical images. Built on the Napari multi-dimensional viewer, box/point prompting is integrated with SAM2-style propagation by treating a 3D volume as a slice sequence, enabling mask propagation from sparse prompts using Medical-SAM2 on top of SAM2. Voxel-level annotation remains essential for developing and validating medical imaging algorithms, yet manual labeling is slow and expensive for 3D scans, and existing integrations frequently emphasize per-slice interaction without providing a unified, cohort-oriented workflow for navigation, propagation, interactive correction, and quantitative export in a single local pipeline. To address this practical limitation, a local-first Napari workflow is provided for efficient 3D annotation across multiple studies using standard DICOM series and/or NIfTI volumes. Users can annotate cases sequentially under a single root folder with explicit proceed/skip actions, initialize objects via box-first prompting (including first/last-slice initialization for single-object propagation), refine predictions with point prompts, and finalize labels through prompt-first correction prior to saving. During export, per-object volumetry and 3D volume rendering are supported, and image geometry is preserved via SimpleITK. The GUI is implemented in Python using Napari and PyTorch, with optional N4 bias-field correction, and is intended exclusively for research annotation workflows. The code is released on the project page: https://github.com/SKKU-IBE/Medical-SAM2GUI/.

</details>


### [31] [Denoising as Path Planning: Training-Free Acceleration of Diffusion Models with DPCache](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22654)
*Bowen Cui,Yuanbin Wang,Huajiang Xu,Biaolong Chen,Aixi Zhang,Hao Jiang,Zhengzheng Jin,Xu Liu,Pipei Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DPCache treats diffusion sampling acceleration as a global path planning problem, using a Path-Aware Cost Tensor and dynamic programming to select key timesteps, enabling training-free speedups with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome error accumulation and artifacts from ignoring global structure in existing fixed/local schedules and improve inference efficiency without training.

Method: Construct Path-Aware Cost Tensor from a calibration set to quantify the path-dependent error of skipping timesteps conditioned on the preceding key timestep; use dynamic programming to choose an optimal sequence of key timesteps that minimize total path cost; during inference, perform full computations only at key timesteps and predict intermediates via cached features.

Result: Experiments on DiT, FLUX, and HunyuanVideo show acceleration up to 4.87x with +0.031 ImageReward relative to the baseline; in FLUX, it even surpasses the full-step baseline by +0.028 ImageReward at 3.54x speedup.

Conclusion: Path-aware global scheduling effectively balances speed and quality and outperforms prior acceleration methods.

Abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable success in image and video generation, yet their practical deployment remains hindered by the substantial computational overhead of multi-step iterative sampling. Among acceleration strategies, caching-based methods offer a training-free and effective solution by reusing or predicting features across timesteps. However, existing approaches rely on fixed or locally adaptive schedules without considering the global structure of the denoising trajectory, often leading to error accumulation and visual artifacts. To overcome this limitation, we propose DPCache, a novel training-free acceleration framework that formulates diffusion sampling acceleration as a global path planning problem. DPCache constructs a Path-Aware Cost Tensor from a small calibration set to quantify the path-dependent error of skipping timesteps conditioned on the preceding key timestep. Leveraging this tensor, DPCache employs dynamic programming to select an optimal sequence of key timesteps that minimizes the total path cost while preserving trajectory fidelity. During inference, the model performs full computations only at these key timesteps, while intermediate outputs are efficiently predicted using cached features. Extensive experiments on DiT, FLUX, and HunyuanVideo demonstrate that DPCache achieves strong acceleration with minimal quality loss, outperforming prior acceleration methods by $+$0.031 ImageReward at 4.87$\times$ speedup and even surpassing the full-step baseline by $+$0.028 ImageReward at 3.54$\times$ speedup on FLUX, validating the effectiveness of our path-aware global scheduling framework. Code will be released at https://github.com/argsss/DPCache.

</details>


### [32] [WaterVideoQA: ASV-Centric Perception and Rule-Compliant Reasoning via Multi-Modal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22923)
*Runwei Guan,Shaofeng Liang,Ningwei Ouyang,Weichen Fei,Shanliang Yao,Wei Dai,Chenhao Ge,Penglei Sun,Xiaohui Zhu,Tao Huang,Ryan Wen Liu,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes WaterVideoQA, a large-scale video QA benchmark for all-waterway environments, and NaviMind, a multi-agent neuro-symbolic system for open-ended maritime reasoning; together they push ASVs toward interpretable, regulation-compliant decision-making and outperform baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges the gap between perception and knowledge-driven cognition in autonomous surface vessels. The maritime domain demands interactive environmental understanding under dynamic conditions, which current perception benchmarks and pattern-matching methods fail to address.

Method: WaterVideoQA: 3,029 video clips across six waterway categories, with variable lighting/weather, designed to stress-test ASV reasoning within a five-tier hierarchical cognitive framework. NaviMind: a multi-agent architecture leveraging Adaptive Semantic Routing, Situation-Aware Hierarchical Reasoning, and Autonomous Self-Reflective Verification to enable interpretable, regulation-compliant decisions.

Result: Experimental results show significant performance gains over existing baselines, establishing a new paradigm for intelligent, trustworthy interaction in dynamic maritime environments.

Conclusion: The work introduces both a challenging, comprehensive benchmark and a capable neuro-symbolic system, advancing ASV capabilities from perception to robust, interpretable reasoning and compliant decision-making.

Abstract: While autonomous navigation has achieved remarkable success in passive perception (e.g., object detection and segmentation), it remains fundamentally constrained by a void in knowledge-driven, interactive environmental cognition. In the high-stakes domain of maritime navigation, the ability to bridge the gap between raw visual perception and complex cognitive reasoning is not merely an enhancement but a critical prerequisite for Autonomous Surface Vessels to execute safe and precise maneuvers. To this end, we present WaterVideoQA, the first large-scale, comprehensive Video Question Answering benchmark specifically engineered for all-waterway environments. This benchmark encompasses 3,029 video clips across six distinct waterway categories, integrating multifaceted variables such as volatile lighting and dynamic weather to rigorously stress-test ASV capabilities across a five-tier hierarchical cognitive framework. Furthermore, we introduce NaviMind, a pioneering multi-agent neuro-symbolic system designed for open-ended maritime reasoning. By synergizing Adaptive Semantic Routing, Situation-Aware Hierarchical Reasoning, and Autonomous Self-Reflective Verification, NaviMind transitions ASVs from superficial pattern matching to regulation-compliant, interpretable decision-making. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly transcends existing baselines, establishing a new paradigm for intelligent, trustworthy interaction in dynamic maritime environments.

</details>


### [33] [Scaling Audio-Visual Quality Assessment Dataset via Crowdsourcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22659)
*Renyu Yang,Jian Jin,Lili Meng,Meiqin Liu,Yilin Wang,Balu Adsumilli,Weisi Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes scalable crowdsourced AVQA data construction and introduces YT-NTU-AVQ, the largest AVQA dataset to date (1,620 A/V sequences) with richer annotations and a crowdsourced evaluation framework; dataset and code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AVQA datasets are small, lack content diversity, and have single overall scores, limiting model development and multimodal perception research.

Method: Develop a crowdsourced subjective experiment framework for AVQA; implement systematic data preparation to cover wide quality levels and semantic scenarios; extend data with additional annotations; validate via YT-NTU-AVQ.

Result: YT-NTU-AVQ dataset consists of 1,620 user-generated A/V sequences; it is the largest and most diverse AVQA dataset to date; accompanying platform code released on GitHub.

Conclusion: The approach enables broader AVQA research and multimodal perception studies; the dataset and platform are publicly available to support future work.

Abstract: Audio-visual quality assessment (AVQA) research has been stalled by limitations of existing datasets: they are typically small in scale, with insufficient diversity in content and quality, and annotated only with overall scores. These shortcomings provide limited support for model development and multimodal perception research. We propose a practical approach for AVQA dataset construction. First, we design a crowdsourced subjective experiment framework for AVQA, breaks the constraints of in-lab settings and achieves reliable annotation across varied environments. Second, a systematic data preparation strategy is further employed to ensure broad coverage of both quality levels and semantic scenarios. Third, we extend the dataset with additional annotations, enabling research on multimodal perception mechanisms and their relation to content. Finally, we validate this approach through YT-NTU-AVQ, the largest and most diverse AVQA dataset to date, consisting of 1,620 user-generated audio and video (A/V) sequences. The dataset and platform code are available at https://github.com/renyu12/YT-NTU-AVQ

</details>


### [34] [GeoWorld: Geometric World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23058)
*Zeyu Zhang,Danning Li,Ian Reid,Richard Hartley*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoWorld uses hyperbolic latent representations via Hyperbolic JEPA to preserve geometric/hierarchical structure and enable stable, long-horizon planning in hyperbolic latent space; shows modest SR improvements over V-JEPA 2 on cross-task/vision-language planning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two key challenges in energy-based predictive world models: (i) Euclidean latent spaces neglect underlying geometric and hierarchical state structure, and (ii) poor long-horizon prediction leading to degradation during extended rollouts.

Method: Introduce Hyperbolic JEPA to map latent representations from Euclidean space onto hyperbolic manifolds and develop Geometric Reinforcement Learning for energy-based optimization, enabling stable multi-step planning in hyperbolic latent space. Empirically evaluate on CrossTask and COIN against the state-of-the-art V-JEPA 2.

Result: Geometric latent representations and hyperbolic planning yield improvements in planning accuracy, reporting approximately 3 percentage points SR improvement in 3-step planning and about 2 percentage points in 4-step planning compared to V-JEPA 2.

Conclusion: Geometric/Hyerbolic latent structures and geometry-aware planning enhance stability and performance for energy-based predictive world models, particularly for short-to-medium horizon planning tasks; demonstrates the effectiveness of hyperbolic geometry for capturing hierarchical relations in latent dynamics.

Abstract: Energy-based predictive world models provide a powerful approach for multi-step visual planning by reasoning over latent energy landscapes rather than generating pixels. However, existing approaches face two major challenges: (i) their latent representations are typically learned in Euclidean space, neglecting the underlying geometric and hierarchical structure among states, and (ii) they struggle with long-horizon prediction, which leads to rapid degradation across extended rollouts. To address these challenges, we introduce GeoWorld, a geometric world model that preserves geometric structure and hierarchical relations through a Hyperbolic JEPA, which maps latent representations from Euclidean space onto hyperbolic manifolds. We further introduce Geometric Reinforcement Learning for energy-based optimization, enabling stable multi-step planning in hyperbolic latent space. Extensive experiments on CrossTask and COIN demonstrate around 3% SR improvement in 3-step planning and 2% SR improvement in 4-step planning compared to the state-of-the-art V-JEPA 2. Project website: https://steve-zeyu-zhang.github.io/GeoWorld.

</details>


### [35] [ArtPro: Self-Supervised Articulated Object Reconstruction with Adaptive Integration of Mobility Proposals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22666)
*Xuelu Li,Zhaonan Wang,Xiaogang Wang,Lei Wu,Manyi Li,Changhe Tu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ArtPro proposes an adaptive, self-supervised pipeline that starts from over-segmentation and iteratively merges mobility proposals using motion-consistency and collision-aware pruning to robustly reconstruct articulated objects, outperforming prior differentiable-rendering-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses sensitivity to initialization and local minima in self-supervised articulated reconstruction, particularly for complex multi-part objects; traditional clustering or pre-trained priors often fail to capture correct articulation.

Method: Begin with geometry- and motion-prior-guided over-segmentation to generate plausible part proposals with motion hypotheses. During optimization, dynamically merge proposals by analyzing motion consistency among neighboring parts, and apply collision-aware motion pruning to prevent incorrect kinematic estimates.

Result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world objects show ArtPro achieves robust reconstruction of complex multi-part objects, with significant improvements in accuracy and stability compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: ArtPro demonstrates that adaptive integration of mobility proposals enables robust, scalable self-supervised articulated object reconstruction, outperforming existing approaches and with potential for broader digital-twin applications.

Abstract: Reconstructing articulated objects into high-fidelity digital twins is crucial for applications such as robotic manipulation and interactive simulation. Recent self-supervised methods using differentiable rendering frameworks like 3D Gaussian Splatting remain highly sensitive to the initial part segmentation. Their reliance on heuristic clustering or pre-trained models often causes optimization to converge to local minima, especially for complex multi-part objects. To address these limitations, we propose ArtPro, a novel self-supervised framework that introduces adaptive integration of mobility proposals. Our approach begins with an over-segmentation initialization guided by geometry features and motion priors, generating part proposals with plausible motion hypotheses. During optimization, we dynamically merge these proposals by analyzing motion consistency among spatial neighbors, while a collision-aware motion pruning mechanism prevents erroneous kinematic estimation. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world objects demonstrate that ArtPro achieves robust reconstruction of complex multi-part objects, significantly outperforming existing methods in accuracy and stability.

</details>


### [36] [Monocular Open Vocabulary Occupancy Prediction for Indoor Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22667)
*Changqing Zhou,Yueru Luo,Han Zhang,Zeyu Jiang,Changhao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geometry-only open-vocabulary 3D occupancy for indoor scenes using 3D Language-Embedded Gaussians, with opacity-aware Poisson-based occupancy aggregation and a Progressive Temperature Decay strategy for semantic alignment; achieves state-of-the-art IoU and mIoU on Occ-ScanNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor environments feature dense geometry and fine-grained semantics that exceed fixed taxonomies; outdoor open-vocabulary methods do not transfer well to indoor settings, necessitating a geometry-driven approach with language alignment.

Method: Binary occupancy supervision (occupied vs free) integrated with 3D Language-Embedded Gaussians as a unified representation. Introduces a stability-enhanced Gaussian-to-Occupancy operator via an opacity-aware Poisson-based volumetric aggregation. Addresses semantic alignment by a Progressive Temperature Decay schedule that gradually sharpens opacities during splatting to strengthen Gaussian-language alignment.

Result: On Occ-ScanNet, achieves 59.50 IoU and 21.05 mIoU in the open-vocabulary setting, surpassing all existing occupancy methods in IoU and outperforming prior open-vocabulary approaches by a large margin in mIoU. Code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of geometry-only supervision for indoor open-vocabulary occupancy, with stabilized geometry encoding and improved language alignment, setting strong baselines for future indoor open-vocabulary research.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D occupancy is vital for embodied agents, which need to understand complex indoor environments where semantic categories are abundant and evolve beyond fixed taxonomies. While recent work has explored open-vocabulary occupancy in outdoor driving scenarios, such methods transfer poorly indoors, where geometry is denser, layouts are more intricate, and semantics are far more fine-grained. To address these challenges, we adopt a geometry-only supervision paradigm that uses only binary occupancy labels (occupied vs free). Our framework builds upon 3D Language-Embedded Gaussians, which serve as a unified intermediate representation coupling fine-grained 3D geometry with a language-aligned semantic embedding. On the geometry side, we find that existing Gaussian-to-Occupancy operators fail to converge under such weak supervision, and we introduce an opacity-aware, Poisson-based approach that stabilizes volumetric aggregation. On the semantic side, direct alignment between rendered features and open-vocabulary segmentation features suffers from feature mixing; we therefore propose a Progressive Temperature Decay schedule that gradually sharpens opacities during splatting, strengthening Gaussian-language alignment. On Occ-ScanNet, our framework achieves 59.50 IoU and 21.05 mIoU in the open-vocabulary setting, surpassing all existing occupancy methods in IoU and outperforming prior open-vocabulary approaches by a large margin in mIoU. Code will be released at https://github.com/JuIvyy/LegoOcc.

</details>


### [37] [Latent Gaussian Splatting for 4D Panoptic Occupancy Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23172)
*Maximilian Luz,Rohit Mohan,Thomas Nürnberg,Yakov Miron,Daniele Cattaneo,Abhinav Valada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LaGS introduces latent Gaussian splatting for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking, fusing multi-view observations into 3D Gaussians and transferring to voxel grids for mask-based segmentation; achieves state-of-the-art results on Occ3D, nuScenes, and Waymo; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve holistic 4D scene understanding that jointly resolves geometry and temporal association, addressing limitations of existing methods that offer only coarse bounding boxes or static voxel occupancy without temporal consistency; requires efficient fusion of multi-view observations into a 3D latent representation.

Method: Proposes Latent Gaussian Splatting (LaGS): end-to-end camera tracking with mask-based multi-view panoptic occupancy prediction. Observations are fused into sparse 3D Gaussians as a latent scene representation, which are then splatted onto a 3D voxel grid and decoded by a mask-based segmentation head to produce panoptic occupancy.

Result: Evaluated on Occ3D nuScenes and Waymo, achieving state-of-the-art performance for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking.

Conclusion: LaGS delivers a unified, efficient framework for 4D panoptic occupancy by combining latent Gaussian fusion with voxel-based decoding, improving temporal-spatial understanding; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Capturing 4D spatiotemporal surroundings is crucial for the safe and reliable operation of robots in dynamic environments. However, most existing methods address only one side of the problem: they either provide coarse geometric tracking via bounding boxes, or detailed 3D structures like voxel-based occupancy that lack explicit temporal association. In this work, we present Latent Gaussian Splatting for 4D Panoptic Occupancy Tracking (LaGS) that advances spatiotemporal scene understanding in a holistic direction. Our approach incorporates camera-based end-to-end tracking with mask-based multi-view panoptic occupancy prediction, and addresses the key challenge of efficiently aggregating multi-view information into 3D voxel grids via a novel latent Gaussian splatting approach. Specifically, we first fuse observations into 3D Gaussians that serve as a sparse point-centric latent representation of the 3D scene, and then splat the aggregated features onto a 3D voxel grid that is decoded by a mask-based segmentation head. We evaluate LaGS on the Occ3D nuScenes and Waymo datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking. We make our code available at https://lags.cs.uni-freiburg.de/.

</details>


### [38] [SPMamba-YOLO: An Underwater Object Detection Network Based on Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement and Global Context Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22674)
*Guanghao Liao,Zhen Liu,Liyuan Cao,Yonghui Yang,Qi Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SPMamba-YOLO, a multi-component underwater detector that fuses SPPELAN, Pyramid Split Attention (PSA), and a Mamba-based state-space module to better handle small, densely distributed underwater objects; achieves notable gains on URPC2022 with favorable efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater imaging suffers from severe light attenuation, color distortion, clutter, and small object sizes. Effective multi-scale feature fusion and global context modeling are needed to improve robustness and accuracy.

Method: Proposes SPPELAN to strengthen multi-scale feature aggregation and enlarge receptive field; introduces PSA to emphasize informative regions and suppress background; embeds a Mamba-based state-space modeling module to capture long-range dependencies and global context; integrates these components into a YOLO-based architecture (SPMamba-YOLO) and evaluates on URPC2022.

Result: Outperforms YOLOv8n baseline by >4.9% mAP@0.5, with particular gains for small and densely distributed underwater objects, while maintaining a favorable balance between accuracy and computational cost.

Conclusion: The combination of SPPELAN, PSA, and Mamba-based state-space modeling provides robust improvements for underwater object detection, addressing core challenges and offering potential applicability to related domains; further ablations and efficiency analyses could strengthen the claims.

Abstract: Underwater object detection is a critical yet challenging research problem owing to severe light attenuation, color distortion, background clutter, and the small scale of underwater targets. To address these challenges, we propose SPMamba-YOLO, a novel underwater object detection network that integrates multi-scale feature enhancement with global context modeling. Specifically, a Spatial Pyramid Pooling Enhanced Layer Aggregation Network (SPPELAN) module is introduced to strengthen multi-scale feature aggregation and expand the receptive field, while a Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) mechanism enhances feature discrimination by emphasizing informative regions and suppressing background interference. In addition, a Mamba-based state space modeling module is incorporated to efficiently capture long-range dependencies and global contextual information, thereby improving detection robustness in complex underwater environments. Extensive experiments on the URPC2022 dataset demonstrate that SPMamba-YOLO outperforms the YOLOv8n baseline by more than 4.9\% in mAP@0.5, particularly for small and densely distributed underwater objects, while maintaining a favorable balance between detection accuracy and computational cost.

</details>


### [39] [Motion-aware Event Suppression for Event Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23204)
*Roberto Pellerito,Nico Messikommer,Giovanni Cioffi,Marco Cannici,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Motion-aware Event Suppression, a real-time framework that jointly segments IMOs and predicts their future motion to anticipatorily suppress dynamic events, achieving high efficiency and demonstrating downstream gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate noise from ego-motion and independent moving objects (IMOs) in event streams, enabling robust real-time perception and enabling downstream acceleration and accuracy improvements.

Method: A lightweight architecture that processes event streams to (1) segment IMOs in the current stream and (2) forecast their future motion for anticipatory suppression, achieving 173 Hz on consumer GPUs with <1 GB memory and improving EVIMO segmentation accuracy by 67% while increasing inference rate by 53%. It also leverages the suppression for downstream tasks.

Result: State-of-the-art improvements on the EVIMO benchmark: 67% gain in segmentation accuracy and 53% higher inference rate; downstream benefits include 83% faster Vision Transformer inference via token pruning and a 13% reduction in Absolute Trajectory Error for event-based visual odometry.

Conclusion: The work presents a novel real-time framework for motion-aware event suppression, demonstrating significant gains in both perception tasks and downstream applications, and highlighting the practicality of lightweight designs for event-camera pipelines.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce the first framework for Motion-aware Event Suppression, which learns to filter events triggered by IMOs and ego-motion in real time. Our model jointly segments IMOs in the current event stream while predicting their future motion, enabling anticipatory suppression of dynamic events before they occur. Our lightweight architecture achieves 173 Hz inference on consumer-grade GPUs with less than 1 GB of memory usage, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods on the challenging EVIMO benchmark by 67\% in segmentation accuracy while operating at a 53\% higher inference rate. Moreover, we demonstrate significant benefits for downstream applications: our method accelerates Vision Transformer inference by 83\% via token pruning and improves event-based visual odometry accuracy, reducing Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) by 13\%.

</details>


### [40] [ViCLIP-OT: The First Foundation Vision-Language Model for Vietnamese Image-Text Retrieval with Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22678)
*Quoc-Khang Tran,Minh-Thien Nguyen,Nguyen-Khang Pham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViCLIP-OT introduces a Vietnamese-focused vision-language model that combines CLIP-style contrastive learning with a similarity-graph regularized optimal transport (SIGROT) loss to improve cross-modal alignment. It achieves state-of-the-art results on Vietnamese benchmarks and zero-shot Crossmodal-3600, indicating improved modality alignment and reduced modality gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource language settings for image-text retrieval face data scarcity and a larger cross-modal modality gap. A scalable method is needed to improve cross-language cross-modal alignment and retrieval performance for Vietnamese.

Method: Integrates CLIP-style contrastive learning with a Similarity-Graph Regularized Optimal Transport (SIGROT) loss. Uses similarity graphs to regularize the transport-based alignment between image and text embeddings, enhancing global cross-modal consistency. Evaluated on UITOpenViIC, KTVIC, and Crossmodal-3600 with embedding-space analysis.

Result: ViCLIP-OT consistently outperforms CLIP and SigLIP baselines in both in-domain and zero-shot settings. On UIT-OpenViIC, average Recall@K = 67.34%, +5.75 pp over CLIP. In zero-shot on Crossmodal-3600, ViCLIP-OT exceeds CLIP by 11.72 pp. Embedding-space analysis confirms improved alignment and reduced modality gap.

Conclusion: The SIGROT integration provides an effective and scalable strategy for cross-modal retrieval in low-resource languages, with practical implications for Vietnamese and potentially other underrepresented languages.

Abstract: Image-text retrieval has become a fundamental component in intelligent multimedia systems; however, most existing vision-language models are optimized for highresource languages and remain suboptimal for low-resource settings such as Vietnamese. This work introduces ViCLIP-OT, a foundation vision-language model specifically designed for Vietnamese image-text retrieval. The proposed framework integrates CLIP-style contrastive learning with a Similarity-Graph Regularized Optimal Transport (SIGROT) loss to enhance global cross-modal consistency and mitigate modality gap issues. Extensive experiments on three Vietnamese benchmarks (UITOpenViIC, KTVIC, and Crossmodal-3600) demonstrate that ViCLIP-OT consistently outperforms CLIP and SigLIP baselines in both in-domain and zero-shot settings. On UIT-OpenViIC, the model achieves an average Recall@K of 67.34%, improving upon CLIP by 5.75 percentage points. In zero-shot evaluation on Crossmodal-3600, ViCLIPOT surpasses CLIP by 11.72 percentage points. Embedding-space analysis further confirms improved alignment and reduced modality gap. The results indicate that integrating SIGROT provides an effective and scalable strategy for cross-modal retrieval in low-resource languages, offering practical implications for intelligent multimedia retrieval systems in Vietnamese and other underrepresented linguistic contexts.

</details>


### [41] [UniScale: Unified Scale-Aware 3D Reconstruction for Multi-View Understanding via Prior Injection for Robotic Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23224)
*Mohammad Mahdavian,Gordon Tan,Binbin Xu,Yuan Ren,Dongfeng Bai,Bingbing Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniScale is a unified, scale-aware multi-view 3D reconstruction framework for robotics that jointly predicts camera intrinsics/extrinsics, scale-invariant depth/point maps, and the scene's metric scale from multi-view images, optionally leveraging geometric priors, all in a single feed-forward model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate environmental structure and metric-scale are essential for vision-based robotic navigation. Existing methods struggle with scale ambiguity and separate handling of intrinsics/poses; there is a need for a single, efficient model that can recover metric scale, utilize known intrinsics/poses, and exploit priors without retraining.

Method: A single feed-forward network jointly predicting camera intrinsics, extrinsics, scale-invariant depth and point maps, and the scene metric scale from multi-view images. The design fuses global contextual reasoning with camera-aware features and allows modular incorporation of auxiliary geometric priors. When intrinsics/poses are known, they can be integrated to boost performance. The model does not require training from scratch and leverages pre-existing world priors without explicit geometric encoding strategies.

Result: Empirical claim of strong generalization and consistent performance across diverse environments on multiple benchmarks; performance improves when camera intrinsics and/or poses are available; implementation will be released.

Conclusion: UniScale provides robust, metric-aware 3D reconstruction within a single, modular model suitable for resource-constrained robotic teams, capable of integrating priors and not requiring retraining.

Abstract: We present UniScale, a unified, scale-aware multi-view 3D reconstruction framework for robotic applications that flexibly integrates geometric priors through a modular, semantically informed design. In vision-based robotic navigation, the accurate extraction of environmental structure from raw image sequences is critical for downstream tasks. UniScale addresses this challenge with a single feed-forward network that jointly estimates camera intrinsics and extrinsics, scale-invariant depth and point maps, and the metric scale of a scene from multi-view images, while optionally incorporating auxiliary geometric priors when available. By combining global contextual reasoning with camera-aware feature representations, UniScale is able to recover the metric-scale of the scene. In robotic settings where camera intrinsics are known, they can be easily incorporated to improve performance, with additional gains obtained when camera poses are also available. This co-design enables robust, metric-aware 3D reconstruction within a single unified model. Importantly, UniScale does not require training from scratch, and leverages world priors exhibited in pre-existing models without geometric encoding strategies, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained robotic teams. We evaluate UniScale on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization and consistent performance across diverse environments. We will release our implementation upon acceptance.

</details>


### [42] [SUPERGLASSES: Benchmarking Vision Language Models as Intelligent Agents for AI Smart Glasses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22683)
*Zhuohang Jiang,Xu Yuan,Haohao Qu,Shanru Lin,Kanglong Liu,Wenqi Fan,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new benchmark (SUPERGLASSES) for VQA with smart glasses data, plus a retrieval-augmented agent (SUPERLENS) that uses object detection, query decoupling, and multimodal web search to improve answers, achieving state-of-the-art and surpassing GPT-4o by 2.19%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs for smart glasses are trained on traditional multimodal datasets that do not reflect real-world, egocentric usage and the necessity to identify the object of interest before leveraging external knowledge.

Method: Create SUPERGLASSES: a dataset of 2,422 egocentric image–question pairs across 14 image domains and 8 query categories, with search trajectories and reasoning annotations; evaluate 26 VLMs; introduce SUPERLENS: a multimodal smart glasses agent performing retrieval-augmented answer generation via automatic object detection, query decoupling, and multimodal web search.

Result: Significant performance gaps exist among existing VLMs on the new benchmark; SUPERLENS achieves state-of-the-art results, surpassing GPT-4o by 2.19 percentage points.

Conclusion: Smart glasses VQA require task-specific solutions and realistic data; the SUPERGLASSES dataset and SUPERLENS framework establish benchmarks and a pathway for improved retrieval-augmented VQA in egocentric settings.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of AI-powered smart glasses, one of the hottest wearable devices, has unlocked new frontiers for multimodal interaction, with Visual Question Answering (VQA) over external knowledge sources emerging as a core application. Existing Vision Language Models (VLMs) adapted to smart glasses are typically trained and evaluated on traditional multimodal datasets; however, these datasets lack the variety and realism needed to reflect smart glasses usage scenarios and diverge from their specific challenges, where accurately identifying the object of interest must precede any external knowledge retrieval. To bridge this gap, we introduce SUPERGLASSES, the first comprehensive VQA benchmark built on real-world data entirely collected by smart glasses devices. SUPERGLASSES comprises 2,422 egocentric image-question pairs spanning 14 image domains and 8 query categories, enriched with full search trajectories and reasoning annotations. We evaluate 26 representative VLMs on this benchmark, revealing significant performance gaps. To address the limitations of existing models, we further propose SUPERLENS, a multimodal smart glasses agent that enables retrieval-augmented answer generation by integrating automatic object detection, query decoupling, and multimodal web search. Our agent achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing GPT-4o by 2.19 percent, and highlights the need for task-specific solutions in smart glasses VQA scenarios.

</details>


### [43] [Risk-Aware World Model Predictive Control for Generalizable End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23259)
*Jiangxin Sun,Feng Xue,Teng Long,Chang Liu,Jian-Fang Hu,Wei-Shi Zheng,Nicu Sebe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A risk-aware world-model predictive control framework (RaWMPC) for end-to-end autonomous driving that eliminates dependence on expert demonstrations by predicting action outcomes, evaluating risk, and distilling risk-avoidance into a generative action proposal network; achieves superior in- and out-of-distribution performance and improved interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning limits generalization when encountering rare/unseen long-tail scenarios because the policy imitates expert actions without understanding the underlying risk. A framework that can make reliable decisions without expert supervision is highly desirable for robust autonomous driving.

Method: 1) Train a stochastic/deterministic world model that simulates the consequences of multiple candidate actions. 2) Introduce a risk-aware interaction strategy to deliberately expose the model to hazardous behaviors so it can learn to predict and avoid catastrophic outcomes. 3) At test time, perform risk evaluation across candidate actions to select low-risk actions. 4) Use self-evaluation distillation to transfer risk-avoidance capabilities from the world model into a generator-based action proposal network without using expert demonstrations.

Result: RaWMPC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios and provides superior decision interpretability.

Conclusion: Risk-aware world modeling, coupled with explicit risk evaluation and distillation into a proposal network, enables reliable, interpretable autonomous driving without expert demonstrations and improves generalization to unseen scenarios.

Abstract: With advances in imitation learning (IL) and large-scale driving datasets, end-to-end autonomous driving (E2E-AD) has made great progress recently. Currently, IL-based methods have become a mainstream paradigm: models rely on standard driving behaviors given by experts, and learn to minimize the discrepancy between their actions and expert actions. However, this objective of "only driving like the expert" suffers from limited generalization: when encountering rare or unseen long-tail scenarios outside the distribution of expert demonstrations, models tend to produce unsafe decisions in the absence of prior experience. This raises a fundamental question: Can an E2E-AD system make reliable decisions without any expert action supervision? Motivated by this, we propose a unified framework named Risk-aware World Model Predictive Control (RaWMPC) to address this generalization dilemma through robust control, without reliance on expert demonstrations. Practically, RaWMPC leverages a world model to predict the consequences of multiple candidate actions and selects low-risk actions through explicit risk evaluation. To endow the world model with the ability to predict the outcomes of risky driving behaviors, we design a risk-aware interaction strategy that systematically exposes the world model to hazardous behaviors, making catastrophic outcomes predictable and thus avoidable. Furthermore, to generate low-risk candidate actions at test time, we introduce a self-evaluation distillation method to distill riskavoidance capabilities from the well-trained world model into a generative action proposal network without any expert demonstration. Extensive experiments show that RaWMPC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios, while providing superior decision interpretability.

</details>


### [44] [No Caption, No Problem: Caption-Free Membership Inference via Model-Fitted Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22689)
*Joonsung Jeon,Woo Jae Kim,Suhyeon Ha,Sooel Son,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoFit enables caption-free membership inference on latent diffusion models by constructing image perturbations and model-fitted embeddings that exploit the model's unconditional prior, boosting separability between training and hold-out samples without captions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy/IP concerns from memorization in diffusion-based text-to-image models. Existing MIAs rely on ground-truth captions; in realistic settings, only images are available. A caption-free attack is therefore needed.

Method: A two-stage process: (i) model-fitted surrogate optimization to perturb the query image, creating a surrogate within regions of the unconditional prior learned from member samples; (ii) surrogate-driven embedding extraction to derive a model-fitted embedding used as a mismatched conditioning for the query image, amplifying the conditional loss for member samples.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and diffusion models shows MoFit outperforms prior VLM-conditioned baselines and is competitive with caption-dependent methods, demonstrating strong caption-free MIA performance.

Conclusion: Caption-free MIAs are viable for diffusion-based generators. MoFit effectively leverages model-fitted surrogates and embeddings to reveal memorization without captions, highlighting privacy risks and motivating further research into defenses and auditing tools.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity text-to-image generation, but their tendency to memorize training data raises critical privacy and intellectual property concerns. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) provide a principled way to audit such memorization by determining whether a given sample was included in training. However, existing approaches assume access to ground-truth captions. This assumption fails in realistic scenarios where only images are available and their textual annotations remain undisclosed, rendering prior methods ineffective when substituted with vision-language model (VLM) captions. In this work, we propose MoFit, a caption-free MIA framework that constructs synthetic conditioning inputs that are explicitly overfitted to the target model's generative manifold. Given a query image, MoFit proceeds in two stages: (i) model-fitted surrogate optimization, where a perturbation applied to the image is optimized to construct a surrogate in regions of the model's unconditional prior learned from member samples, and (ii) surrogate-driven embedding extraction, where a model-fitted embedding is derived from the surrogate and then used as a mismatched condition for the query image. This embedding amplifies conditional loss responses for member samples while leaving hold-outs relatively less affected, thereby enhancing separability in the absence of ground-truth captions. Our comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and diffusion models demonstrate that MoFit consistently outperforms prior VLM-conditioned baselines and achieves performance competitive with caption-dependent methods.

</details>


### [45] [GFRRN: Explore the Gaps in Single Image Reflection Removal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22695)
*Yu Chen,Zewei He,Xingyu Liu,Zixuan Chen,Zheming Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Gap-Free Reflection Removal Network (GFRRN) for single image reflection removal by integrating PEFT with Mona layers, a label generator for synthetic/real data label consistency, a Gaussian Adaptive Frequency Learning Block (G-AFLB) for frequency priors, and Dynamic Agent Attention (DAA) to replace window-based attention, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two key problems in SIRR: (i) the semantic understanding gap between features of pre-trained models and reflection-removal models, and (ii) reflection label inconsistencies between synthetic and real-world training data.

Method: 1) Use parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) by inserting Mona layers into a pre-trained backbone; 2) introduce a label generator to unify reflection labels across synthetic and real-world data; 3) propose Gaussian-based Adaptive Frequency Learning Block (G-AFLB) to adaptively learn and fuse frequency priors; 4) employ Dynamic Agent Attention (DAA) as an alternative to window-based attention to model inter- and intra-window significance; 5) integrate these into Gap-Free Reflection Removal Network (GFRRN).

Result: Experimental results on benchmark datasets show that GFRRN achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art SIRR methods, demonstrating improved effectiveness and robustness.

Conclusion: GFRRN effectively mitigates semantic and label inconsistencies in SIRR and leverages adaptive frequency priors and dynamic attention to enhance reflection removal performance, offering a robust framework for single image reflection removal across synthetic and real-world data.

Abstract: Prior dual-stream methods with the feature interaction mechanism have achieved remarkable performance in single image reflection removal (SIRR). However, they often struggle with (1) semantic understanding gap between the features of pre-trained models and those of reflection removal models, and (2) reflection label inconsistencies between synthetic and real-world training data. In this work, we first adopt the parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategy by integrating several learnable Mona layers into the pre-trained model to align the training directions. Then, a label generator is designed to unify the reflection labels for both synthetic and real-world data. In addition, a Gaussian-based Adaptive Frequency Learning Block (G-AFLB) is proposed to adaptively learn and fuse the frequency priors, and a Dynamic Agent Attention (DAA) is employed as an alternative to window-based attention by dynamically modeling the significance levels across windows (inter-) and within an individual window (intra-). These components constitute our proposed Gap-Free Reflection Removal Network (GFRRN). Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our GFRRN, achieving superior performance against state-of-the-art SIRR methods.

</details>


### [46] [UFO-DETR: Frequency-Guided End-to-End Detector for UAV Tiny Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22712)
*Yuankai Chen,Kai Lin,Qihong Wu,Xinxuan Yang,Jiashuo Lai,Ruoen Chen,Haonan Shi,Minfan He,Meihua Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UFO-DETR: an end-to-end UAV object detector with an LSKNet backbone, DAttention and AIFI for multi-scale spatial reasoning, and DynFreq-C3 for cross-space frequency enhancement, achieving strong small-target detection with lower computational cost than RT-DETR-L.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle small-target detection in UAV imagery plagued by scale variation, dense, and dominated small targets, while reducing model complexity for edge devices; current detectors rely on hand-crafted components and are not UAV-optimized.

Method: Introduce UFO-DETR with an LSKNet-based backbone to optimize receptive fields and reduce parameters; employ DAttention and AIFI modules to model multi-scale spatial relations; add DynFreq-C3 to enhance small-target detection via cross-space frequency feature enhancement; end-to-end detector for UAVs.

Result: Compared with RT-DETR-L, UFO-DETR shows significant gains in detection performance and computational efficiency, indicating strong suitability for edge computing on UAV platforms.

Conclusion: UFO-DETR provides an accurate, efficient solution for small-target detection in UAV imagery and demonstrates potential for deployment on UAV edge devices, with room for expanded benchmarks and ablations.

Abstract: Small target detection in UAV imagery faces significant challenges such as scale variations, dense distribution, and the dominance of small targets. Existing algorithms rely on manually designed components, and general-purpose detectors are not optimized for UAV images, making it difficult to balance accuracy and complexity. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an end-to-end object detection framework, UFO-DETR, which integrates an LSKNet-based backbone network to optimize the receptive field and reduce the number of parameters. By combining the DAttention and AIFI modules, the model flexibly models multi-scale spatial relationships, improving multi-scale target detection performance. Additionally, the DynFreq-C3 module is proposed to enhance small target detection capability through cross-space frequency feature enhancement. Experimental results show that, compared to RT-DETR-L, the proposed method offers significant advantages in both detection performance and computational efficiency, providing an efficient solution for UAV edge computing.

</details>


### [47] [SoPE: Spherical Coordinate-Based Positional Embedding for Enhancing Spatial Perception of 3D LVLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22716)
*Guanting Ye,Qiyan Zhao,Wenhao Yu,Liangyu Yuan,Mingkai Li,Xiaofeng Zhang,Jianmin Ji,Yanyong Zhang,Qing Jiang,Ka-Veng Yuen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SoPE provides spherical-coordinate positional embedding for 3D LVLMs to capture geometry and angles, plus multi-scale frequency mixing, improving 3D multimodal understanding and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RoPE's 3D use fails to preserve 3D spatial structure and angular dependencies; a representation that respects 3D geometry is needed for better cross-modal alignment in 3D LVLMs.

Method: Map point-cloud token indices to 3D spherical coordinates and apply SoPE to encode both spatial locations and directional angles; introduce a multi-scale frequency mixing strategy to fuse features across multiple frequency domains.

Result: Reported improvements on multiple 3D scene benchmarks and successful real-world deployment demonstrating generalization.

Conclusion: SoPE offers geometrically faithful 3D positional encoding and effective multi-scale fusion to enhance 3D LVLM performance and generalization.

Abstract: 3D Large Vision-Language Models (3D LVLMs) built upon Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across various multimodal tasks. However, their inherited position-dependent modeling mechanism, Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), remains suboptimal for 3D multimodal understanding. The vanilla RoPE formulation fails to preserve essential three-dimensional spatial structures when encoding 3D tokens, and its relative distance computation overlooks angular dependencies, hindering the model's ability to capture directional variations in visual representations. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Spherical Coordinate-based Positional Embedding (SoPE). Our method maps point-cloud token indices into a 3D spherical coordinate space, enabling unified modeling of spatial locations and directional angles. This formulation preserves the inherent geometric structure of point-cloud data, enhances spatial awareness, and yields more consistent and expressive geometric representations for multimodal learning. In addition, we introduce a multi-scale frequency mixing strategy to fuse feature information across different frequency domains. Experimental results on multiple 3D scene benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our approach, while real-world deployment experiments further demonstrate its strong generalization capability.

</details>


### [48] [IRSDE-Despeckle: A Physics-Grounded Diffusion Model for Generalizable Ultrasound Despeckling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22717)
*Shuoqi Chen,Yujia Wu,Geoffrey P. Luke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based ultrasound despeckling using Image Restoration SDE, trained on simulated paired data to remove speckle while preserving anatomy; includes uncertainty via cross-model variance and reveals domain shift under probe variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speckle artifacts degrade ultrasound image quality and hinder interpretation; existing filters and learning methods struggle to robustly despeck while preserving edges; diffusion models offer probabilistic restoration and uncertainty quantification; large paired datasets are hard to obtain, so simulated data from MRI provides a feasible supervision source.

Method: Use an Image Restoration Stochastic Differential Equations diffusion framework for despeckling. Train on large paired datasets generated by simulating ultrasound images from speckle-free MR images using the Matlab Ultrasound Toolbox. The model outputs speckle-suppressed images while preserving edges/contrast. Estimate prediction uncertainty via cross-model variance. Evaluate sensitivity to simulation/probe settings to assess domain shift.

Result: On a held-out simulated test set, the method consistently outperforms classical filters and recent learning-based despeckling baselines. Cross-model variance correlates with reconstruction error, offering a practical indicator of difficult regions. Domain shift is observed with changing probe settings, underscoring the need for diverse training and adaptation for clinical deployment.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based despeckling can achieve higher-quality, edge-preserving reconstructions with usable uncertainty signals; robust clinical deployment will require diversified training data and domain adaptation to account for probe/settings variations.

Abstract: Ultrasound imaging is widely used for real-time, noninvasive diagnosis, but speckle and related artifacts reduce image quality and can hinder interpretation. We present a diffusion-based ultrasound despeckling method built on the Image Restoration Stochastic Differential Equations framework. To enable supervised training, we curate large paired datasets by simulating ultrasound images from speckle-free magnetic resonance images using the Matlab UltraSound Toolbox. The proposed model reconstructs speckle-suppressed images while preserving anatomically meaningful edges and contrast. On a held-out simulated test set, our approach consistently outperforms classical filters and recent learning-based despeckling baselines. We quantify prediction uncertainty via cross-model variance and show that higher uncertainty correlates with higher reconstruction error, providing a practical indicator of difficult or failure-prone regions. Finally, we evaluate sensitivity to simulation probe settings and observe domain shift, motivating diversified training and adaptation for robust clinical deployment.

</details>


### [49] [HulluEdit: Single-Pass Evidence-Consistent Subspace Editing for Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22727)
*Yangguang Lin,Quan Fang,Yufei Li,Jiachen Sun,Junyu Gao,Jitao Sang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HulluEdit enables single-pass, reference-free intervention in LVLMs by orthogonally decomposing hidden states into visual evidence, conflicting priors, and residual uncertainty, and edits only the prior subspace to suppress hallucinations without harming visual grounding, achieving state-of-the-art results on POPE/CHAIR with efficiency and generalization on MME.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object hallucination in LVLMs undermines reliability. Existing approaches are either computationally expensive (requiring reference models or multiple forward passes) or risk suppressing genuine visual evidence via static edits. A lightweight, single-pass, reference-free method that preserves grounding is needed.

Method: Orthogonal subspace editing: decompose model hidden states into three orthogonal subspaces—visual evidence, conflicting priors, and residual uncertainty. Apply edits exclusively to the prior subspace to suppress hallucinations while keeping the visual subspace intact. The approach is single-pass and reference-free, with a mathematical guarantee that edits to the prior subspace do not affect the visual component.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art hallucination reduction on POPE and CHAIR across diverse LVLM architectures; preserves general capabilities on MME; maintains efficient inference; outperforms contrastive decoding and static subspace editing baselines.

Conclusion: Introduces a new, efficient pathway toward more trustworthy LVLMs by targeting hallucination-prone priors without perturbing visual grounding, enabling robust performance with minimal computational overhead.

Abstract: Object hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) significantly hinders their reliable deployment. Existing methods struggle to balance efficiency and accuracy: they often require expensive reference models and multiple forward passes, or apply static edits that risk suppressing genuine visual evidence. To address this, we introduce HulluEdit, a single-pass, reference-free intervention framework. Our core innovation is orthogonal subspace editing: we decompose the hidden states of the model into orthogonal subspaces - visual evidence, conflicting priors, and residual uncertainty - enabling selective suppression of hallucinatory patterns without interfering with visual grounding. This approach mathematically guarantees that edits applied to the prior subspace leave the visual component entirely unaffected. Extensive experiments show that HulluEdit achieves state-of-the-art hallucination reduction on benchmarks including POPE and CHAIR across diverse architectures, while preserving general capabilities on MME and maintaining efficient inference. Our method consistently outperforms contrastive decoding and static subspace editing baselines, offering a new pathway toward more trustworthy LVLMs.

</details>


### [50] [Asymmetric Idiosyncrasies in Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22734)
*Muzi Tao,Chufan Shi,Huijuan Wang,Shengbang Tong,Xuezhe Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Caption models imprint strong stylistic signatures detectable by text classifiers (~99.7% accuracy); however, generated images largely erase these cues (≤50% accuracy), revealing a cross-modal discrepancy. The work introduces a classification-based framework to quantify caption-model idiosyncrasies and text-to-image prompt-following ability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how stylistic idiosyncrasies of captioning models transfer to text-to-image outputs and assess how well T2I systems follow prompts.

Method: Train neural classifiers to predict the originating caption model from either the generated caption or its corresponding image; evaluate classification accuracy; analyze what caption variations (level of detail, color/texture emphasis, object distribution) survive in images.

Result: Caption-model classification on captions achieves ~99.7% accuracy; classification on generated images drops to at most 50%, even for the Flux model; generated images fail to preserve key caption variations.

Conclusion: Proposes a novel framework to quantify caption-model idiosyncrasies and the prompt-following ability of text-to-image systems, highlighting a notable cross-modal discrepancy between captions and their visual outputs.

Abstract: In this work, we study idiosyncrasies in the caption models and their downstream impact on text-to-image models. We design a systematic analysis: given either a generated caption or the corresponding image, we train neural networks to predict the originating caption model. Our results show that text classification yields very high accuracy (99.70\%), indicating that captioning models embed distinctive stylistic signatures. In contrast, these signatures largely disappear in the generated images, with classification accuracy dropping to at most 50\% even for the state-of-the-art Flux model. To better understand this cross-modal discrepancy, we further analyze the data and find that the generated images fail to preserve key variations present in captions, such as differences in the level of detail, emphasis on color and texture, and the distribution of objects within a scene. Overall, our classification-based framework provides a novel methodology for quantifying both the stylistic idiosyncrasies of caption models and the prompt-following ability of text-to-image systems.

</details>


### [51] [AMLRIS: Alignment-aware Masked Learning for Referring Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22740)
*Tongfei Chen,Shuo Yang,Yuguang Yang,Linlin Yang,Runtang Guo,Changbai Li,He Long,Chunyu Xie,Dawei Leng,Baochang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AML (Alignment-Aware Masked Learning) enhances Referring Image Segmentation by explicitly estimating pixel-level vision-language alignment and masking poorly aligned regions during training to emphasize trustworthy cues, achieving state-of-the-art results on RefCOCO and improved robustness to diverse descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RIS requires precise cross-modal alignment; misalignment in training regions can degrade performance and generalization across varied descriptions and scenarios.

Method: A training strategy that explicitly estimates pixel-level vision-language alignment and applies alignment-aware masking to filter out poorly aligned regions during optimization, guiding the model to focus on trustworthy cues.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO datasets; enhanced robustness to diverse descriptions and scenarios.

Conclusion: AML is an effective RIS training paradigm that improves accuracy and robustness by enforcing explicit alignment estimation and selective learning on trustworthy visual-language cues.

Abstract: Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment an object in an image identified by a natural language expression. The paper introduces Alignment-Aware Masked Learning (AML), a training strategy to enhance RIS by explicitly estimating pixel-level vision-language alignment, filtering out poorly aligned regions during optimization, and focusing on trustworthy cues. This approach results in state-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO datasets and also enhances robustness to diverse descriptions and scenarios

</details>


### [52] [ProjFlow: Projection Sampling with Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Exact Spatial Motion Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22742)
*Akihisa Watanabe,Qing Yu,Edgar Simo-Serra,Kent Fujiwara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProjFlow is a training-free sampler that exactly enforces linear spatial constraints in human motion (zero-shot) while preserving realism, using a kinematics-aware metric over skeletal topology; it handles sparse inputs via a fading pseudo-observation time-varying formulation and shows competitive results with training-based controllers on motion inpainting and 2D-to-3D lifting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard spatial constraints and sparse input handling in motion synthesis often degrade naturalness and require task-specific training. A training-free, exact, and fast solution for linear inverse problems in animation would improve robustness and efficiency across tasks like inpainting and lifting.

Method: Introduce ProjFlow, a training-free sampler that solves linear inverse problems with zero-shot exact constraint satisfaction. It uses a novel kinematics-aware metric encoding skeletal topology to distribute corrections coherently across the skeleton, avoiding artifacts from naive projection. For sparse inputs, it employs a time-varying formulation with pseudo-observations that fade during sampling to interpolate gaps.

Result: Empirical evaluation on motion inpainting and 2D-to-3D lifting shows ProjFlow achieves exact constraint satisfaction and renders realism that matches or exceeds zero-shot baselines, while remaining competitive with training-based controllers.

Conclusion: ProjFlow demonstrates that exact linear constraint satisfaction in motion generation can be achieved without training by leveraging a topology-aware corrective scheme and a fading pseudo-observation strategy for sparse data, offering a practical, efficient alternative to task-specific or heavy optimization approaches.

Abstract: Generating human motion with precise spatial control is a challenging problem. Existing approaches often require task-specific training or slow optimization, and enforcing hard constraints frequently disrupts motion naturalness. Building on the observation that many animation tasks can be formulated as a linear inverse problem, we introduce ProjFlow, a training-free sampler that achieves zero-shot, exact satisfaction of linear spatial constraints while preserving motion realism. Our key advance is a novel kinematics-aware metric that encodes skeletal topology. This metric allows the sampler to enforce hard constraints by distributing corrections coherently across the entire skeleton, avoiding the unnatural artifacts of naive projection. Furthermore, for sparse inputs, such as filling in long gaps between a few keyframes, we introduce a time-varying formulation using pseudo-observations that fade during sampling. Extensive experiments on representative applications, motion inpainting, and 2D-to-3D lifting, demonstrate that ProjFlow achieves exact constraint satisfaction and matches or improves realism over zero-shot baselines, while remaining competitive with training-based controllers.

</details>


### [53] [SPATIALALIGN: Aligning Dynamic Spatial Relationships in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22745)
*Fengming Liu,Tat-Jen Cham,Chuanxia Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPATIALALIGN enhances text-to-video models to faithfully render dynamic spatial relationships by fine-tuning with zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization, using a geometry-based DSR-SCORE metric and a new DSR-diverse dataset; results show improved spatial alignment and a dataset/metric for robust evaluation; code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current T2V systems prioritize aesthetics and often neglect spatial constraints described in prompts. There is a need to quantify and improve alignment of generated videos with dynamic spatial relationships (DSR).

Method: Introduce zeroth-order regularized DPO to fine-tune T2V models toward DSR alignment; propose DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric to measure alignment between videos and DSR prompts; build a dataset of text-video pairs with diverse DSRs; conduct extensive experiments.

Result: Fine-tuned models significantly outperform baselines in capturing spatial relationships; DSR-SCORE provides a direct evaluation of spatial alignment; dataset supports research; code to be released.

Conclusion: SPATIALALIGN demonstrates that integrating geometry-based evaluation and zeroth-order optimization can substantially improve the faithful representation of dynamic spatial relationships in T2V, enabling more controllable and faithful video generation.

Abstract: Most text-to-video (T2V) generators prioritize aesthetic quality, but often ignoring the spatial constraints in the generated videos. In this work, we present SPATIALALIGN, a self-improvement framework that enhances T2V models capabilities to depict Dynamic Spatial Relationships (DSR) specified in text prompts. We present a zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune T2V models towards better alignment with DSR. Specifically, we design DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric that quantitatively measures the alignment between generated videos and the specified DSRs in prompts, which is a step forward from prior works that rely on VLM for evaluation. We also conduct a dataset of text-video pairs with diverse DSRs to facilitate the study. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our fine-tuned model significantly out performs the baseline in spatial relationships. The code will be released in Link.

</details>


### [54] [Beyond Detection: Multi-Scale Hidden-Code for Natural Image Deepfake Recovery and Factual Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22759)
*Yuan-Chih Chen,Chun-Shien Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified hidden-code recovery framework enabling retrieval and restoration from watermarking; introduces ImageNet-S benchmark for paired image-label factual retrieval; shows competitive retrieval and reconstruction; compatible with various watermarking pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gap in the literature: image authenticity work centers on detection/localization, while recovery of tampered content for factual retrieval remains underexplored.

Method: Encode semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code, refined with multi-scale vector quantization; leverage conditional Transformer modules to improve contextual reasoning; designed to be compatible with multiple watermarking pipelines; introduces ImageNet-S as a benchmark for paired image-label retrieval tasks.

Result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S demonstrate promising retrieval and reconstruction performance, validating the framework’s effectiveness and compatibility with diverse watermarking pipelines.

Conclusion: The framework lays a foundation for general-purpose image recovery beyond detection and localization, enabling both retrieval and restoration from tampered content.

Abstract: Recent advances in image authenticity have primarily focused on deepfake detection and localization, leaving recovery of tampered contents for factual retrieval relatively underexplored. We propose a unified hidden-code recovery framework that enables both retrieval and restoration from post-hoc and in-generation watermarking paradigms. Our method encodes semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code representation, refined through multi-scale vector quantization, and enhances contextual reasoning via conditional Transformer modules. To enable systematic evaluation for natural images, we construct ImageNet-S, a benchmark that provides paired image-label factual retrieval tasks. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S demonstrate that our method exhibits promising retrieval and reconstruction performance while remaining fully compatible with diverse watermarking pipelines. This framework establishes a foundation for general-purpose image recovery beyond detection and localization.

</details>
