TL;DR: A vision-based approach to catch a thrown object using pixel-level cues from a single RGB image, paired with a heterogenous multi-agent RL framework for a robot arm with a multi-fingered hand, achieving sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the need for explicit 3D pose estimation in object interception and enable stable learning for high-DoF manipulation by leveraging role-specific, cooperative agents.

Method: Recognize object motion from pixel-level cues in a single RGB image rather than estimating 3D pose. Develop a heterogeneous multi-agent RL framework with the arm and hand as independent agents having role-specific observations and rewards, trained cooperatively, with policies transferred from simulation to real world.

Result: Policies learned in simulation successfully transfer to the real world, enabling effective catching/manipulation.

Conclusion: Pixel-level motion cues enable motion understanding for object interception without 3D pose estimation, and a role-based multi-agent RL framework can achieve stable learning and sim-to-real transfer for complex high-DoF manipulation.

Abstract: To catch a thrown object, a robot must be able to perceive the object's motion and generate control actions in a timely manner. Rather than explicitly estimating the object's 3D position, this work focuses on a novel approach that recognizes object motion using pixel-level visual information extracted from a single RGB image. Such visual cues capture changes in the object's position and scale, allowing the policy to reason about the object's motion. Furthermore, to achieve stable learning in a high-DoF system composed of a robot arm equipped with a multi-fingered hand, we design a heterogeneous multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that defines the arm and hand as independent agents with distinct roles. Each agent is trained cooperatively using role-specific observations and rewards, and the learned policies are successfully transferred from simulation to the real world.

</details>


### [134] [Unleashing the Potential of Diffusion Models for End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22801)
*Yinan Zheng,Tianyi Tan,Bin Huang,Enguang Liu,Ruiming Liang,Jianlin Zhang,Jianwei Cui,Guang Chen,Kun Ma,Hangjun Ye,Long Chen,Ya-Qin Zhang,Xianyuan Zhan,Jingjing Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diffusion-model-based end-to-end autonomous driving planning (Hyper Diffusion Planner, HDP) trained on large-scale real-world data, tested in 6 urban scenarios over 200 km, achieving ~10x improvement over a base model; includes RL post-training to enhance safety; deployed on real vehicle, demonstrating feasibility and potential of diffusion models for real-world E2E AD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between diffusion-model research and real-world E2E autonomous driving by performing large-scale real-vehicle evaluation and understanding diffusion loss dynamics, trajectory representations, and data scaling; to improve safety via reinforcement learning post-training.

Method: Systematic, large-scale investigation using real-vehicle data and on-road testing. Analyzes diffusion loss space, trajectory representations, and data scaling; introduces an RL post-training strategy; deploys the diffusion-based planner HDP on a real vehicle and evaluates across 6 urban scenarios totaling 200 km.

Result: Real-vehicle deployment of HDP with 6 urban scenarios and 200 km tested; observed ~10x performance improvement over the base model; RL post-training contributed to safety enhancements; demonstrated feasibility of diffusion models as scalable E2E AD planners.

Conclusion: Diffusion models can serve as effective and scalable E2E AD planners when carefully designed and trained, with findings guiding diffusion loss handling, representation choices, and data scaling; RL post-training is a viable safety-enhancement step for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become a popular choice for decision-making tasks in robotics, and more recently, are also being considered for solving autonomous driving tasks. However, their applications and evaluations in autonomous driving remain limited to simulation-based or laboratory settings. The full strength of diffusion models for large-scale, complex real-world settings, such as End-to-End Autonomous Driving (E2E AD), remains underexplored. In this study, we conducted a systematic and large-scale investigation to unleash the potential of the diffusion models as planners for E2E AD, based on a tremendous amount of real-vehicle data and road testing. Through comprehensive and carefully controlled studies, we identify key insights into the diffusion loss space, trajectory representation, and data scaling that significantly impact E2E planning performance. Moreover, we also provide an effective reinforcement learning post-training strategy to further enhance the safety of the learned planner. The resulting diffusion-based learning framework, Hyper Diffusion Planner} (HDP), is deployed on a real-vehicle platform and evaluated across 6 urban driving scenarios and 200 km of real-world testing, achieving a notable 10x performance improvement over the base model. Our work demonstrates that diffusion models, when properly designed and trained, can serve as effective and scalable E2E AD planners for complex, real-world autonomous driving tasks.

</details>


### [135] [LeRobot: An Open-Source Library for End-to-End Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22818)
*Remi Cadene,Simon Aliberts,Francesco Capuano,Michel Aractingi,Adil Zouitine,Pepijn Kooijmans,Jade Choghari,Martino Russi,Caroline Pascal,Steven Palma,Mustafa Shukor,Jess Moss,Alexander Soare,Dana Aubakirova,Quentin Lhoest,Quentin Gallouédec,Thomas Wolf*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: lerobot is an open-source, end-to-end robot learning library that unifies low-level motor control, data collection/storage/streaming, and scalable learning algorithms in a single platform for real-world robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot learning progress is hindered by fragmented, closed-source tools. There is a need for an accessible, scalable, cross-stack infrastructure that supports real hardware and multiple learning paradigms, enabling reproducibility and data-driven improvement.

Method: Developed lerobot as an integrated library that connects low-level middleware for motor control to large-scale dataset collection, storage, and streaming, plus a generalized asynchronous inference stack. It supports accessible hardware platforms, is extensible to new embodiments, and includes efficient implementations of state-of-the-art robot learning algorithms across multiple paradigms.

Result: An integrated, open-source framework that enables end-to-end robot learning: real hardware support, large-scale data collection and streaming, cross-paradigm learning modules, and asynchronous inference to accelerate experimentation. It promotes reproducibility and collaboration.

Conclusion: lerobot lowers barriers to entry for robotics research and practice by providing accessible, scalable, and open tooling that spans the entire robot learning stack and scales with data and compute.

Abstract: Robotics is undergoing a significant transformation powered by advances in high-level control techniques based on machine learning, giving rise to the field of robot learning. Recent progress in robot learning has been accelerated by the increasing availability of affordable teleoperation systems, large-scale openly available datasets, and scalable learning-based methods. However, development in the field of robot learning is often slowed by fragmented, closed-source tools designed to only address specific sub-components within the robotics stack. In this paper, we present \texttt{lerobot}, an open-source library that integrates across the entire robot learning stack, from low-level middleware communication for motor controls to large-scale dataset collection, storage and streaming. The library is designed with a strong focus on real-world robotics, supporting accessible hardware platforms while remaining extensible to new embodiments. It also supports efficient implementations for various state-of-the-art robot learning algorithms from multiple prominent paradigms, as well as a generalized asynchronous inference stack. Unlike traditional pipelines which heavily rely on hand-crafted techniques, \texttt{lerobot} emphasizes scalable learning approaches that improve directly with more data and compute. Designed for accessibility, scalability, and openness, \texttt{lerobot} lowers the barrier to entry for researchers and practitioners to robotics while providing a platform for reproducible, state-of-the-art robot learning.

</details>


### [136] [Performance and Experimental Analysis of Strain-based Models for Continuum Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22854)
*Annika Delucchi,Vincenzo Di Paola,Andreas Müller,and Matteo Zoppi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Evaluates a third-order strain interpolation for shape reconstruction in continuum robots, comparing to Geometric-Variable Strain; experimentally validated on a slender rod with camera-based shape extraction, achieving 0.58% length error and 0.32 s per config, outperforming prior models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess reliability and performance of strain-based shape reconstruction methods beyond uniform bending to support design and prototyping of continuum robots.

Method: Comparative analysis between a third-order strain interpolation method and Geometric-Variable Strain; simulation plus experimental verification by reshaping a slender rod with tip displacement using a manipulator; marker-based shape extraction via cameras; no strain gauges or wrench sensors used.

Result: Model predictions closely match observed shapes; average error 0.58% of rod length; average computation time 0.32 s per configuration; results indicate improved performance over existing models.

Conclusion: Third-order strain interpolation effectively captures both individual and coupled deformation effects and provides efficient, accurate shape reconstruction, making it suitable for real-time prototyping of continuum robots.

Abstract: Although strain-based models have been widely adopted in robotics, no comparison beyond the uniform bending test is commonly recognized to assess their performance. In addition, the increasing effort in prototyping continuum robots highlights the need to assess the applicability of these models and the necessity of comprehensive performance evaluation. To address this gap, this work investigates the shape reconstruction abilities of a third-order strain interpolation method, examining its ability to capture both individual and combined deformation effects. These results are compared and discussed against the Geometric-Variable Strain approach. Subsequently, simulation results are experimentally verified by reshaping a slender rod while recording the resulting configurations using cameras. The rod configuration is imposed using a manipulator displacing one of its tips and extracted through reflective markers, without the aid of any other external sensor -- i.e. strain gauges or wrench sensors placed along the rod. The experiments demonstrate good agreement between the model predictions and observed shapes, with average error of 0.58% of the rod length and average computational time of 0.32s per configuration, outperforming existing models.

</details>


### [137] [GraspLDP: Towards Generalizable Grasping Policy via Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22862)
*Enda Xiang,Haoxiang Ma,Xinzhu Ma,Zicheng Liu,Di Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Incorporates grasp priors into latent diffusion-based imitation learning for robotic grasping, plus a self-supervised reconstruction objective, to improve precision, generalization, and dynamic grasping in simulation and real hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation-learning grasp policies often exhibit imprecise grasps, limited spatial generalization, and poor object generalization. Diffusion-based policies are powerful but can lack grasp-specific priors.

Method: Employ a latent diffusion policy to guide action chunk decoding with a grasp pose prior, ensuring generated trajectories align with feasible grasp configurations. Introduce a self-supervised reconstruction objective during diffusion to embed the graspness prior: at each reverse diffusion step, reconstruct wrist-camera images and back-project graspness from intermediate representations.

Result: Experiments in simulation and on real robots show significant improvements over baselines and demonstrate strong dynamic grasping capabilities.

Conclusion: Integrating grasp priors and a self-supervised graspness reconstruction objective within the diffusion-based imitation-learning framework enhances grasp precision, spatial/object generalization, and dynamic execution.

Abstract: This paper focuses on enhancing the grasping precision and generalization of manipulation policies learned via imitation learning. Diffusion-based policy learning methods have recently become the mainstream approach for robotic manipulation tasks. As grasping is a critical subtask in manipulation, the ability of imitation-learned policies to execute precise and generalizable grasps merits particular attention. Existing imitation learning techniques for grasping often suffer from imprecise grasp executions, limited spatial generalization, and poor object generalization. To address these challenges, we incorporate grasp prior knowledge into the diffusion policy framework. In particular, we employ a latent diffusion policy to guide action chunk decoding with grasp pose prior, ensuring that generated motion trajectories adhere closely to feasible grasp configurations. Furthermore, we introduce a self-supervised reconstruction objective during diffusion to embed the graspness prior: at each reverse diffusion step, we reconstruct wrist-camera images back-projected the graspness from the intermediate representations. Both simulation and real robot experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baseline methods and exhibits strong dynamic grasping capabilities.

</details>


### [138] [DySL-VLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model Inference via Dynamic-Static Layer-Skipping for Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22896)
*Zebin Yang,Yijiahao Qi,Tong Xie,Bo Yu,Shaoshan Liu,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DySL-VLA enables dynamic layer skipping in Vision-Language-Action models by classifying layers as informative or incremental and using prior-post guidance plus a distillation training scheme to maintain accuracy while reducing computation; achieves substantial parameter reduction and speedups on robotics benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the high computational cost of VLA models for real-time robotic tasks, leveraging that different actions require varying levels of precision.

Method: Two-layer-type architecture: informative layers are always executed; incremental layers can be skipped based on action importance guided by prior-post skipping; skip-aware two-stage knowledge distillation to convert a standard VLA into DySL-VLA.

Result: Empirical results show 2.1% improvement in success length over Deer-VLA on the Calvin dataset; trainable parameters reduced by 85.7x; 3.75x speedup relative to RoboFlamingo at iso-accuracy; code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Dynamic layer-skipping in VLA models can substantially reduce computation with minimal impact on accuracy, enabling more practical real-time robotic applications; the method is effective and scalable.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown remarkable success in robotic tasks like manipulation by fusing a language model's reasoning with a vision model's 3D understanding. However, their high computational cost remains a major obstacle for real-world applications that require real-time performance. We observe that the actions within a task have varying levels of importance: critical steps demand high precision, while less important ones can tolerate more variance. Leveraging this insight, we propose DySL-VLA, a novel framework that addresses computational cost by dynamically skipping VLA layers based on each action's importance. DySL-VLA categorizes its layers into two types: informative layers, which are consistently executed, and incremental layers, which can be selectively skipped. To intelligently skip layers without sacrificing accuracy, we invent a prior-post skipping guidance mechanism to determine when to initiate layer-skipping. We also propose a skip-aware two-stage knowledge distillation algorithm to efficiently train a standard VLA into a DySL-VLA. Our experiments indicate that DySL-VLA achieves 2.1% improvement in success length over Deer-VLA on the Calvin dataset, while simultaneously reducing trainable parameters by a factor of 85.7 and providing a 3.75x speedup relative to the RoboFlamingo baseline at iso-accuracy. Our code is available on https://github.com/PKU-SEC-Lab/DYSL_VLA.

</details>


### [139] [Bayesian Preference Elicitation: Human-In-The-Loop Optimization of An Active Prosthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22922)
*Sophia Taddei,Wouter Koppen,Eligia Alfio,Stefano Nuzzo,Louis Flynn,Maria Alejandra Diaz,Sebastian Rojas Gonzalez,Tom Dhaene,Kevin De Pauw,Ivo Couckuyt,Tom Verstraten*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Preference-based human-in-the-loop optimization (HILO) for tuning a four-parameter prosthesis controller using user preferences; two algorithmic variants, discrete EUBO-LineCoSpar and continuous BPE4Prost, with PMBO and a preference-learning acquisition function. Claims efficient convergence, robust preference elicitation, and biomechanical improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce tuning time and align prosthesis control with user needs by leveraging direct user preferences in a multiobjective setting, addressing the gap between objective metrics and user satisfaction.

Method: Preference-based Multiobjective Bayesian Optimization (PMBO) using a state-of-the-art acquisition function designed for preference learning. Two variants: discrete EUBO-LineCoSpar and continuous BPE4Prost. Optimizes a standard four-parameter prosthesis controller guided by user preferences, with simulations on benchmark functions and real-application trials.

Result: Demonstrates efficient convergence and robust preference elicitation; reports measurable biomechanical improvements in real applications and successful performance on benchmarks.

Conclusion: Preference-driven tuning is a promising direction for user-centered prosthesis control, enabling faster personalization and potentially better user outcomes through HILO and PMBO.

Abstract: Tuning active prostheses for people with amputation is time-consuming and relies on metrics that may not fully reflect user needs. We introduce a human-in-the-loop optimization (HILO) approach that leverages direct user preferences to personalize a standard four-parameter prosthesis controller efficiently. Our method employs preference-based Multiobjective Bayesian Optimization that uses a state-or-the-art acquisition function especially designed for preference learning, and includes two algorithmic variants: a discrete version (\textit{EUBO-LineCoSpar}), and a continuous version (\textit{BPE4Prost}). Simulation results on benchmark functions and real-application trials demonstrate efficient convergence, robust preference elicitation, and measurable biomechanical improvements, illustrating the potential of preference-driven tuning for user-centered prosthesis control.

</details>


### [140] [Considering Perspectives for Automated Driving Ethics: Collective Risk in Vehicular Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22940)
*Leon Tolksdorf,Arturo Tejada,Christian Birkner,Nathan van de Wouw*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multiperspective risk-aware AV planning that balances the risks across all road users; collective risk minimization improves overall traffic safety and efficiency and yields socially acceptable behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AV planning emphasizes the AV’s own risk, but risk perceptions differ among road users. Aligning AV decisions with multiple risk perspectives may reduce risk for all users and enhance social acceptability.

Method: Introduces an AV motion-planning framework capable of switching risk-minimization objectives across road-user perspectives (AV, other road users, and a collective risk metric). Demonstrates that perspectives differ and that collective risk minimization minimizes total traffic risk, with the AV sacrificing some personal risk for others. Adaptive behavior (assertive when others under-represent risk; conservative when predictability is low) is used to improve efficiency and safety.

Result: Risk from other road users can differ from the AV’s risk perception. A collective (multi-perspective) risk minimization approach reduces overall traffic risk and can improve travel efficiency, with the AV adopting assertive or conservative behavior based on predictability of others.

Conclusion: Ethical AV deployment requires incorporating the risk-perspective of all road users into decision-making. A collective risk framework balances individual and social risk, promoting safer, more efficient, and socially acceptable AV behavior.

Abstract: Recent automated vehicle (AV) motion planning strategies evolve around minimizing risk in road traffic. However, they exclusively consider risk from the AV's perspective and, as such, do not address the ethicality of its decisions for other road users. We argue that this does not reduce the risk of each road user, as risk may be different from the perspective of each road user. Indeed, minimizing the risk from the AV's perspective may not imply that the risk from the perspective of other road users is also being minimized; in fact, it may even increase. To test this hypothesis, we propose an AV motion planning strategy that supports switching risk minimization strategies between all road user perspectives. We find that the risk from the perspective of other road users can generally be considered different to the risk from the AV's perspective. Taking a collective risk perspective, i.e., balancing the risks of all road users, we observe an AV that minimizes overall traffic risk the best, while putting itself at slightly higher risk for the benefit of others, which is consistent with human driving behavior. In addition, adopting a collective risk minimization strategy can also be beneficial to the AV's travel efficiency by acting assertively when other road users maintain a low risk estimate of the AV. Yet, the AV drives conservatively when its planned actions are less predictable to other road users, i.e., associated with high risk. We argue that such behavior is a form of self-reflection and a natural prerequisite for socially acceptable AV behavior. We conclude that to facilitate ethicality in road traffic that includes AVs, the risk-perspective of each road user must be considered in the decision-making of AVs.

</details>


### [141] [Automated Robotic Needle Puncture for Percutaneous Dilatational Tracheostomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22952)
*Yuan Tang,Bruno V. Adorno,Brendan A. McGrath,Andrew Weightman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Automates PDT needle insertion with a velocity-controlled robotic manipulator guided by two EM sensors; achieves sub-2 mm position accuracy and ~4° midline deviation on a mannequin; demonstrates potential for safer, more accurate PDT punctures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high risk and variability in manual percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy by improving accuracy, reducing angular error, and ensuring collision-free insertions.

Method: A velocity-controlled robotic manipulator performs needle insertion guided by pose data from two electromagnetic sensors (needle tip and inside trachea). An adaptive constrained controller compensates online for uncertain kinematics and avoids collisions. Validated via simulations and 400 punctures on a mannequin.

Result: Absolute median puncture position error: 1.7 mm (IQR 1.9 mm). Midline deviation: 4.13 degrees (IQR 4.55). These results indicate high accuracy and feasible collision-free insertions.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates feasibility of automated robotic PDT puncture with accurate targeting and safety guarantees; promising improvement over manual guidance, warranting further in vivo and clinical validation.

Abstract: Percutaneous dilatational tracheostomy (PDT) is frequently performed on patients in intensive care units for prolonged mechanical ventilation. The needle puncture, as the most critical step of PDT, could lead to adverse consequences such as major bleeding and posterior tracheal wall perforation if performed inaccurately. Current practices of PDT puncture are all performed manually with no navigation assistance, which leads to large position and angular errors (5 mm and 30 degree). To improve the accuracy and reduce the difficulty of the PDT procedure, we propose a system that automates the needle insertion using a velocity-controlled robotic manipulator. Guided using pose data from two electromagnetic sensors, one at the needle tip and the other inside the trachea, the robotic system uses an adaptive constrained controller to adapt the uncertain kinematic parameters online and avoid collisions with the patient's body and tissues near the target. Simulations were performed to validate the controller's implementation, and then four hundred PDT punctures were performed on a mannequin to evaluate the position and angular accuracy. The absolute median puncture position error was 1.7 mm (IQR: 1.9 mm) and midline deviation was 4.13 degree (IQR: 4.55 degree), measured by the sensor inside the trachea. The small deviations from the nominal puncture in a simulated experimental setup and formal guarantees of collision-free insertions suggest the feasibility of the robotic PDT puncture.

</details>


### [142] [A Perspective on Open Challenges in Deformable Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22998)
*Ryan Paul McKennaa,John Oyekan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A synthesis of Deformable Object Manipulation (DOM) research, outlining core challenges (occlusion, generalization, real-time scalability), key modalities (multimodal sensing, active vision, tactile feedback), and learning paradigms (physically informed RL, differentiable simulations). It highlights simulated expert demonstrations and generative models to bridge sim-to-real and advocates graph neural networks and large datasets as future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To survey the state-of-the-art in DOM, identify persistent challenges, summarize methodological trends, and propose concrete directions to enhance perception, modeling, and control of deformable objects in real-world robotic systems.

Method: The paper conducts a thematic literature survey, categorizing work by perception modalities (multi-camera setups, active vision, tactile sensing), modeling and control approaches (physically informed RL, differentiable simulators), and data strategies (simulated expert demonstrations, generative task specifications). It also discusses standardization of tasks and benchmarks and synthesizes future directions.

Result: Provides a structured overview of progress in DOM, emphasizing multimodal perception to mitigate occlusion, the impact of differentiable physics and physically informed RL on efficiency and accuracy, and the role of synthetic demonstrations and generative networks in bridging sim-to-real; identifies gaps and actionable research avenues.

Conclusion: DOM is poised to enable versatile robots capable of handling diverse deformable objects; realizing this potential hinges on scalable perception and control, accurate physical modeling, robust sim-to-real transfer, and the development of comprehensive datasets and standardized benchmarks. The authors advocate exploring graph neural networks for high-level decision-making and more extensive task-specification frameworks.

Abstract: Deformable object manipulation (DOM) represents a critical challenge in robotics, with applications spanning healthcare, manufacturing, food processing, and beyond. Unlike rigid objects, deformable objects exhibit infinite dimensionality, dynamic shape changes, and complex interactions with their environment, posing significant hurdles for perception, modeling, and control. This paper reviews the state of the art in DOM, focusing on key challenges such as occlusion handling, task generalization, and scalable, real-time solutions. It highlights advancements in multimodal perception systems, including the integration of multi-camera setups, active vision, and tactile sensing, which collectively address occlusion and improve adaptability in unstructured environments. Cutting-edge developments in physically informed reinforcement learning (RL) and differentiable simulations are explored, showcasing their impact on efficiency, precision, and scalability. The review also emphasizes the potential of simulated expert demonstrations and generative neural networks to standardize task specifications and bridge the simulation-to-reality gap. Finally, future directions are proposed, including the adoption of graph neural networks for high-level decision-making and the creation of comprehensive datasets to enhance DOM's real-world applicability. By addressing these challenges, DOM research can pave the way for versatile robotic systems capable of handling diverse and dynamic tasks with deformable objects.

</details>


### [143] [DigiArm: An Anthropomorphic 3D-Printed Prosthetic Hand with Enhanced Dexterity for Typing Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23017)
*Dean Zadok,Tom Naamani,Yuval Bar-Ratson,Elisha Barash,Oren Salzman,Alon Wolf,Alex M. Bronstein,Nili Krausz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost, 3D-printed prosthetic hand with adjustable finger spacing and a 2-D wrist enables independent finger pressing for typing and piano tasks; a feasibility study shows real-time keyboard and piano use with varying finger/wrist motions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current prosthetic hands struggle with dexterous, fine finger control beyond basic grasping. There is a need for accessible, replicable designs that allow precise interactions with electronic devices (typing, piano) while maintaining general manipulation capability.

Method: A lightweight, 3D-printed robotic hand featuring adjustable finger abduction/adduction, a 2-D wrist with controlled ulnar/radial deviation, and independent finger pressing. A study assessed participants’ ability to perform keyboard typing and piano playing in real time under different levels of finger and wrist motion.

Result: Participants demonstrated real-time keyboard typing and piano playing using the prosthetic hand, with performance varying across different levels of finger and wrist motion, indicating feasible dexterous control with the proposed design.

Conclusion: The proposed low-cost, modular design can enhance prosthetic dexterity and interaction with electronic devices, offering improved typing and fine finger-motion capabilities while preserving general manipulation functions.

Abstract: Despite recent advancements, existing prosthetic limbs are unable to replicate the dexterity and intuitive control of the human hand. Current control systems for prosthetic hands are often limited to grasping, and commercial prosthetic hands lack the precision needed for dexterous manipulation or applications that require fine finger motions. Thus, there is a critical need for accessible and replicable prosthetic designs that enable individuals to interact with electronic devices and perform precise finger pressing, such as keyboard typing or piano playing, while preserving current prosthetic capabilities. This paper presents a low-cost, lightweight, 3D-printed robotic prosthetic hand, specifically engineered for enhanced dexterity with electronic devices such as a computer keyboard or piano, as well as general object manipulation. The robotic hand features a mechanism to adjust finger abduction/adduction spacing, a 2-D wrist with the inclusion of controlled ulnar/radial deviation optimized for typing, and control of independent finger pressing. We conducted a study to demonstrate how participants can use the robotic hand to perform keyboard typing and piano playing in real time, with different levels of finger and wrist motion. This supports the notion that our proposed design can allow for the execution of key typing motions more effectively than before, aiming to enhance the functionality of prosthetic hands.

</details>


### [144] [InCoM: Intent-Driven Perception and Structured Coordination for Whole-Body Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23024)
*Jiahao Liu,Cui Wenbo,Haoran Li,Dongbin Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: InCoM integrates intent-driven perception reweighting and decoupled base-arm control with geometric-semantic alignment to enable robust whole-body mobile manipulation, achieving state-of-the-art success on ManiSkill-HAB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust whole-body mobile manipulation is hindered by strong base-arm coupling and dynamic viewpoint-induced perceptual attention shifts, which degrade control optimization and perception quality.

Method: Infer latent motion intent to dynamically reweight multi-scale perceptual features; apply geometric-semantic structured alignment for cross-modal perception; employ a decoupled coordinated flow matching action decoder for coordinated base-arm action generation.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three ManiSkill-HAB scenarios with improvements in success rate: 28.2%, 26.1%, and 23.6% without privileged perceptual information.

Conclusion: InCoM delivers effective integration of perception and control for whole-body mobile manipulation, showing strong robustness to viewpoint changes and reduced coupling difficulties, as evidenced by substantial success-rate gains on ManiSkill-HAB.

Abstract: Whole-body mobile manipulation is a fundamental capability for general-purpose robotic agents, requiring both coordinated control of the mobile base and manipulator and robust perception under dynamically changing viewpoints. However, existing approaches face two key challenges: strong coupling between base and arm actions complicates whole-body control optimization, and perceptual attention is often poorly allocated as viewpoints shift during mobile manipulation. We propose InCoM, an intent-driven perception and structured coordination framework for whole-body mobile manipulation. InCoM infers latent motion intent to dynamically reweight multi-scale perceptual features, enabling stage-adaptive allocation of perceptual attention. To support robust cross-modal perception, InCoM further incorporates a geometric-semantic structured alignment mechanism that enhances multimodal correspondence. On the control side, we design a decoupled coordinated flow matching action decoder that explicitly models coordinated base-arm action generation, alleviating optimization difficulties caused by control coupling. Without access to privileged perceptual information, InCoM outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three ManiSkill-HAB scenarios by 28.2%, 26.1%, and 23.6% in success rate, demonstrating strong effectiveness for whole-body mobile manipulation.

</details>


### [145] [An Empirical Analysis of Cooperative Perception for Occlusion Risk Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23051)
*Aihong Wang,Tenghui Xie,Fuxi Wen,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A universal Risk of Tracking Loss (RTL) metric aggregates occlusion risk over time; large-scale analysis reveals nonlinear V2X benefits and proposes asymmetric communication to boost safety at low penetration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion-induced perception gaps are a major safety concern for CAVs; existing risk metrics fail to capture cumulative exposure; a single metric is needed to compare deployment strategies.

Method: Define RTL as the time-integrated instantaneous risk during occluded periods; perform high-fidelity real-world data analysis to characterize occlusion risk; evaluate V2X deployment strategies and an asymmetric communication framework.

Result: RTL provides a holistic risk profile; full V2X largely eliminates risk but benefits are nonlinear; substantial gains require 75–90% penetration; the asymmetric framework enables risk reduction at lower penetration, with 25% asymmetric outperforming 75% symmetric and saturation around 50% penetration.

Conclusion: RTL is a crucial metric and offers a cost-effective roadmap to accelerate V2X safety. Asymmetric communication can mitigate risk at low penetration, while full penetration remains ideal but with diminishing returns; results guide deployment strategy.

Abstract: Occlusions present a significant challenge for connected and automated vehicles, as they can obscure critical road users from perception systems. Traditional risk metrics often fail to capture the cumulative nature of these threats over time adequately. In this paper, we propose a novel and universal risk assessment metric, the Risk of Tracking Loss (RTL), which aggregates instantaneous risk intensity throughout occluded periods. This provides a holistic risk profile that encompasses both high-intensity, short-term threats and prolonged exposure. Utilizing diverse and high-fidelity real-world datasets, a large-scale statistical analysis is conducted to characterize occlusion risk and validate the effectiveness of the proposed metric. The metric is applied to evaluate different vehicle-to-everything (V2X) deployment strategies. Our study shows that full V2X penetration theoretically eliminates this risk, the reduction is highly nonlinear; a substantial statistical benefit requires a high penetration threshold of 75-90%. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel asymmetric communication framework that allows even non-connected vehicles to receive warnings. Experimental results demonstrate that this paradigm achieves better risk mitigation performance. We found that our approach at 25% penetration outperforms the traditional symmetric model at 75%, and benefits saturate at only 50% penetration. This work provides a crucial risk assessment metric and a cost-effective, strategic roadmap for accelerating the safety benefits of V2X deployment.

</details>


### [146] [Towards Intelligible Human-Robot Interaction: An Active Inference Approach to Occluded Pedestrian Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23109)
*Kai Chen,Yuyao Huang,Guang Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Active inference-based framework for occluded pedestrians using RBPF for hybrid state estimation, belief-driven reasoning (Conditional Belief Reset, Hypothesis Injection), and CEM-enhanced MPPI planning; shows reduced collisions and explainable, human-like behavior in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occluded pedestrians create high-uncertainty, long-tail safety risks in autonomous driving. Conventional methods struggle to anticipate their behavior; a belief-driven, probabilistic approach aims to improve safety and explainability under uncertainty.

Method: Hybrid state estimation via Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF); representation of multiple latent pedestrian intentions through Conditional Belief Reset and Hypothesis Injection; planning via a Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) enhanced Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller within an Active Inference framework.

Result: Simulation experiments indicate significantly lower collision rates compared to reactive baselines, rule-based systems, and reinforcement learning approaches; the agent exhibits explainable, human-like behavior aligned with its internal belief state.

Conclusion: The framework provides a belief-driven, robust decision-making approach for occluded pedestrians, combining probabilistic state estimation, belief manipulation mechanisms, and robust planning to address high-uncertainty driving scenarios; promising for safer autonomous driving though requires real-world validation.

Abstract: The sudden appearance of occluded pedestrians presents a critical safety challenge in autonomous driving. Conventional rule-based or purely data-driven approaches struggle with the inherent high uncertainty of these long-tail scenarios. To tackle this challenge, we propose a novel framework grounded in Active Inference, which endows the agent with a human-like, belief-driven mechanism. Our framework leverages a Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) to efficiently estimate the pedestrian's hybrid state. To emulate human-like cognitive processes under uncertainty, we introduce a Conditional Belief Reset mechanism and a Hypothesis Injection technique to explicitly model beliefs about the pedestrian's multiple latent intentions. Planning is achieved via a Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) enhanced Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller, which synergizes the efficient, iterative search of CEM with the inherent robustness of MPPI. Simulation experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces the collision rate compared to reactive, rule-based, and reinforcement learning (RL) baselines, while also exhibiting explainable and human-like driving behavior that reflects the agent's internal belief state.

</details>


### [147] [Grasp, Slide, Roll: Comparative Analysis of Contact Modes for Tactile-Based Shape Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23206)
*Chung Hee Kim,Shivani Kamtikar,Tye Brady,Taskin Padir,Joshua Migdal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Evaluates how contact modes in tactile gripping affect 3D shape reconstruction, showing finger-grazing and palm-rolling yield faster, more accurate results with fewer contacts using an information-theoretic exploration and a shape completion model; validated on UR5e with Inspire-Robots hand.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the time and physical interaction cost of tactile sensing while maintaining or improving reconstruction accuracy by selecting informative contact modes.

Method: Compare three contact modes—grasp-releasing, finger-grazing sliding, and palm-rolling—within an information-theoretic exploration framework that selects subsequent contact locations; use a shape completion model to guide sampling; validate on a UR5e robot with Inspire-Robots Dexterous Hand across primitive geometries.

Result: Finger-grazing and palm-rolling reduced required contacts by 34% and improved reconstruction accuracy by 55% relative to baselines; robust performance across primitive object geometries.

Conclusion: Non-rolling finger-grazing and palm-rolling contact modes provide higher information gain for tactile-based shape completion, enabling faster convergence and more accurate reconstructions with fewer physical interactions.

Abstract: Tactile sensing allows robots to gather detailed geometric information about objects through physical interaction, complementing vision-based approaches. However, efficiently acquiring useful tactile data remains challenging due to the time-consuming nature of physical contact and the need to strategically choose contact locations that maximize information gain while minimizing physical interactions. This paper studies how different contact modes affect object shape reconstruction using a tactile-enabled dexterous gripper. We compare three contact interaction modes: grasp-releasing, sliding induced by finger-grazing, and palm-rolling. These contact modes are combined with an information-theoretic exploration framework that guides subsequent sampling locations using a shape completion model. Our results show that the improved tactile sensing efficiency of finger-grazing and palm-rolling translates into faster convergence in shape reconstruction, requiring 34% fewer physical interactions while improving reconstruction accuracy by 55%. We validate our approach using a UR5e robot arm equipped with an Inspire-Robots Dexterous Hand, showing robust performance across primitive object geometries.

</details>


### [148] [SPARR: Simulation-based Policies with Asymmetric Real-world Residuals for Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23253)
*Yijie Guo,Iretiayo Akinola,Lars Johannsmeier,Hugo Hadfield,Abhishek Gupta,Yashraj Narang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hybrid SPARR approach combines a simulation-trained base policy with a real-world residual policy to bridge sim-to-real gaps in robotic assembly, achieving high success with reduced supervision and improved efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the sim-to-real gap in assembly by leveraging strong priors from simulation while adapting to real-world discrepancies via a residual policy trained with visual input and sparse rewards, reducing need for human supervision.

Method: Train a base policy in simulation using low-level state observations and dense rewards; train a real-world residual policy using visual observations and sparse rewards to compensate for dynamics and sensor noise; integrate policies to form SPARR; evaluate on two-part assembly tasks with real-world experiments.

Result: Near-perfect success on diverse two-part tasks; 38.4% higher success rate vs zero-shot sim-to-real SOTA; 29.7% faster cycle time; requires no human expertise, unlike other real-world RL approaches.

Conclusion: Hybrid approach effectively combines simulation priors with real-world adaptation to improve robustness and efficiency in robotic assembly; demonstrates practical viability and reduced need for supervision; potential limitations and scope for broader tasks and longer-horizon tasks could be explored.

Abstract: Robotic assembly presents a long-standing challenge due to its requirement for precise, contact-rich manipulation. While simulation-based learning has enabled the development of robust assembly policies, their performance often degrades when deployed in real-world settings due to the sim-to-real gap. Conversely, real-world reinforcement learning (RL) methods avoid the sim-to-real gap, but rely heavily on human supervision and lack generalization ability to environmental changes. In this work, we propose a hybrid approach that combines a simulation-trained base policy with a real-world residual policy to efficiently adapt to real-world variations. The base policy, trained in simulation using low-level state observations and dense rewards, provides strong priors for initial behavior. The residual policy, learned in the real world using visual observations and sparse rewards, compensates for discrepancies in dynamics and sensor noise. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that our method, SPARR, achieves near-perfect success rates across diverse two-part assembly tasks. Compared to the state-of-the-art zero-shot sim-to-real methods, SPARR improves success rates by 38.4% while reducing cycle time by 29.7%. Moreover, SPARR requires no human expertise, in contrast to the state-of-the-art real-world RL approaches that depend heavily on human supervision.

</details>


### [149] [Simple Models, Real Swimming: Digital Twins for Tendon-Driven Underwater Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23283)
*Mike Y. Michelis,Nana Obayashi,Josie Hughes,Robert K. Katzschmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-calibrated, stateless hydrodynamics model for a tendon-driven fish robot in MuJoCo enabling real-time simulation and scalable learning for underwater control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft aquatic robots pose modeling challenges due to fluid–structure interaction; existing models are computationally expensive and hinder learning-based control.

Method: Develop a tendon-driven fish robot; implement a simplified, stateless hydrodynamics model in MuJoCo; fit five fluid parameters from two real swimming trajectories; test generalization across actuation frequencies and unseen actuation; compare to analytical models; enable reinforcement learning for target tracking.

Result: Simulation runs faster than real-time; five calibrated fluid parameters reproduce experimental behavior and generalize across actuation; outperforms elongated body theory; achieves 93% success in target tracking with RL; open-source environment; demonstrates digital twin viability for soft underwater robots.

Conclusion: Simple, data-calibrated stateless models can serve as effective digital twins for soft underwater robots, enabling scalable learning and control in aquatic environments.

Abstract: Mimicking the graceful motion of swimming animals remains a core challenge in soft robotics due to the complexity of fluid-structure interaction and the difficulty of controlling soft, biomimetic bodies. Existing modeling approaches are often computationally expensive and impractical for complex control or reinforcement learning needed for realistic motions to emerge in robotic systems. In this work, we present a tendon-driven fish robot modeled in an efficient underwater swimmer environment using a simplified, stateless hydrodynamics formulation implemented in the widespread robotics framework MuJoCo. With just two real-world swimming trajectories, we identify five fluid parameters that allow a matching to experimental behavior and generalize across a range of actuation frequencies. We show that this stateless fluid model can generalize to unseen actuation and outperform classical analytical models such as the elongated body theory. This simulation environment runs faster than real-time and can easily enable downstream learning algorithms such as reinforcement learning for target tracking, reaching a 93% success rate. Due to the simplicity and ease of use of the model and our open-source simulation environment, our results show that even simple, stateless models -- when carefully matched to physical data -- can serve as effective digital twins for soft underwater robots, opening up new directions for scalable learning and control in aquatic environments.

</details>


### [150] [Interface-Aware Trajectory Reconstruction of Limited Demonstrations for Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23287)
*Demiana R. Barsoum,Mahdieh Nejati Javaremi,Larisa Y. C. Loke,Brenna D. Argall*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A trajectory reconstruction algorithm lifts low-dimensional interface demonstrations into the robot's full control space, yielding faster, more efficient, and user-preferred motions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users with severe motor impairments control high-DoF robots via low-dimensional interfaces, causing demonstrations that reflect interface limits rather than user intent. Reconstructing full trajectories aims to recover authentic user intent in the robot's space.

Method: Introduce a trajectory reconstruction algorithm that reasons about task goals, environment, and interface constraints to map interface-limited demonstrations into the robot's full (7-DoF) control space. Evaluate with real-world ADL-inspired tasks teleoperated by a 2-D joystick and a 1-D sip-and-puff interface on two distinct 7-DoF arms.

Result: Lifted trajectories are faster and more efficient than their interface-constrained counterparts while respecting user preferences.

Conclusion: The approach enables faster, more efficient robot motion aligned with user intent by compensating for interface limitations without violating task and environmental constraints.

Abstract: Assistive robots offer agency to humans with severe motor impairments. Often, these users control high-DoF robots through low-dimensional interfaces, such as using a 1-D sip-and-puff interface to operate a 6-DoF robotic arm. This mismatch results in having access to only a subset of control dimensions at a given time, imposing unintended and artificial constraints on robot motion. As a result, interface-limited demonstrations embed suboptimal motions that reflect interface restrictions rather than user intent. To address this, we present a trajectory reconstruction algorithm that reasons about task, environment, and interface constraints to lift demonstrations into the robot's full control space. We evaluate our approach using real-world demonstrations of ADL-inspired tasks performed via a 2-D joystick and 1-D sip-and-puff control interface, teleoperating two distinct 7-DoF robotic arms. Analyses of the reconstructed demonstrations and derived control policies show that lifted trajectories are faster and more efficient than their interface-constrained counterparts while respecting user preferences.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [151] [Graph Your Way to Inspiration: Integrating Co-Author Graphs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Model Based Scientific Idea Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22215)
*Pengzhen Xie,Huizhi Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GYWI combines author knowledge graphs with retrieval-augmented generation to provide controllable context and traceable inspiration pathways for LLM-based scientific idea generation; introduces an author-centered KG construction, a hybrid RAG/GraphRAG retrieval, and a prompt optimization strategy via reinforcement learning; evaluated on arXiv data showing improvements in novelty, reliability, and relevance across multiple LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often generate ideas without explicit controllable context or traceable inspiration; external author-centered knowledge bases are needed to steer generation and document inspiration paths.

Method: Construct author-centered knowledge graphs and inspiration source sampling; implement a hybrid retrieval mechanism combining RAG and GraphRAG to form a hybrid context; develop a prompt optimization strategy using reinforcement learning principles to guide LLMs leveraging the hybrid context; evaluation dataset from arXiv (2018-2023); evaluation methods including automatic MCQ assessment, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, and semantic-space visualization.

Result: GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in novelty, reliability, and relevance; experiments across GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, and Gemini 2.5.

Conclusion: Hybrid context from author knowledge graphs and RAG-based retrieval, coupled with prompt optimization, provides controllable context and traceable inspiration for scientific idea generation, yielding higher novelty, reliability, and relevance across diverse LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate potential in the field of scientific idea generation. However, the generated results often lack controllable academic context and traceable inspiration pathways. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a scientific idea generation system called GYWI, which combines author knowledge graphs with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to form an external knowledge base to provide controllable context and trace of inspiration path for LLMs to generate new scientific ideas. We first propose an author-centered knowledge graph construction method and inspiration source sampling algorithms to construct external knowledge base. Then, we propose a hybrid retrieval mechanism that is composed of both RAG and GraphRAG to retrieve content with both depth and breadth knowledge. It forms a hybrid context. Thirdly, we propose a Prompt optimization strategy incorporating reinforcement learning principles to automatically guide LLMs optimizing the results based on the hybrid context. To evaluate the proposed approaches, we constructed an evaluation dataset based on arXiv (2018-2023). This paper also develops a comprehensive evaluation method including empirical automatic assessment in multiple-choice question task, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, and semantic space visualization analysis. The generated ideas are evaluated from the following five dimensions: novelty, feasibility, clarity, relevance, and significance. We conducted experiments on different LLMs including GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, and Gemini 2.5. Experimental results show that GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in multiple metrics such as novelty, reliability, and relevance.

</details>


### [152] [FIRE: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Financial Intelligence and Reasoning Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22273)
*Xiyuan Zhang,Huihang Wu,Jiayu Guo,Zhenlin Zhang,Yiwei Zhang,Liangyu Huo,Xiaoxiao Ma,Jiansong Wan,Xuewei Jiao,Yi Jing,Jian Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark named FIRE assesses LLMs on theoretical financial knowledge and practical financial tasks using exam-style questions and a 3,000-question evaluation set, evaluated across state-of-the-art models (including XuanYuan 4.0) with a public release of questions and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantitatively and comprehensively evaluate LLMs’ financial competence, both in theory and in practical business scenarios, and to map the capability boundaries of current models in finance.

Method: Construct an evaluation framework (matrix) covering financial subdomains; curate theoretical questions from recognized finance qualifications; gather 3,000 scenario questions (closed-form with reference answers and open-ended rubrics); evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs (e.g., XuanYuan 4.0) against FIRE; release questions and evaluation code publicly.

Result: FIRE enables systematic analysis of LLM capabilities in finance and identifies capability boundaries; provides a benchmark and release that supports ongoing research in financial AI.

Conclusion: FIRE offers a comprehensive, open benchmark for assessing both theoretical and practical financial proficiency of LLMs, promoting reproducibility and future advancements in financial-domain AI.

Abstract: We introduce FIRE, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both the theoretical financial knowledge of LLMs and their ability to handle practical business scenarios. For theoretical assessment, we curate a diverse set of examination questions drawn from widely recognized financial qualification exams, enabling evaluation of LLMs deep understanding and application of financial knowledge. In addition, to assess the practical value of LLMs in real-world financial tasks, we propose a systematic evaluation matrix that categorizes complex financial domains and ensures coverage of essential subdomains and business activities. Based on this evaluation matrix, we collect 3,000 financial scenario questions, consisting of closed-form decision questions with reference answers and open-ended questions evaluated by predefined rubrics. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of state-of-the-art LLMs on the FIRE benchmark, including XuanYuan 4.0, our latest financial-domain model, as a strong in-domain baseline. These results enable a systematic analysis of the capability boundaries of current LLMs in financial applications. We publicly release the benchmark questions and evaluation code to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [153] [Multi-Level Causal Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22287)
*Willem Schooltink,Fabio Massimo Zennaro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, enabling multiple detailed causal models to be mapped into sub-systems of a coarser model; introduces a multi-resolution marginal problem and shows applications to statistical/causal marginal problems and data merging across representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fragmentation of causal models across representations and the need to preserve causal relations during coarsening; extend abstraction to embeddings to support many-to-subsystem mappings and dataset integration.

Method: Formally define causal embeddings, introduce a generalized consistency notion, define multi-resolution marginal problem; relate it to statistical and causal marginal problems; demonstrate with merging datasets from differently represented models.

Result: Provides a theoretical framework showing causal embeddings are useful for resolving marginal problems and for data fusion across heterogeneous representations, with implications for merging datasets from models with different representations.

Conclusion: Causal embeddings generalize abstraction to multi-model settings, enabling consistent coarsening and practical dataset merging across heterogeneous causal representations.

Abstract: Abstractions of causal models allow for the coarsening of models such that relations of cause and effect are preserved. Whereas abstractions focus on the relation between two models, in this paper we study a framework for causal embeddings which enable multiple detailed models to be mapped into sub-systems of a coarser causal model. We define causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, and present a generalized notion of consistency. By defining a multi-resolution marginal problem, we showcase the relevance of causal embeddings for both the statistical marginal problem and the causal marginal problem; furthermore, we illustrate its practical use in merging datasets coming from models with different representations.

</details>


### [154] [Agent Behavioral Contracts: Formal Specification and Runtime Enforcement for Reliable Autonomous AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22302)
*Varun Pratap Bhardwaj*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ABC introduces Design-by-Contract principles for autonomous AI agents by defining runtime-enforceable contracts with Precondition, Invariant, Governance, and Recovery components. It formalizes a probabilistic satisfaction notion and proves a Drift Bounds Theorem for drift control under recovery, with composition conditions and degradation bounds. Implemented as AgentAssert and evaluated on AgentContract-Bench with 1,980 sessions across 7 models from 6 vendors. Demonstrates detection of soft violations, high hard-constraint compliance, drift bound (D*<0.27), and low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between informal prompts and formal behavioral specifications to reduce drift, governance failures, and project failures in agentic AI deployments.

Method: Define ABC as C=(P,I,G,R); formalize (p, delta, k)-satisfaction; prove Drift Bounds Theorem; derive composition and degradation bounds for multi-agent chains; implement as AgentAssert; evaluate on AgentContract-Bench (200 scenarios, 7 models, 6 vendors) across 1,980 sessions.

Result: Contracted agents detect 5.2-6.8 soft violations per session missed by uncontracted baselines (p<0.0001, Cohen's d=6.7-33.8); achieve 88-100% hard constraint compliance; bound drift to D*<0.27; 100% recovery for frontier models and 17-100% across models; overhead <10 ms per action.

Conclusion: ABC provides a principled, runtime-enforceable framework that improves reliability and governance of autonomous AI agents, with practical feasibility demonstrated through implementation and empirical evaluation.

Abstract: Traditional software relies on contracts -- APIs, type systems, assertions -- to specify and enforce correct behavior. AI agents, by contrast, operate on prompts and natural language instructions with no formal behavioral specification. This gap is the root cause of drift, governance failures, and frequent project failures in agentic AI deployments. We introduce Agent Behavioral Contracts (ABC), a formal framework that brings Design-by-Contract principles to autonomous AI agents. An ABC contract C = (P, I, G, R) specifies Preconditions, Invariants, Governance policies, and Recovery mechanisms as first-class, runtime-enforceable components. We define (p, delta, k)-satisfaction -- a probabilistic notion of contract compliance that accounts for LLM non-determinism and recovery -- and prove a Drift Bounds Theorem showing that contracts with recovery rate gamma > alpha (the natural drift rate) bound behavioral drift to D* = alpha/gamma in expectation, with Gaussian concentration in the stochastic setting. We establish sufficient conditions for safe contract composition in multi-agent chains and derive probabilistic degradation bounds. We implement ABC in AgentAssert, a runtime enforcement library, and evaluate on AgentContract-Bench, a benchmark of 200 scenarios across 7 models from 6 vendors. Results across 1,980 sessions show that contracted agents detect 5.2-6.8 soft violations per session that uncontracted baselines miss entirely (p < 0.0001, Cohen's d = 6.7-33.8), achieve 88-100% hard constraint compliance, and bound behavioral drift to D* < 0.27 across extended sessions, with 100% recovery for frontier models and 17-100% across all models, at overhead < 10 ms per action.

</details>


### [155] [Vibe Researching as Wolf Coming: Can AI Agents with Skills Replace or Augment Social Scientists?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22401)
*Yongjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI agents enable end-to-end, autonomous research pipelines (vibe researching) and show speed/coverage advantages but lag in originality and tacit knowledge; a cognitive delegation boundary across the entire pipeline is proposed, with five responsible-practice principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how autonomous AI-enabled research workflows will transform social science work, anticipating impacts on practice, education, and professional stratification.

Method: - Define vibe researching as AI-driven end-to-end research workflows.
- Operationalize using a 21-skill Claude Code plugin as a case study for the full pipeline from idea to submission.
- Develop a cognitive task framework with two dimensions (codifiability and tacit knowledge) to locate a non-sequential delegation boundary that spans the entire pipeline.
- Analyze implications and propose five principles for responsible vibe researching.

Result: - Proposes a cognitive delegation boundary that cuts across all stages of the research pipeline.
- Presents a conceptual framework contrasting codifiability vs tacit knowledge to map delegation potential and limits.
- Identifies three profession-level implications: augmentation under fragile conditions, stratification risk, and a pedagogical crisis.
- Articulates five guiding principles for responsible vibe researching to govern adoption and practice.

Conclusion: AI agents offer speed, coverage, and methodological scaffolding but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge; responsible adoption requires governance, training, and consideration of social consequences via the five principles.

Abstract: AI agents -- systems that execute multi-step reasoning workflows with persistent state, tool access, and specialist skills -- represent a qualitative shift from prior automation technologies in social science. Unlike chatbots that respond to isolated queries, AI agents can now read files, run code, query databases, search the web, and invoke domain-specific skills to execute entire research pipelines autonomously. This paper introduces the concept of vibe researching -- the AI-era parallel to ``vibe coding'' (Karpathy, 2025) -- and uses scholar-skill, a 21-skill plugin for Claude Code covering the full research pipeline from idea to submission, as an illustrative case. I develop a cognitive task framework that classifies research activities along two dimensions -- codifiability and tacit knowledge requirement -- to identify a delegation boundary that is cognitive, not sequential: it cuts through every stage of the research pipeline, not between stages. I argue that AI agents excel at speed, coverage, and methodological scaffolding but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge. The paper concludes with an analysis of three implications for the profession -- augmentation with fragile conditions, stratification risk, and a pedagogical crisis -- and proposes five principles for responsible vibe researching.

</details>


### [156] [Towards Autonomous Memory Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22406)
*Xinle Wu,Rui Zhang,Mustafa Anis Hussain,Yao Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autonomous memory agents (U-Mem) proactively acquire and curate knowledge via a cost-aware extraction cascade and semantic-aware Thompson sampling, yielding superior memory-augmented LLM performance and surpassing RL-based optimization on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome passive, bounded, and reactive memory in prior systems; enable continuous, low-cost online memory expansion with uncertainty-driven inputs.

Method: Two components: (i) a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade from cheap self/teacher signals to tool-verified research, escalating to expert feedback as needed; (ii) semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration/exploitation over memories and reduce cold-start bias.

Result: Consistent improvements over prior memory baselines on verifiable/non-verifiable benchmarks; surpass RL-based optimization; HotpotQA +14.6 points (Qwen2.5-7B); AIME25 +7.33 points (Gemini-2.5-flash).

Conclusion: Autonomous, cost-aware memory extraction and uncertainty-driven memory selection enable proactive knowledge curation, enhancing memory-augmented LLMs across tasks.

Abstract: Recent memory agents improve LLMs by extracting experiences and conversation history into an external storage. This enables low-overhead context assembly and online memory update without expensive LLM training. However, existing solutions remain passive and reactive; memory growth is bounded by information that happens to be available, while memory agents seldom seek external inputs in uncertainties. We propose autonomous memory agents that actively acquire, validate, and curate knowledge at a minimum cost. U-Mem materializes this idea via (i) a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade that escalates from cheap self/teacher signals to tool-verified research and, only when needed, expert feedback, and (ii) semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration and exploitation over memories and mitigate cold-start bias. On both verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks, U-Mem consistently beats prior memory baselines and can surpass RL-based optimization, improving HotpotQA (Qwen2.5-7B) by 14.6 points and AIME25 (Gemini-2.5-flash) by 7.33 points.

</details>


### [157] [Exploring Human Behavior During Abstract Rule Inference and Problem Solving with the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22408)
*Caroline Ahn,Quan Do,Leah Bakst,Michael P. Pascale,Joseph T. McGuire,Michael E. Hasselmo,Chantal E. Stern*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CogARC is a human-adapted ARC subset designed to study abstract reasoning. Across two experiments with 260 participants solving 75 problems, participants achieved high accuracy but showed substantial strategy variability and rich time-resolved behavior, offering insights into generalization and misgeneralization under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To illuminate cognitive strategies underlying rapid, sparse-example rule learning and to provide a human-centric benchmark for abstract reasoning, bridging gaps between cognitive science and AI.

Method: Two experiments (n=40 and n=220) administered to 260 participants who freely generated solutions to 75 abstract visual problems. High-temporal-resolution data captured example viewing, edits, and multi-attempt submissions. Analyses focused on accuracy, response times, solution trajectories, and convergence/divergence of strategies, with attention to problem difficulty and learning effects.

Result: Participants were generally successful (mean accuracy ~90% in exp1, ~80% in exp2). Performance varied across problems and participants. Harder problems induced longer deliberation and greater strategy divergence. Over time, responses were initiated more quickly, but accuracy declined slightly, suggesting familiarity with task structure rather than improved rule-learning. Even incorrect solutions tended to converge, with trajectories ranging from direct, efficient convergence to extended exploration or restarts before reaching stability.

Conclusion: CogARC offers a rich behavioral platform for studying human abstract reasoning, including how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt strategies under uncertainty; findings have implications for cognitive theories of abstraction and for developing AI systems that emulate human rule discovery.

Abstract: Humans exhibit remarkable flexibility in abstract reasoning, and can rapidly learn and apply rules from sparse examples. To investigate the cognitive strategies underlying this ability, we introduce the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (CogARC), a diverse human-adapted subset of the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) which was originally developed to benchmark abstract reasoning in artificial intelligence. Across two experiments, CogARC was administered to a total of 260 human participants who freely generated solutions to 75 abstract visual reasoning problems. Success required inferring input-output rules from a small number of examples to transform the test input into one correct test output. Participants' behavior was recorded at high temporal resolution, including example viewing, edit sequences, and multi-attempt submissions. Participants were generally successful (mean accuracy ~90% for experiment 1 (n=40), ~80% for experiment 2 (n=220) across problems), but performance varied widely across problems and participants. Harder problems elicited longer deliberation times and greater divergence in solution strategies. Over the course of the task, participants initiated responses more quickly but showed a slight decline in accuracy, suggesting increased familiarity with the task structure rather than improved rule-learning ability. Importantly, even incorrect solutions were often highly convergent, even when the problem-solving trajectories differed in length and smoothness. Some trajectories progressed directly and efficiently toward a stable outcome, whereas others involved extended exploration or partial restarts before converging. Together, these findings highlight CogARC as a rich behavioral environment for studying human abstract reasoning, providing insight into how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt their strategies under uncertainty.

</details>


### [158] [Epistemic Filtering and Collective Hallucination: A Jury Theorem for Confidence-Calibrated Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22413)
*Jonas Karge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A selective-participation framework with calibration and confidence gating extends the Condorcet Jury Theorem to sequential abstention, with non-asymptotic guarantees and Monte Carlo validation; potential AI safety benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CJTs assume fixed participation; real-world settings include abstention. Allowing agents to abstain after learning their own competence can improve group accuracy and robustness, with relevance to AI safety and reducing hallucinations.

Method: Probabilistic calibration phase where agents update beliefs about fixed competence, followed by a final confidence gate deciding voting vs abstention; derive a non-asymptotic lower bound on group success probability; prove the selective participation generalizes CJT to a sequential, confidence-gated setting; Monte Carlo simulations for validation.

Result: Derived non-asymptotic lower bound; theoretical generalization of CJT to sequential gating; empirical validation via Monte Carlo; discussion on AI safety applications to mitigate hallucinations in aggregate LLM decisions.

Conclusion: Selective participation improves collective accuracy; the CJT guarantees extend to a sequential, confidence-gated framework; framework offers practical utility for AI systems where abstention reduces erroneous consensus.

Abstract: We investigate the collective accuracy of heterogeneous agents who learn to estimate their own reliability over time and selectively abstain from voting. While classical epistemic voting results, such as the \textit{Condorcet Jury Theorem} (CJT), assume fixed participation, real-world aggregation often benefits from allowing agents to say ``I don't know.'' We propose a probabilistic framework where agents engage in a \textit{calibration} phase, updating beliefs about their own fixed competence, before facing a final confidence gate that determines whether to vote or abstain. We derive a non-asymptotic lower bound on the group's success probability and prove that this \textit{selective participation} generalizes the asymptotic guarantees of the CJT to a sequential, confidence-gated setting. Empirically, we validate these bounds via Monte Carlo simulations. While our results are general, we discuss their potential application to AI safety, outlining how this framework can mitigate \textit{hallucinations} in collective LLM decision-making.

</details>


### [159] [ArchAgent: Agentic AI-driven Computer Architecture Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22425)
*Raghav Gupta,Akanksha Jain,Abraham Gonzalez,Alexander Novikov,Po-Sen Huang,Matej Balog,Marvin Eisenberger,Sergey Shirobokov,Ngân Vũ,Martin Dixon,Borivoje Nikolić,Parthasarathy Ranganathan,Sagar Karandikar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ArchAgent uses AlphaEvolve to automatically design cache replacement policies beyond parameter tuning, achieving SoTA IPC gains on Google traces (5.3%) and SPEC06 (0.9%), discovering policies 3–5x faster than humans, plus post-silicon tuning (2.4%), and highlighting simulator-escape risks and broader implications for agentic hardware design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To meet growing compute demand by accelerating hardware design with agentic AI, enabling automatic discovery of novel cache replacement mechanisms rather than mere parameter tweaks.

Method: An automated search/discovery framework (ArchAgent) built atop AlphaEvolve that searches for novel cache-replacement logic; evaluated on public workloads (Google Workload Traces, SPEC06); includes post-silicon runtime-parameter tuning; discusses potential simulator manipulation by agents.

Result: Claims include 5.3% IPC speedup over the prior SoTA on multi-core traces, 0.9% on SPEC06, ArchAgent achieving these gains 3–5x faster than prior human-developed policies, and 2.4% IPC gain from post-silicon tuning; also notes a ‘simulator escape’ phenomenon.

Conclusion: Agentic AI can substantially accelerate hardware design and policy discovery, but raises concerns about evaluation breadth, reproducibility, overfitting to workloads, and simulator vulnerabilities; warrants broader validation and safeguards for future agentic hardware research.

Abstract: Agile hardware design flows are a critically needed force multiplier to meet the exploding demand for compute. Recently, agentic generative AI systems have demonstrated significant advances in algorithm design, improving code efficiency, and enabling discovery across scientific domains.
  Bridging these worlds, we present ArchAgent, an automated computer architecture discovery system built on AlphaEvolve.
