Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes stochasticity in DRAs via information acquisition MDP; identifies three variance sources; shows reducing stochasticity improves output quality; achieves 22% reduction on DeepSearchQA with maintained quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance reliability and quality of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) in real-world deployment by understanding and mitigating stochasticity in their information acquisition, compression, and inference processes.

Method: Model DRAs as information acquisition Markov Decision Processes; quantify variance from information acquisition, information compression, and inference; conduct controlled experiments across decision steps to assess impact on outputs; propose mitigation via structured outputs and ensemble-based query generation; evaluate on DeepSearchQA.

Result: Variance is primarily driven by inference and early-stage stochasticity; reducing stochasticity improves output quality; proposed methods reduce average stochasticity by 22% while preserving high research quality.

Conclusion: Stochasticity in DRAs is tractable; mitigation strategies (structured outputs, ensembles) can reduce variance without sacrificing quality, enhancing reliability for deployment.

Abstract: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) are promising agentic systems that gather and synthesize information to support research across domains such as financial decision-making, medical analysis, and scientific discovery. Despite recent improvements in research quality (e.g., outcome accuracy when ground truth is available), DRA system design often overlooks a critical barrier to real-world deployment: stochasticity. Under identical queries, repeated executions of DRAs can exhibit substantial variability in terms of research outcome, findings, and citations. In this paper, we formalize the study of stochasticity in DRAs by modeling them as information acquisition Markov Decision Processes. We introduce an evaluation framework that quantifies variance in the system and identify three sources of it: information acquisition, information compression, and inference. Through controlled experiments, we investigate how stochasticity from these modules across different decision steps influences the variance of DRA outputs. Our results show that reducing stochasticity can improve research output quality, with inference and early-stage stochasticity contributing the most to DRA output variance. Based on these findings, we propose strategies for mitigating stochasticity while maintaining output quality via structured output and ensemble-based query generation. Our experiments on DeepSearchQA show that our proposed mitigation methods reduce average stochasticity by 22% while maintaining high research quality.

</details>


### [213] [CXReasonAgent: Evidence-Grounded Diagnostic Reasoning Agent for Chest X-rays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23276)
*Hyungyung Lee,Hangyul Yoon,Edward Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CXReasonAgent integrates an LLM with clinically grounded diagnostic tools to perform evidence-grounded diagnostic reasoning from chest X-ray, and introduces CXReasonDial benchmark (1,946 dialogues across 12 tasks) showing improved faithful grounding and verifiability over LVLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the tendency of large vision-language models to produce plausible but ungrounded or unverifiable diagnostics in medical imaging; need reliable, verifiable, adaptable AI for clinical chest radiography.

Method: Develop CXReasonAgent by coupling an LLM with clinically grounded diagnostic tools and image-derived evidence; evaluate on CXReasonDial, a multi-turn dialogue benchmark with 1,946 dialogues across 12 diagnostic tasks.

Result: CXReasonAgent yields faithfully grounded responses and demonstrates more reliable and verifiable diagnostic reasoning than LVLMs, according to the benchmark results described.

Conclusion: Integrating clinically grounded diagnostic tools with LLMs is essential for safe, reliable AI in clinical chest radiography and potentially other medical imaging domains.

Abstract: Chest X-ray plays a central role in thoracic diagnosis, and its interpretation inherently requires multi-step, evidence-grounded reasoning. However, large vision-language models (LVLMs) often generate plausible responses that are not faithfully grounded in diagnostic evidence and provide limited visual evidence for verification, while also requiring costly retraining to support new diagnostic tasks, limiting their reliability and adaptability in clinical settings. To address these limitations, we present CXReasonAgent, a diagnostic agent that integrates a large language model (LLM) with clinically grounded diagnostic tools to perform evidence-grounded diagnostic reasoning using image-derived diagnostic and visual evidence. To evaluate these capabilities, we introduce CXReasonDial, a multi-turn dialogue benchmark with 1,946 dialogues across 12 diagnostic tasks, and show that CXReasonAgent produces faithfully grounded responses, enabling more reliable and verifiable diagnostic reasoning than LVLMs. These findings highlight the importance of integrating clinically grounded diagnostic tools, particularly in safety-critical clinical settings.

</details>


### [214] [ODEBrain: Continuous-Time EEG Graph for Modeling Dynamic Brain Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23285)
*Haohui Jia,Zheng Chen,Lingwei Zhu,Rikuto Kotoge,Jathurshan Pradeepkumar,Yasuko Matsubara,Jimeng Sun,Yasushi Sakurai,Takashi Matsubara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Neural ODE-based framework (ODEBRAIN) for continuous-time latent dynamics forecasting of EEG using spectral graph features, improving robustness and generalization over discretized recurrent latent variable models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional latent variable models discretize time via recurrent architectures, causing cumulative prediction errors and failing to capture instantaneous, nonlinear EEG dynamics; need continuous-time modeling of neural population dynamics.

Method: Construct spectral graph nodes from combined spatio-temporal-frequency EEG features; apply Neural Ordinary Differential Equations to model continuous latent dynamics; train for forecasting and capture stochastic variations at arbitrary time points.

Result: Empirical experiments show significant improvement over existing methods in EEG dynamic forecasting, with enhanced robustness and generalization.

Conclusion: ODEBRAIN provides a principled continuous-time latent dynamics framework that better captures instantaneous nonlinear brain activity and stochastic variability, offering improved EEG forecasting and potential clinical/neuroscientific utility.

Abstract: Modeling neural population dynamics is crucial for foundational neuroscientific research and various clinical applications. Conventional latent variable methods typically model continuous brain dynamics through discretizing time with recurrent architecture, which necessarily results in compounded cumulative prediction errors and failure of capturing instantaneous, nonlinear characteristics of EEGs. We propose ODEBRAIN, a Neural ODE latent dynamic forecasting framework to overcome these challenges by integrating spatio-temporal-frequency features into spectral graph nodes, followed by a Neural ODE modeling the continuous latent dynamics. Our design ensures that latent representations can capture stochastic variations of complex brain states at any given time point. Extensive experiments verify that ODEBRAIN can improve significantly over existing methods in forecasting EEG dynamics with enhanced robustness and generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [215] [The logic of KM belief update is contained in the logic of AGM belief revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23302)
*Giacomo Bonanno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KM-style belief update axioms are captured within a modal language with B, >, and □; AGM revision axioms embed within KM, so AGM is a special case of KM update; for the strong KM update, the remaining difference from AGM collapses to a single axiom about unsurprising information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to compare KM belief update with AGM belief revision, establishing a formal inclusion and clarifying what distinguishes KM from AGM.

Method: formally translate both KM and AGM axiom systems into modal languages L_KM and L_AGM, prove that every L_KM axiom is a theorem of L_AGM, and analyze the strong KM variant to identify the sole axiom responsible for the remaining gap.

Result: every axiom of L_KM is derivable in L_AGM; hence AGM revision is a special case of KM update; in the strong KM variant, the difference from L_AGM reduces to one axiom concerning unsurprising information.

Conclusion: AGM belief revision can be viewed as a specific instance of KM belief update; the only substantive gap under the strong KM regime is an axiom about information not initially disbelieved, isolating the core distinction.

Abstract: For each axiom of KM belief update we provide a corresponding axiom in a modal logic containing three modal operators: a unimodal belief operator $B$, a bimodal conditional operator $>$ and the unimodal necessity operator $\square$. We then compare the resulting logic to the similar logic obtained from converting the AGM axioms of belief revision into modal axioms and show that the latter contains the former. Denoting the latter by $\mathcal L_{AGM}$ and the former by $\mathcal L_{KM}$ we show that every axiom of $\mathcal L_{KM}$ is a theorem of $\mathcal L_{AGM}$. Thus AGM belief revision can be seen as a special case of KM belief update. For the strong version of KM belief update we show that the difference between $\mathcal L_{KM}$ and $\mathcal L_{AGM}$ can be narrowed down to a single axiom, which deals exclusively with unsurprising information, that is, with formulas that were not initially disbelieved.

</details>


### [216] [Invariant Transformation and Resampling based Epistemic-Uncertainty Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23315)
*Sha Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Resampling-based inference using transformed inputs to reduce epistemic uncertainty via aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in AI inference (epistemic and aleatoric) leads to errors. Observations that errors across invariant input transformations can be partially independent suggest a way to improve accuracy without solely increasing model size.

Method: Generate multiple transformed versions of each input that preserve the underlying semantics, run the trained model on each transformed input, and aggregate the multiple outputs (e.g., averaging, voting) to form a final prediction. This forms a runtime ensemble or resampling scheme.

Result: Expected improvement in inference accuracy via ensemble-like aggregation; potential robustness gains and reduced susceptibility to model misspecification; preserves a path to balance computational cost (through transform count) and performance.

Conclusion: Resampling-based inference is a promising strategy to boost accuracy and manage the size-performance trade-off. Empirical validation across domains and a study of optimal transformation sets and aggregation rules are warranted.

Abstract: An artificial intelligence (AI) model can be viewed as a function that maps inputs to outputs in high-dimensional spaces. Once designed and well trained, the AI model is applied for inference. However, even optimized AI models can produce inference errors due to aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties. Interestingly, we observed that when inferring multiple samples based on invariant transformations of an input, inference errors can show partial independences due to epistemic uncertainty. Leveraging this insight, we propose a "resampling" based inferencing that applies to a trained AI model with multiple transformed versions of an input, and aggregates inference outputs to a more accurate result. This approach has the potential to improve inference accuracy and offers a strategy for balancing model size and performance.

</details>


### [217] [Generalized Rapid Action Value Estimation in Memory-Constrained Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23318)
*Aloïs Rautureau,Tristan Cazenave,Éric Piette*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAVE2, GRAVER, and GRAVER2 offer memory-efficient variants of GRAVE for General Game Playing by adding two-level search and node recycling, achieving similar strength with far fewer stored nodes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRAVE's memory overhead due to additional win/visit statistics per node makes it impractical in memory-constrained settings, limiting its real-world applicability in GGP.

Method: Introduce two-level search, node recycling, and a combination of both techniques to extend GRAVE. Each variant reduces stored nodes while aiming to preserve GRAVE's playing strength.

Result: Drastic reduction in the number of stored nodes while matching the playing strength of GRAVE.

Conclusion: The proposed GRAVE2, GRAVER, and GRAVER2 variants enable practical deployment of GRAVE-like strength in memory-limited environments and broaden GRAVE's applicability in General Game Playing.

Abstract: Generalized Rapid Action Value Estimation (GRAVE) has been shown to be a strong variant within the Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) family of algorithms for General Game Playing (GGP). However, its reliance on storing additional win/visit statistics at each node makes its use impractical in memory-constrained environments, thereby limiting its applicability in practice. In this paper, we introduce the GRAVE2, GRAVER and GRAVER2 algorithms, which extend GRAVE through two-level search, node recycling, and a combination of both techniques, respectively. We show that these enhancements enable a drastic reduction in the number of stored nodes while matching the playing strength of GRAVE.

</details>


### [218] [LLM Novice Uplift on Dual-Use, In Silico Biology Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23329)
*Chen Bo Calvin Zhang,Christina Q. Knight,Nicholas Kruus,Jason Hausenloy,Pedro Medeiros,Nathaniel Li,Aiden Kim,Yury Orlovskiy,Coleman Breen,Bryce Cai,Jasper Götting,Andrew Bo Liu,Samira Nedungadi,Paula Rodriguez,Yannis Yiming He,Mohamed Shaaban,Zifan Wang,Seth Donoughe,Julian Michael*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-model, multi-benchmark study shows novices with LLMs outperform internet-only users by ~4.16x on eight biosecurity tasks; in several benchmarks with expert baselines, novices with LLMs match or exceed experts; standalone LLMs can outperform LLM-assisted users; results highlight substantial uplift and the need for ongoing uplift evaluations alongside benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether access to large language models meaningfully uplifts novices on biosecurity-relevant tasks, addressing questions of scientific acceleration and dual-use risk beyond standard benchmarks.

Method: A multi-model, multi-benchmark human uplift study where novices were given LLM access or internet-only access across eight biosecurity-relevant task sets. Tasks allowed up to 13 hours total work. Compare accuracy against controls and, where available, expert baselines.

Result: LLM access yielded a 4.16× accuracy uplift (95% CI [2.63, 6.87]) for novices. On four benchmarks with expert baselines, novices with LLMs outperformed experts on three. Standalone LLMs frequently surpassed LLM-assisted novices, suggesting suboptimal elicitation of LLM contributions by users. Most participants (∼89.6%) reported little difficulty obtaining dual-use information despite safeguards.

Conclusion: LLMs substantially uplift novices on complex biological tasks that were previously the domain of trained practitioners. The findings support continuing, interactive uplift evaluations alongside traditional benchmarks to understand performance gains and dual-use implications.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) perform increasingly well on biology benchmarks, but it remains unclear whether they uplift novice users -- i.e., enable humans to perform better than with internet-only resources. This uncertainty is central to understanding both scientific acceleration and dual-use risk. We conducted a multi-model, multi-benchmark human uplift study comparing novices with LLM access versus internet-only access across eight biosecurity-relevant task sets. Participants worked on complex problems with ample time (up to 13 hours for the most involved tasks). We found that LLM access provided substantial uplift: novices with LLMs were 4.16 times more accurate than controls (95% CI [2.63, 6.87]). On four benchmarks with available expert baselines (internet-only), novices with LLMs outperformed experts on three of them. Perhaps surprisingly, standalone LLMs often exceeded LLM-assisted novices, indicating that users were not eliciting the strongest available contributions from the LLMs. Most participants (89.6%) reported little difficulty obtaining dual-use-relevant information despite safeguards. Overall, LLMs substantially uplift novices on biological tasks previously reserved for trained practitioners, underscoring the need for sustained, interactive uplift evaluations alongside traditional benchmarks.

</details>


### [219] [Toward Expert Investment Teams:A Multi-Agent LLM System with Fine-Grained Trading Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23330)
*Kunihiro Miyazaki,Takanobu Kawahara,Stephen Roberts,Stefan Zohren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-grained task decomposition in multi-agent LLM trading improves risk-adjusted returns over coarse-grained designs on Japanese stock data, with alignment between analytics and decision preferences being key; standard portfolio optimization that reduces correlation with index and variance further boosts performance; design implications for agent structure/task configuration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of coarse-grained prompts in multi-agent LLM trading, aiming to improve inference quality, transparency, and practical performance by decomposing investment analysis into explicit sub-tasks.

Method: Propose a multi-agent LLM framework with explicit fine-grained task decomposition; evaluate on Japanese stock data (prices, statements, news, macro) under leakage-controlled backtesting; analyze intermediate agent outputs for alignment; perform standard portfolio optimization leveraging low index correlation and output variance.

Result: Fine-grained task decomposition enhances risk-adjusted returns relative to coarse-grained designs; alignment between analytical outputs and downstream decision preferences is a key performance driver; portfolio optimization approach yields superior results.

Conclusion: Agent architecture and task configuration are critical for effective LLM-based trading systems; findings inform practical design choices for agent roles and task granularity to improve performance and transparency in financial trading.

Abstract: The advancement of large language models (LLMs) has accelerated the development of autonomous financial trading systems. While mainstream approaches deploy multi-agent systems mimicking analyst and manager roles, they often rely on abstract instructions that overlook the intricacies of real-world workflows, which can lead to degraded inference performance and less transparent decision-making. Therefore, we propose a multi-agent LLM trading framework that explicitly decomposes investment analysis into fine-grained tasks, rather than providing coarse-grained instructions. We evaluate the proposed framework using Japanese stock data, including prices, financial statements, news, and macro information, under a leakage-controlled backtesting setting. Experimental results show that fine-grained task decomposition significantly improves risk-adjusted returns compared to conventional coarse-grained designs. Crucially, further analysis of intermediate agent outputs suggests that alignment between analytical outputs and downstream decision preferences is a critical driver of system performance. Moreover, we conduct standard portfolio optimization, exploiting low correlation with the stock index and the variance of each system's output. This approach achieves superior performance. These findings contribute to the design of agent structure and task configuration when applying LLM agents to trading systems in practical settings.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [220] [Flip Distance of Triangulations of Convex Polygons / Rotation Distance of Binary Trees is NP-complete](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22874)
*Joseph Dorfer*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: NP-hardness of shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons; settles rotation distance complexity; extends flip-sequence techniques from non-crossing spanning trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the long-standing question of the complexity of transforming triangulations via flips; connect to rotation distance of binary trees and the 1-skeleton of the Associahedron (Tamari lattice) to understand computational limits.

Method: Develops flip-sequence techniques, adapting counterparts introduced for non-crossing spanning trees (Bjerkevik et al. SODA25; SoCG26).

Result: Proves that computing the minimum number of flips to transform one triangulation into another is NP-hard; equivalently, computing rotation distance between binary trees is NP-hard.

Conclusion: Provides the first strong complexity result for flip-based transformations in triangulations and binary trees; lays groundwork for further hardness and approximation results in combinatorial polyhedra and related lattice structures.

Abstract: Flips in triangulations of convex polygons arise in many different settings. They are isomorphic to rotations in binary trees, define edges in the 1-skeleton of the Associahedron and cover relations in the Tamari Lattice.
  The complexity of determining the minimum number of flips that transform one triangulation of a convex point set into another remained a tantalizing open question for many decades. We settle this question by proving that computing shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons, and therefore also computing the rotation distance of binary trees, is NP-hard.
  For our proof we develop techniques for flip sequences of triangulations whose counterparts were introduced for the study of flip sequences of non-crossing spanning trees by Bjerkevik, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SODA25] and Bjerkevik, Dorfer, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SoCG26].

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [221] [To Deceive is to Teach? Forging Perceptual Robustness via Adversarial Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22227)
*Yicheng Bao,Xuhong Wang,Xin Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOT-SFT provides a large adversarial dataset and an adversarial opponent training (AOT) self-play framework where an Attacker edits images to continually challenge a Defender MLLM, yielding improved perceptual robustness and fewer hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs show perceptual fragility in complex visuals due to reliance on finite, expensive-to-scale datasets, which limits robustness and increases hallucinations; a scalable training paradigm is needed to bootstrap robustness.

Method: Introduce AOT-SFT (adversarial dataset) and AOT (self-play framework). AOT uses a co-evolving system: an Attacker that edits images to create a dynamic curriculum of perturbations, and a Defender MLLM that learns to adapt to these edits through self-generated training data.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows enhanced perceptual robustness of the Defender and reduced hallucinations under adversarial manipulations; demonstrates scalability and effectiveness of the self-play paradigm for robust MLLMs.

Conclusion: AOT provides a scalable, self-supervised adversarial training paradigm for improving the reliability of MLLMs by continually challenging them with adversarial visual manipulations.

Abstract: Despite their impressive capabilities, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit perceptual fragility when confronted with visually complex scenes. This weakness stems from a reliance on finite training datasets, which are prohibitively expensive to scale and impose a ceiling on model robustness. We introduce \textbf{AOT-SFT}, a large-scale adversarial dataset for bootstrapping MLLM robustness. Building on this, we propose \textbf{AOT (Adversarial Opponent Training)}, a self-play framework that forges MLLM robustness by creating its own training data. Our method orchestrates a co-evolution between an image-editing Attacker and a Defender MLLM, where the Attacker generates a diverse and dynamic curriculum of image manipulations, forcing the Defender to adapt and improve. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AOT enhances the Defender's perceptual robustness and reduces hallucinations, establishing a scalable paradigm for training more reliable MLLMs.

</details>


### [222] [Patient-Centered, Graph-Augmented Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Passive Surveillance for Early Stroke Risk Detection in High-Risk Individuals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22228)
*Jiyeong Kim,Stephen P. Ma,Nirali Vora,Nicholas W. Larsen,Julia Adler-Milstein,Jonathan H. Chen,Selen Bozkurt,Abeed Sarker,Juhee Cho,Jindeok Joo,Natali Pageler,Fatima Rodriguez,Christopher Sharp,Eleni Linos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Passive, diabetes-focused stroke risk detection via patient-reported symptoms; builds a symptom taxonomy with a dual ML pipeline (heterogeneous GNN + EN/LASSO); creates a hybrid risk screener with temporal proximity; evaluated 3–90 day windows in EHR simulations; achieved near-perfect specificity/PPV and good sensitivity, highest at 90 days.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in patient-recognized stroke symptoms to reduce care delays by leveraging patient language and automated risk screening within high-risk diabetic populations.

Method: Construct a symptom taxonomy from patient language; develop a dual ML pipeline combining a heterogeneous graph neural network and elastic-net/LASSO for symptom-to-risk mapping; integrate symptom relevance with temporal proximity into a hybrid risk screening system; evaluate performance using EHR-based simulations across 3–90 day windows, with conservative thresholds to minimize false alerts.

Result: The screening system achieved specificity of 1.00 and prevalence-adjusted PPV of 1.00, with sensitivity ~0.72; precision prioritized, with highest performance in the 90-day window.

Conclusion: Patient-reported language enables high-precision, low-burden early stroke risk detection, potentially providing a valuable time window for clinical evaluation and intervention in high-risk individuals.

Abstract: Stroke affected millions annually, yet poor symptom recognition often delayed care-seeking. To address risk recognition gap, we developed a passive surveillance system for early stroke risk detection using patient-reported symptoms among individuals with diabetes. Constructing a symptom taxonomy grounded in patients own language and a dual machine learning pipeline (heterogeneous GNN and EN/LASSO), we identified symptom patterns associated with subsequent stroke. We translated findings into a hybrid risk screening system integrating symptom relevance and temporal proximity, evaluated across 3-90 day windows through EHR-based simulations. Under conservative thresholds, intentionally designed to minimize false alerts, the screening system achieved high specificity (1.00) and prevalence-adjusted positive predictive value (1.00), with good sensitivity (0.72), an expected trade-off prioritizing precision, that was highest in 90-day window. Patient-reported language alone supported high-precision, low-burden early stroke risk detection, that could offer a valuable time window for clinical evaluation and intervention for high-risk individuals.

</details>


### [223] [Improving Spatial Allocation for Energy System Coupling with Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22249)
*Xuanhao Mu,Jakob Geiges,Nan Liu,Thorsten Schlachter,Veit Hagenmeyer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-supervised heterogeneous graph neural network assigns physically meaningful weights to high-resolution geographic units, improving Voronoi-based allocation in energy-system analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coupling models across spatial scales is hard when resolutions mismatch. Relying on a single geographic attribute leads to biased or simplistic weightings; there is a need for scalable, physically plausible weighting that leverages multiple attributes and data-driven signals.

Method: Represent high-resolution geographic units as nodes in a heterogeneous graph, incorporating multiple geographic features as node/edge attributes. Use self-supervised learning to generate weights for each grid point, then integrate these weights into a Voronoi-based allocation method to go beyond proximity-only coupling.

Result: Weights produced by the method significantly improve the scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility of cluster-based Voronoi diagrams, yielding higher precision than traditional single-attribute approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed self-supervised heterogeneous GNN provides a scalable, data-driven weighting scheme that enhances cross-resolution energy-system modeling by enriching Voronoi-based allocations with multiple geographic factors.

Abstract: In energy system analysis, coupling models with mismatched spatial resolutions is a significant challenge. A common solution is assigning weights to high-resolution geographic units for aggregation, but traditional models are limited by using only a single geospatial attribute. This paper presents an innovative method employing a self-supervised Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network to address this issue. This method models high-resolution geographic units as graph nodes, integrating various geographical features to generate physically meaningful weights for each grid point. These weights enhance the conventional Voronoi-based allocation method, allowing it to go beyond simply geographic proximity by incorporating essential geographic information.In addition, the self-supervised learning paradigm overcomes the lack of accurate ground-truth data. Experimental results demonstrate that applying weights generated by this method to cluster-based Voronoi Diagrams significantly enhances scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility, while increasing precision compared to traditional methods.

</details>


### [224] [Zatom-1: A Multimodal Flow Foundation Model for 3D Molecules and Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22251)
*Alex Morehead,Miruna Cretu,Antonia Panescu,Rishabh Anand,Maurice Weiler,Tynan Perez,Samuel Blau,Steven Farrell,Wahid Bhimji,Anubhav Jain,Hrushikesh Sahasrabuddhe,Pietro Lio,Tommi Jaakkola,Rafael Gomez-Bombarelli,Rex Ying,N. Benjamin Erichson,Michael W. Mahoney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Zatom-1 is a multimodal Transformer foundation model unifying generative and predictive learning for 3D molecules and materials via a multimodal flow-matching objective, enabling scalable pretraining, fast sampling, and cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for a single model that handles both molecular and material domains and supports both generation and prediction to enable representation sharing and transfer across domains and tasks.

Method: Train a Transformer with a multimodal flow-matching objective to jointly model discrete atom types and continuous 3D geometries; enable scalable pretraining; use joint generative pretraining as universal initialization for downstream multi-task prediction of properties, energies, and forces; achieve fast, stable sampling and cross-domain transfer.

Result: Matches or outperforms specialized baselines on both generative and predictive benchmarks; reduces generative inference time by over an order of magnitude; joint pretraining enables predictive transfer from materials to molecular property prediction.

Conclusion: Zatom-1 demonstrates effective cross-domain transfer and unified pretraining for 3D chemical modeling, enabling scalable, efficient generative and predictive capabilities with potential for broader applicability across domains and tasks.

Abstract: General-purpose 3D chemical modeling encompasses molecules and materials, requiring both generative and predictive capabilities. However, most existing AI approaches are optimized for a single domain (molecules or materials) and a single task (generation or prediction), which limits representation sharing and transfer. We introduce Zatom-1, the first foundation model that unifies generative and predictive learning of 3D molecules and materials. Zatom-1 is a Transformer trained with a multimodal flow matching objective that jointly models discrete atom types and continuous 3D geometries. This approach supports scalable pretraining with predictable gains as model capacity increases, while enabling fast and stable sampling. We use joint generative pretraining as a universal initialization for downstream multi-task prediction of properties, energies, and forces. Empirically, Zatom-1 matches or outperforms specialized baselines on both generative and predictive benchmarks, while reducing the generative inference time by more than an order of magnitude. Our experiments demonstrate positive predictive transfer between chemical domains from joint generative pretraining: modeling materials during pretraining improves molecular property prediction accuracy.

</details>


### [225] [Causal Direction from Convergence Time: Faster Training in the True Causal Direction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22254)
*Abdulrahman Tamim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CCA: A new optimization-dynamics criterion for causal direction; train two networks Y|X and X|Y and compare convergence speeds under additive noise. The forward (causal) direction converges faster due to a lower irreducible loss floor and non-separable gradient noise in the reverse direction. Demonstrated strong synthetic performance across architectures (26/30 overall; 30/30 on sine/exponential) and integrated into Causal Compression Learning (CCL) with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a causality criterion based on optimization dynamics rather than statistical independence or distributional asymmetries, addressing limitations of RESIT, IGCI, and SkewScore by leveraging convergence rates and gradient dynamics.

Method: Train two models predicting Y from X and X from Y; compare expected gradient steps to reach a fixed loss; under Y=f(X)+ε with ε ⟂ X and f nonlinear/injective, reverse residuals stay dependent on input, causing a higher irreducible loss floor and gradient-noise in optimization; require proper z-scoring; evaluate on synthetic benchmarks across six architectures.

Result: Empirical results show 26/30 correct causal identifications across six architectures; 30/30 on sine and exponential data-generating processes; CCA performs well on synthetic data.

Conclusion: CCA provides an optimization-time criterion for causal direction, distinct from distribution-based methods, and is embedded in the broader Causal Compression Learning (CCL) framework with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

Abstract: We introduce Causal Computational Asymmetry (CCA), a principle for causal direction identification based on optimization dynamics in which one neural network is trained to predict $Y$ from $X$ and another to predict $X$ from $Y$, and the direction that converges faster is inferred to be causal. Under the additive noise model $Y = f(X) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \perp X$ and $f$ nonlinear and injective, we establish a formal asymmetry: in the reverse direction, residuals remain statistically dependent on the input regardless of approximation quality, inducing a strictly higher irreducible loss floor and non-separable gradient noise in the optimization dynamics, so that the reverse model requires strictly more gradient steps in expectation to reach any fixed loss threshold; consequently, the forward (causal) direction converges in fewer expected optimization steps. CCA operates in optimization-time space, distinguishing it from methods such as RESIT, IGCI, and SkewScore that rely on statistical independence or distributional asymmetries, and proper z-scoring of both variables is required for valid comparison of convergence rates. On synthetic benchmarks, CCA achieves 26/30 correct causal identifications across six neural architectures, including 30/30 on sine and exponential data-generating processes. We further embed CCA into a broader framework termed Causal Compression Learning (CCL), which integrates graph structure learning, causal information compression, and policy optimization, with all theoretical guarantees formally proved and empirically validated on synthetic datasets.

</details>


### [226] [Deep Sequence Modeling with Quantum Dynamics: Language as a Wave Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22255)
*Ahmed Nebli,Hadi Saadatdoorabi,Kevin Yam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A complex-valued, unitary sequence model using a learned time-dependent Hamiltonian is proposed; it leverages quantum interference to resolve conflicting interpretations, preserves norm via a Cayley discretization, and uses the Born rule for readout. A separation theorem shows a quadratic representational gap in favor of the complex model over real-valued baselines; a continuity equation for latent probability flow provides a diagnostic tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome gating-based recurrent models' limitations by exploiting quantum-inspired interference and unitary evolution, preserving probability mass and enabling richer phase-based discrimination.

Method: Model latent state as a complex wave function evolving under a learned, time-dependent Hamiltonian on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space. Enforce unitarity via Cayley (Crank–Nicolson) discretization. Extract token probabilities with the Born rule (quadratic measurement). Compare to real-valued models via a separation theorem that shows tasks solvable exactly by the complex model but not by affine-softmax readouts unless state dimensionality grows quadratically. Derive a continuity equation for latent probability with conserved pairwise currents.

Result: Demonstrates a quadratic gap: a family of disambiguation tasks solvable exactly by a complex unitary model of dimension N, while any real-valued orthogonal model with standard affine-softmax readout requires Ω(N^2) state dimension. The Born-rule readout enables access to pairwise phase correlations via lifting to rank-one Hermitian matrices. Accompanies a continuity equation yielding conserved pairwise currents as a diagnostic of information flow.

Conclusion: The work formalizes an explicit representational advantage for complex/unitary, quantum-inspired sequence models and provides practical diagnostics for tracing information flow, suggesting design principles where phase coherence and interference are exploited to improve sequence modeling beyond real-valued networks.

Abstract: We introduce a sequence modeling framework in which the latent state is a complex-valued wave function evolving on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space under a learned, time-dependent Hamiltonian. Unlike standard recurrent architectures that rely on gating mechanisms to suppress competing hypotheses, our framework utilizes quantum interference: the Hamiltonian steers the phases of complex amplitudes so that conflicting interpretations cancel while compatible ones reinforce. The dynamics are strictly unitary, ensuring that the state norm is preserved exactly at every time step via a Cayley (Crank--Nicolson) discretization. Token probabilities are extracted using the Born rule, a quadratic measurement operator that couples magnitudes and relative phases. Our primary theoretical contribution is a separation theorem characterizing the representational advantage of this readout: we define a family of disambiguation tasks that a complex unitary model of dimension $N$ solves exactly, but which requires a state dimension of $Ω(N^2)$ for any real-valued orthogonal model equipped with a standard affine-softmax readout. This quadratic gap arises because the Born rule implicitly lifts the $N$-dimensional state into the space of rank-one Hermitian matrices, accessing pairwise phase correlations that are inaccessible to linear projections. Finally, we derive a continuity equation for the latent probability mass, yielding conserved pairwise currents that serve as a built-in diagnostic for tracing information flow between dimensions.

</details>


### [227] [Orthogonal Weight Modification Enhances Learning Scalability and Convergence Efficiency without Gradient Backpropagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22259)
*Guoqing Ma,Shan Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOCO is a perturbation-based, low-rank, cluster-orthogonal weight-update method for non-backprop learning on neuromorphic systems, enabling deep spiking networks with O(1) parallel updates and improved convergence and continual learning compared to other non-BP approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backpropagation is computationally expensive and ill-suited for real-time neuromorphic hardware. Non-BP alternatives are desirable but struggle with efficiency and scalability.

Method: A perturbation-based weight update strategy that enforces low-rank structure and orthogonality constraints among perturbations (LOw-rank Cluster Orthogonal). This reduces variance in node-perturbation gradient estimates, enabling stable learning. The approach supports local training of deep spiking networks (over 10 layers) with O(1) parallel time complexity for updates.

Result: LOCO demonstrated the ability to locally train deeper spiking networks than previously reported (>10 layers), showed strong continual learning ability, improved convergence efficiency, and better task performance relative to other brain-inspired non-BP methods.

Conclusion: Low-rank, orthogonality-constrained perturbations offer a scalable, efficient non-BP learning route for neuromorphic systems, potentially enabling high-performance, real-time, lifelong learning with shallow parallelism.

Abstract: Recognizing the substantial computational cost of backpropagation (BP), non-BP methods have emerged as attractive alternatives for efficient learning on emerging neuromorphic systems. However, existing non-BP approaches still face critical challenges in efficiency and scalability. Inspired by neural representations and dynamic mechanisms in the brain, we propose a perturbation-based approach called LOw-rank Cluster Orthogonal (LOCO) weight modification. We find that low-rank is an inherent property of perturbation-based algorithms. Under this condition, the orthogonality constraint limits the variance of the node perturbation (NP) gradient estimates and enhances the convergence efficiency. Through extensive evaluations on multiple datasets, LOCO demonstrates the capability to locally train the deepest spiking neural networks to date (more than 10 layers), while exhibiting strong continual learning ability, improved convergence efficiency, and better task performance compared to other brain-inspired non-BP algorithms. Notably, LOCO requires only O(1) parallel time complexity for weight updates, which is significantly lower than that of BP methods. This offers a promising direction for achieving high-performance, real-time, and lifelong learning on neuromorphic systems.

</details>


### [228] [Code World Models for Parameter Control in Evolutionary Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22260)
*Camilo Chacón Sartori,Guillem Rodríguez Corominas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-synthesized simulators of optimizer dynamics (Code World Models) enable greedy planning to control mutation strength in stochastic combinatorial optimization, achieving near-optimal or state-of-the-art performance across LO/ONEMAX, JUMP_k, and NK-landscapes. The approach outperforms baselines like DQN, requires fewer offline trajectories, and generalizes across instances with robust results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether an LLM can learn the behavior of an optimizer from suboptimal trajectories and leverage that knowledge to actively control the optimizer in stochastic combinatorial settings.

Method: Extend Code World Models to synthesize Python simulators of the optimizer's dynamics from suboptimal trajectories of 1+1-RLS_k. Use greedy planning on the simulator to select mutation strength k at each step. Evaluate on LO, ONEMAX, JUMP_k, and NK-landscapes; compare to baselines (e.g., DQN) and examine sample efficiency, success rate, and generalization; include empirical transition statistics in prompts.

Result: CWM-greedy achieves within 6% of the optimal policy on LO/ONEMAX without optimal trajectories; attains 100% success on JUMP_k where baselines fail; on NK-landscapes (15 instances) it outperforms baselines (36.94 vs 36.32, p<0.001) when prompts include empirical transition statistics; outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline vs 500 online), success rate (100% vs 58%), and generalization (k=3: 78% vs 0%); robustness confirmed across 5 runs.

Conclusion: LLMs can learn dynamic models of optimizers and use them to plan actions that control optimization in stochastic settings, achieving strong, robust performance and suggesting potential for LLM-based surrogate planners in combinatorial optimization; empirical statistics in prompts enhance transferability.

Abstract: Can an LLM learn how an optimizer behaves -- and use that knowledge to control it? We extend Code World Models (CWMs), LLM-synthesized Python programs that predict environment dynamics, from deterministic games to stochastic combinatorial optimization. Given suboptimal trajectories of $(1{+}1)$-$\text{RLS}_k$, the LLM synthesizes a simulator of the optimizer's dynamics; greedy planning over this simulator then selects the mutation strength $k$ at each step. On \lo{} and \onemax{}, CWM-greedy performs within 6\% of the theoretically optimal policy -- without ever seeing optimal-policy trajectories. On \jump{$_k$}, where a deceptive valley causes all adaptive baselines to fail (0\% success rate), CWM-greedy achieves 100\% success rate -- without any collection policy using oracle knowledge of the gap parameter. On the NK-Landscape, where no closed-form model exists, CWM-greedy outperforms all baselines across fifteen independently generated instances ($36.94$ vs.\ $36.32$; $p<0.001$) when the prompt includes empirical transition statistics. The CWM also outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline trajectories vs.\ 500 online episodes), success rate (100\% vs.\ 58\%), and generalization ($k{=}3$: 78\% vs.\ 0\%). Robustness experiments confirm stable synthesis across 5 independent runs.

</details>


### [229] [Sustainable LLM Inference using Context-Aware Model Switching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22261)
*Yuvarani,Akashdeep Singh,Zahra Fathanah,Salsabila Harlen,Syeikha Syafura Al-Zahra binti Zahari,Hema Subramaniam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Context-aware model switching for LLM inference reduces energy use by routing queries to appropriately sized models using caching, rule-based complexity scoring, ML intent classification, and user adaptation; yields up to 67.5% energy savings with 93.6% output quality and ~68% faster response for simple queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of the one-size-fits-all inference paradigm, which wastes energy by defaulting to the largest model for every query. A scalable, sustainable solution is needed for real-world workloads where task complexity varies.

Method: Propose Context-Aware Model Switching for Energy-Efficient LLM Inference architecture combining caching for repeated queries, rule-based complexity scoring for fast and explainable decisions, machine learning classification to capture semantic intent, and a user-adaptive component that learns from interaction patterns over time. Evaluation used real conversation workloads with three open-source models (Gemma3 1B, Gemma3 4B, Qwen3 4B). Metrics included energy consumption (NVML GPU power telemetry), response latency, routing accuracy, and output quality (BERTScore F1).

Result: The approach reduced energy consumption by up to 67.5% compared to always using the largest model while maintaining a high output quality of 93.6%. Simple queries also saw a ~68% reduction in response time.

Conclusion: Context-aware model switching offers a practical, scalable path to energy-efficient AI systems, delivering significant efficiency gains with minimal sacrifices in response quality.

Abstract: Large language models have become central to many AI applications, but their growing energy consumption raises serious sustainability concerns. A key limitation in current AI deployments is the reliance on a one-size-fits-all inference strategy where most systems route every request to the same large model, regardless of task complexity, leading to substantial and unnecessary energy waste. To address this issue, we propose a context-aware model switching approach that dynamically selects an appropriate language model based on query complexity. The proposed system uses a Context-Aware Model Switching for Energy-Efficient LLM Inference that combines caching for repeated queries, rulebased complexity scoring for fast and explainable decisions, machine learning classification to capture semantic intent, and a user-adaptive component that learns from interaction patterns over time. The proposed architecture was evaluated using real conversation workloads and three open-source language models (Gemma3 1B, Gemma3 4B and Qwen3 4B) with different computational costs, measuring energy consumption (via NVML GPU power telemetry), response latency, routing accuracy, and output quality (BERTScore F1) to reflect real-world usage conditions. Experimental results show that the model switching approach can reduce energy consumption by up to 67.5% compared to always using the largest model while maintaining a response quality of 93.6%. In addition, the response time for simple queries also improved significantly by approximately 68%. These results show that model switching inference offers a practical and scalable path toward more energy-efficient and sustainable AI systems, demonstrating that significant efficiency gains can be achieved without major sacrifices in response quality.

</details>


### [230] [Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22265)
*Chika Maduabuchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM) imposes a global entropy-rate budget on trajectory measures, yielding a convex Wasserstein-space optimization that links flow matching to Schrodinger bridges and entropic OT, improving mode coverage and avoiding low-entropy bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard flow-matching objectives can create low-entropy bottlenecks in trajectories, degrading semantic mode coverage and information geometry; a principled entropy constraint is needed to preserve diversity.

Method: Constrained variational principle on continuity-equation paths enforcing d/dt H(mu_t) >= -lambda; leads to convex optimization in Wasserstein space with KKT/Pontryagin conditions; stochastic-control representation equivalent to a Schrodinger bridge with an explicit entropy multiplier.

Result: In pure transport regime, recovers entropic OT geodesics; Gamma-converges to classical OT as lambda -> 0; provides certificate-style mode-coverage and density-floor guarantees with Lipschitz stability; constructs near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching.

Conclusion: ECFM provides a principled, tractable framework to control information geometry in time-indexed generative flows, unifying entropic OT and Schrodinger bridge formulations while offering theoretical guarantees on mode coverage and stability.

Abstract: Modern vision generators transport a base distribution to data through time-indexed measures, implemented as deterministic flows (ODEs) or stochastic diffusions (SDEs). Despite strong empirical performance, standard flow-matching objectives do not directly control the information geometry of the trajectory, allowing low-entropy bottlenecks that can transiently deplete semantic modes. We propose Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM): a constrained variational principle over continuity-equation paths enforcing a global entropy-rate budget d/dt H(mu_t) >= -lambda. ECFM is a convex optimization in Wasserstein space with a KKT/Pontryagin system, and admits a stochastic-control representation equivalent to a Schrodinger bridge with an explicit entropy multiplier. In the pure transport regime, ECFM recovers entropic OT geodesics and Gamma-converges to classical OT as lambda -> 0. We further obtain certificate-style mode-coverage and density-floor guarantees with Lipschitz stability, and construct near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching.

</details>


### [231] [Data-Driven Supervision of a Thermal-Hydraulic Process Towards a Physics-Based Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22267)
*Osimone Imhogiemhe,Yoann Jus,Hubert Lejeune,Saïd Moussaoui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A digital twin framework for real-time fault detection/diagnosis in a thermal-hydraulic process, integrating numerical simulation with machine learning for online parameter-change detection and estimation, validated on a scenario with a single parameter change.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance real-time process supervision to improve safety, continuous operation and efficiency by combining physics-based simulation with data-driven methods within a digital twin.

Method: Develop a digital twin using numerical simulation of the system plus machine learning components. Implement modules for detecting process parameter changes and online estimation of their values, forming a fault detection and diagnosis algorithm validated on a test scenario with a single parameter change.

Result: The approach achieves good accuracy in localizing parameter variations and updating their values in the specified test scenario.

Conclusion: Digital-twin-based fault detection/diagnosis for thermal-hydraulic processes is feasible and effective in the tested scenario; future work should address multiple simultaneous faults and broader validation.

Abstract: The real-time supervision of production processes is a common challenge across several industries. It targets process component monitoring and its predictive maintenance in order to ensure safety, uninterrupted production and maintain high efficiency level. The rise of advanced tools for the simulation of physical systems in addition to data-driven machine learning models offers the possibility to design numerical tools dedicated to efficient system monitoring. In that respect, the digital twin concept presents an adequate framework that proffers solution to these challenges. The main purpose of this paper is to develop such a digital twin dedicated to fault detection and diagnosis in the context of a thermal-hydraulic process supervision. Based on a numerical simulation of the system, in addition to machine learning methods, we propose different modules dedicated to process parameter change detection and their on-line estimation. The proposed fault detection and diagnosis algorithm is validated on a specific test scenario, with single one-off parameter change occurrences in the system. The numerical results show good accuracy in terms of parameter variation localization and the update of their values.

</details>


### [232] [AutoQRA: Joint Optimization of Mixed-Precision Quantization and Low-rank Adapters for Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22268)
*Changhai Zhou,Shiyang Zhang,Yuhua Zhou,Qian Qiao,Jun Gao,Cheng Jin,Kaizhou Qin,Weizhong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoQRA jointly optimizes per-layer quantization bit-width and LoRA rank during mixed-quantized fine-tuning, achieving near FP performance under memory budgets, with a two-stage search: (1) global multi-fidelity evolutionary search with warm-start priors for candidate screening, (2) trust-region Bayesian optimization for local refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) misses critical interactions between bit-width and LoRA rank. A fixed memory budget yields varying outcomes across configurations, so joint optimization is needed to exploit these interactions and to mitigate quantization noise during training.

Method: Two-stage search: (1) global multi-fidelity evolutionary search initialized with layer-wise importance priors, using specialized operators and a performance model to efficiently screen configurations; (2) trust-region Bayesian optimization to refine promising regions under the memory constraint, enabling per-layer adaptation to compensate quantization noise.

Result: AutoQRA delivers performance close to full-precision fine-tuning with a memory footprint comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.

Conclusion: Joint per-layer optimization of quantization bit-width and LoRA rank under a memory budget is effective, enabling strong adaptation under tight resources by exploiting interactions and mitigating quantization noise during training.

Abstract: Quantization followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning has emerged as a promising paradigm for downstream adaptation under tight GPU memory constraints. However, this sequential pipeline fails to leverage the intricate interaction between quantization bit-width and LoRA rank. Specifically, a carefully optimized quantization allocation with low quantization error does not always translate to strong fine-tuning performance, and different bit-width and rank configurations can lead to significantly varying outcomes under the same memory budget. To address this limitation, we propose AutoQRA, a joint optimization framework that simultaneously optimizes the bit-width and LoRA rank configuration for each layer during the mixed quantized fine-tuning process. To tackle the challenges posed by the large discrete search space and the high evaluation cost associated with frequent fine-tuning iterations, AutoQRA decomposes the optimization process into two stages. First, it first conducts a global multi-fidelity evolutionary search, where the initial population is warm-started by injecting layer-wise importance priors. This stage employs specific operators and a performance model to efficiently screen candidate configurations. Second, trust-region Bayesian optimization is applied to locally refine promising regions of the search space and identify optimal configurations under the given memory budget. This approach enables active compensation for quantization noise in specific layers during training. Experiments show that AutoQRA achieves performance close to full-precision fine-tuning with a memory footprint comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.

</details>


### [233] [CQSA: Byzantine-robust Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22269)
*Arnab Nath,Harsh Kasyap*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CQSA modularizes quantum secure aggregation by clustering clients into small GHZ-based groups to improve entanglement fidelity and enable Byzantine-robustness; it analyzes cluster-level aggregates to detect malicious contributions, achieving stable convergence and higher fidelity under depolarizing noise than global QSA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome scalability and security limitations of single-global-GHZ QSA in federated learning: deteriorating fidelity with large entangled states and lack of Byzantine detection; enable practical, near-term quantum hardware implementations.

Method: Partition clients into random clusters; perform local quantum aggregation within each cluster using high-fidelity, low-qubit GHZ states; server analyzes relationships between cluster aggregates using statistical measures (cosine similarity, Euclidean distance) to identify anomalies; theoretical analysis and simulations under depolarizing noise.

Result: CQSA provides stable model convergence and higher state fidelity than global QSA in simulations; better resilience to depolarizing noise due to localized entanglement and modular aggregation.

Conclusion: A clustered, modular approach aligns quantum hardware constraints with security needs, enabling Byzantine-robust, scalable quantum-assisted FL and practical deployment path for QSA.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data. However, shared local model updates remain vulnerable to inference and poisoning attacks. Secure aggregation schemes have been proposed to mitigate these attacks. In this work, we aim to understand how these techniques are implemented in quantum-assisted FL. Quantum Secure Aggregation (QSA) has been proposed, offering information-theoretic privacy by encoding client updates into the global phase of multipartite entangled states. Existing QSA protocols, however, rely on a single global Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state shared among all participating clients. This design poses fundamental challenges: fidelity of large-scale GHZ states deteriorates rapidly with the increasing number of clients; and (ii) the global aggregation prevents the detection of Byzantine clients. We propose Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation (CQSA), a modular aggregation framework that reconciles the physical constraints of near-term quantum hardware along with the need for Byzantine-robustness in FL. CQSA randomly partitions the clients into small clusters, each performing local quantum aggregation using high-fidelity, low-qubit GHZ states. The server analyzes statistical relationships between cluster-level aggregates employing common statistical measures such as cosine similarity and Euclidean distance to identify malicious contributions. Through theoretical analysis and simulations under depolarizing noise, we demonstrate that CQSA ensures stable model convergence, achieves superior state fidelity over global QSA.

</details>


### [234] [Prior Knowledge-enhanced Spatio-temporal Epidemic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22270)
*Sijie Ruan,Jinyu Li,Jia Wei,Zenghao Xu,Jie Bao,Junshi Xu,Junyang Qiu,Hanning Yuan,Xiaoxiao Wang,Shuliang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STOEP is a hybrid spatio-temporal epidemic predictor that fuses implicit spatio-temporal priors with explicit expert priors via three components (CAL, SPE, FMF). It improves RMSE by 11.1% over strong baselines on COVID-19 and influenza data and has been deployed in a provincial CDC in China.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome weak-signal sensitivity, simplistic spatial modeling, and unstable parameter estimation in epidemic forecasting by incorporating both learned priors from data and structured expert priors.

Method: Three-part framework: (1) Case-aware Adjacency Learning (CAL) dynamically adjusts regional dependencies using historical infection patterns; (2) Space-informed Parameter Estimating (SPE) uses learnable spatial priors to amplify weak signals; (3) Filter-based Mechanistic Forecasting (FMF) applies expert-guided adaptive thresholding to regularize parameters.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world COVID-19 and influenza datasets shows STOEP outperforms the best baseline by 11.1% in RMSE. The system is deployed at a provincial CDC in China.

Conclusion: Integrating implicit spatio-temporal priors with explicit expert priors yields robust improvements in predictive accuracy and demonstrates practical deployment potential for public health forecasting.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal epidemic forecasting is critical for public health management, yet existing methods often struggle with insensitivity to weak epidemic signals, over-simplified spatial relations, and unstable parameter estimation. To address these challenges, we propose the Spatio-Temporal priOr-aware Epidemic Predictor (STOEP), a novel hybrid framework that integrates implicit spatio-temporal priors and explicit expert priors. STOEP consists of three key components: (1) Case-aware Adjacency Learning (CAL), which dynamically adjusts mobility-based regional dependencies using historical infection patterns; (2) Space-informed Parameter Estimating (SPE), which employs learnable spatial priors to amplify weak epidemic signals; and (3) Filter-based Mechanistic Forecasting (FMF), which uses an expert-guided adaptive thresholding strategy to regularize epidemic parameters. Extensive experiments on real-world COVID-19 and influenza datasets demonstrate that STOEP outperforms the best baseline by 11.1% in RMSE. The system has been deployed at one provincial CDC in China to facilitate downstream applications.

</details>


### [235] [Support Tokens, Stability Margins, and a New Foundation for Robust LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22271)
*Deepak Agarwal,Dhyey Dharmendrakumar Mavani,Suyash Gupta,Karthik Sethuraman,Tejas Dharamsi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic reinterpretation of causal self-attention reveals structural barriers on attention parameters, creating a geometry of token space with a margin-like boundary and support tokens; LLMs can be modeled as stochastic processes over token subsets, leading to a Bayesian MAP objective with a smooth log-barrier penalty that improves robustness with minimal training changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify self-attention under a probabilistic framework, uncover inherent geometrical constraints in attention parameters, and provide a rigorous probabilistic treatment of sequence modeling in LLMs.

Method: Reformulate causal self-attention within a probabilistic framework akin to probabilistic PCA, derive a change-of-variables barrier constraint on attention parameters, analyze the induced geometry and its relation to decoding dynamics; model LLMs as stochastic processes over power sets of the token space; propose a Bayesian framework and MAP objective by adding a smooth log-barrier penalty to the cross-entropy loss.

Result: Identification of a barrier constraint leading to structured geometries in token space and a margin interpretation for attention, introduction of the concept of support tokens, and a probabilistic MAP training objective that yields more robust models with only minor training modifications.

Conclusion: The probabilistic framing provides theoretical insights into LLM decoding dynamics and robustness, linking attention parameters to ill-conditioning boundaries and connecting sequence modeling to Bayesian MAP estimation with practical training implications.

Abstract: Self-attention is usually described as a flexible, content-adaptive way to mix a token with information from its past. We re-interpret causal self-attention transformers, the backbone of modern foundation models, within a probabilistic framework, much like how classical PCA is extended to probabilistic PCA. However, this re-formulation reveals a surprising and deeper structural insight: due to a change-of-variables phenomenon, a barrier constraint emerges on the self-attention parameters. This induces a highly structured geometry on the token space, providing theoretical insights into the dynamics of LLM decoding. This reveals a boundary where attention becomes ill-conditioned, leading to a margin interpretation similar to classical support vector machines. Just like support vectors, this naturally gives rise to the concept of support tokens.
  Furthermore, we show that LLMs can be interpreted as a stochastic process over the power set of the token space, providing a rigorous probabilistic framework for sequence modeling. We propose a Bayesian framework and derive a MAP estimation objective that requires only a minimal modification to standard LLM training: the addition of a smooth log-barrier penalty to the usual cross-entropy loss. We demonstrate that this provides more robust models without sacrificing out-of-sample accuracy and that it is straightforward to incorporate in practice.

</details>


### [236] [Positional-aware Spatio-Temporal Network for Large-Scale Traffic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22274)
*Runfei Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight PASTN model for traffic flow forecasting that uses positional-aware embeddings to distinguish nodes and a temporal attention module to capture long-range dependencies, achieving effective and efficient forecasting across county, megalopolis, and state-scale datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of node-wise distinct representations and long-range temporal modeling in large-scale traffic forecasting, and tackle deployment challenges due to growing data size by proposing a lightweight end-to-end approach.

Method: Introduce PASTN with positional-aware embeddings to separate node representations and a temporal attention module to enhance long-range temporal perception; end-to-end, lightweight architecture designed for efficiency across large graphs.

Result: Empirical experiments on datasets spanning county to state scale demonstrate improved forecasting accuracy and efficiency; ablations confirm the contributions of positional-aware embeddings and temporal attention to performance and scalability.

Conclusion: PASTN offers an effective and efficient solution for traffic flow forecasting in large-scale settings, leveraging node-specific embeddings and temporal attention to achieve strong performance with reduced computational burden.

Abstract: Traffic flow forecasting has emerged as an indispensable mission for daily life, which is required to utilize the spatiotemporal relationship between each location within a time period under a graph structure to predict future flow. However, the large travel demand for broader geographical areas and longer time spans requires models to distinguish each node clearly and possess a holistic view of the history, which has been paid less attention to in prior works. Furthermore, increasing sizes of data hinder the deployment of most models in real application environments. To this end, in this paper, we propose a lightweight Positional-aware Spatio-Temporal Network (PASTN) to effectively capture both temporal and spatial complexities in an end-to-end manner. PASTN introduces positional-aware embeddings to separate each node's representation, while also utilizing a temporal attention module to improve the long-range perception of current models. Extensive experiments verify the effectiveness and efficiency of PASTN across datasets of various scales (county, megalopolis and state). Further analysis demonstrates the efficacy of newly introduced modules either.

</details>


### [237] [X-REFINE: XAI-based RElevance input-Filtering and archItecture fiNe-tuning for channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22277)
*Abdul Karim Gizzini,Yahia Medjahdi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: X-REFINE is an XAI-driven framework that jointly optimizes input-filtering and neural architecture for AI-native 6G wireless systems, using a decomposition-based, sign-stabilized LRP epsilon rule to generate high-resolution relevance for subcarriers and hidden neurons, enabling faithful component selection and reduced complexity while preserving BER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the black-box and high-complexity nature of deep learning in critical 6G tasks (e.g., channel estimation). Perturbation-based XAI alone ignores internal architectural optimization, necessitating a framework that couples interpretability with structural refinement.

Method: Apply a decomposition-based, sign-stabilized LRP epsilon rule to backpropagate predictions, producing fine-grained relevance scores for subcarriers and neurons. Use these scores to perform joint input-filtering and architecture fine-tuning to select the most faithful model components.

Result: Simulation demonstrates an improved interpretability–performance–complexity trade-off, with substantially reduced computational burden while maintaining robust BER across varied scenarios.

Conclusion: X-REFINE offers a practical pathway to interpretable and efficient AI-native wireless architectures, integrating explanation-driven input filtering with architecture optimization to enhance deployment viability in 6G contexts.

Abstract: AI-native architectures are vital for 6G wireless communications. The black-box nature and high complexity of deep learning models employed in critical applications, such as channel estimation, limit their practical deployment. While perturbation-based XAI solutions offer input filtering, they often neglect internal structural optimization. We propose X-REFINE, an XAI-based framework for joint input-filtering and architecture fine-tuning. By utilizing a decomposition-based, sign-stabilized LRP epsilon rule, X-REFINE backpropagates predictions to derive high-resolution relevance scores for both subcarriers and hidden neurons. This enables a holistic optimization that identifies the most faithful model components. Simulation results demonstrate that X-REFINE achieves a superior interpretability-performance-complexity trade-off, significantly reducing computational complexity while maintaining robust bit error rate (BER) performance across different scenarios.

</details>


### [238] [Integrating Machine Learning Ensembles and Large Language Models for Heart Disease Prediction Using Voting Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22280)
*Md. Tahsin Amin,Tanim Ahmmod,Zannatul Ferdus,Talukder Naemul Hasan Naem,Ehsanul Ferdous,Arpita Bhattacharjee,Ishmam Ahmed Solaiman,Nahiyan Bin Noor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML ensembles outperform LLMs on a structured cardiovascular disease dataset; a hybrid ML-LLM approach offers a small accuracy/AUC gain over pure ML, suggesting potential for robust clinical decision-support tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cardiovascular disease is the leading global cause of death, necessitating accurate risk stratification and reliable decision-support systems. The emergence of LLMs offers zero-shot and few-shot reasoning that could complement traditional ML on tabular healthcare data.

Method: A merged dataset of 1,190 patient records was used to compare traditional ML ensembles (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) against open-source LLMs accessed via OpenRouter APIs. A hybrid fusion of ML ensemble and LLM reasoning under Gemini 2.5 Flash was also evaluated.

Result: Traditional ML ensembles achieved 95.78% accuracy and ROC-AUC 0.96. LLMs achieved 78.9% accuracy in zero-shot and 72.6% in few-shot settings. The hybrid ML-LLM approach reached 96.62% accuracy and ROC-AUC 0.97, yielding the best overall performance.

Conclusion: Ensemble ML remains the strongest approach for structured tabular prediction in this domain, but integrating LLM reasoning can provide marginal gains in uncertainty scenarios. The hybrid ML-LLM framework shows promise for more reliable clinical decision-support tools.

Abstract: Cardiovascular disease is the primary cause of death globally, necessitating early identification, precise risk classification, and dependable decision-support technologies. The advent of large language models (LLMs) provides new zero-shot and few-shot reasoning capabilities, even though machine learning (ML) algorithms, especially ensemble approaches like Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, are excellent at modeling complex, non-linear patient data and routinely beat logistic regression. This research predicts cardiovascular disease using a merged dataset of 1,190 patient records, comparing traditional machine learning models (95.78% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.96) with open-source large language models via OpenRouter APIs. Finally, a hybrid fusion of the ML ensemble and LLM reasoning under Gemini 2.5 Flash achieved the best results (96.62% accuracy, 0.97 AUC), showing that LLMs (78.9 % accuracy) work best when combined with ML models rather than used alone. Results show that ML ensembles achieved the highest performance (95.78% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.96), while LLMs performed moderately in zero-shot (78.9%) and slightly better in few-shot (72.6%) settings. The proposed hybrid method enhanced the strength in uncertain situations, illustrating that ensemble ML is considered the best structured tabular prediction case, but it can be integrated with hybrid ML-LLM systems to provide a minor increase and open the way to more reliable clinical decision-support tools.

</details>


### [239] [BrepCoder: A Unified Multimodal Large Language Model for Multi-task B-rep Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22284)
*Mingi Kim,Yongjun Kim,Jungwoo Kang,Hyungki Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BrepCoder proposes a unified multimodal LLM for CAD using B-rep as code, enabling diverse tasks via a two-stage pretraining approach that translates B-rep modeling sequences into Python-like code for generalization across completion, error correction, and CAD-QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CAD DL approaches focus on point clouds/images and task-specific models, while the industry-standard Boundary Representation (B-rep) is underutilized. A unified model that handles B-rep and multiple tasks could generalize better and reduce task-specific engineering.

Method: Represent B-rep as structural code by translating CAD modeling sequences into Python-like code using LLMs. Train in two stages: (1) pre-train on reverse engineering to learn geometric features and design logic; (2) adapt to downstream tasks by aligning code with B-rep and extending to completion, error correction, and CAD-QA.

Result: BrepCoder demonstrates superior generalization across diverse CAD tasks and positions itself as a general-purpose CAD agent by effectively leveraging B-rep-as-code and two-stage training.

Conclusion: Interpreting B-rep as structural code enables a unified, generalizable CAD model; BrepCoder shows potential to unify multiple CAD tasks under a single MLLM-based agent, inviting further expansion to additional tasks and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Recent advancements in deep learning have actively addressed complex challenges within the Computer-Aided Design (CAD) domain.However, most existing approaches rely on task-specifi c models requiring structural modifi cations for new tasks, and they predominantly focus on point clouds or images rather than the industry-standard Boundary Representation (B-rep) format. To address these limitations, we propose BrepCoder, a unifi ed Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) that performs diverse CAD tasks from B-rep inputs. By leveraging the code generation capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), we convert CAD modeling sequences into Python-like code and align them with B-rep.
