We then adopt a two-stage training strategy: First, pre-training on reverse engineering to learn geometric features and design logic. Second, eff ectively extending the model to various downstream tasks such as completion, error correction, and CAD-QA. Consequently, by interpreting B-rep as structural code, BrepCoder achieves superior generalization across diverse tasks, demonstrating its potential as a general-purpose CAD agent.

</details>


### [240] [Early Risk Stratification of Dosing Errors in Clinical Trials Using Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22285)
*Félicien Hêche,Sohrab Ferdowsi,Anthony Yazdani,Sara Sansaloni-Pastor,Douglas Teodoro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal ML framework predicting high dosing error risk in clinical trials using pre-initiation data; late-fusion of structured features (XGBoost) and trialtext (ClinicalModernBERT) yields AUC-ROC 0.862 with well-calibrated risk categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable proactive quality management by anticipating trial-level dosing error risk before trial start, reducing patient risk and resource waste.

Method: Assembled a ClinicalTrials.gov dataset (42,112 trials) with structured/semi-structured/unstructured protocol data. Labeled trials for elevated dosing error rate using adverse event reports, MedDRA terms, and Wilson confidence intervals. Compared: (1) XGBoost on structured features, (2) ClinicalModernBERT on text, (3) late-fusion of both modalities. Applied post-hoc probability calibration for interpretable trial-level risk stratification.

Result: Late-fusion achieved highest AUC-ROC of 0.862. Calibrated outputs enabled robust, monotonic risk stratification; higher predicted risk groups contained higher proportions of trials labeled with high dosing error rate, aligning with predicted probabilities.

Conclusion: Presents a reproducible, scalable ML framework for early, trial-level risk stratification of CTs at risk of high dosing error rates, supporting proactive, risk-based quality management in clinical research.

Abstract: Objective: The objective of this study is to develop a machine learning (ML)-based framework for early risk stratification of clinical trials (CTs) according to their likelihood of exhibiting a high rate of dosing errors, using information available prior to trial initiation. Materials and Methods: We constructed a dataset from ClinicalTrials.gov comprising 42,112 CTs. Structured, semi-structured trial data, and unstructured protocol-related free-text data were extracted. CTs were assigned binary labels indicating elevated dosing error rate, derived from adverse event reports, MedDRA terminology, and Wilson confidence intervals. We evaluated an XGBoost model trained on structured features, a ClinicalModernBERT model using textual data, and a simple late-fusion model combining both modalities. Post-hoc probability calibration was applied to enable interpretable, trial-level risk stratification. Results: The late-fusion model achieved the highest AUC-ROC (0.862). Beyond discrimination, calibrated outputs enabled robust stratification of CTs into predefined risk categories. The proportion of trials labeled as having an excessively high dosing error rate increased monotonically across higher predicted risk groups and aligned with the corresponding predicted probability ranges. Discussion: These findings indicate that dosing error risk can be anticipated at the trial level using pre-initiation information. Probability calibration was essential for translating model outputs into reliable and interpretable risk categories, while simple multimodal integration yielded performance gains without requiring complex architectures. Conclusion: This study introduces a reproducible and scalable ML framework for early, trial-level risk stratification of CTs at risk of high dosing error rates, supporting proactive, risk-based quality management in clinical research.

</details>


### [241] [OmniZip: Learning a Unified and Lightweight Lossless Compressor for Multi-Modal Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22286)
*Yan Zhao,Zhengxue Cheng,Junxuan Zhang,Dajiang Zhou,Qunshan Gu,Qi Wang,Li Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniZip is a unified, lightweight lossless compressor for multi-modal data, using a modality-unified tokenizer, modality-routing context learning, and modality-routing feedforward with reparameterization to achieve competitive compression and edge-device efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for a single compressor that handles diverse data types (image, text, speech, tactile, database, gene sequence) to avoid deploying multiple modality-specific compressors; existing learning-based methods are usually single-modality or too heavy for practical multi-modal use.

Method: A lightweight backbone with three components: (1) modality-unified tokenizer that reversibly transforms data into tokens, (2) modality-routing context learning for flexible cross-modal context modeling, (3) modality-routing feedforward to enhance nonlinear representation. A reparameterization training strategy is used to increase model capacity.

Result: OmniZip outperforms or matches state-of-the-art compressors across multiple modalities. It achieves 42%, 57%, 62%, 42%, and 53% higher compression efficiency than gzip on CLIC-M, TouchandGo, enwik9, LibriSpeech, and WikiSQL, respectively. It supports near real-time inference on edge devices (~1 MB/s on MacBook CPUs and iPhone NPUs). Code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: OmniZip provides a unified, efficient, and practical solution for multi-modal lossless compression, reducing the need for multiple modality-specific compressors while enabling edge-friendly deployment.

Abstract: Lossless compression is essential for efficient data storage and transmission. Although learning-based lossless compressors achieve strong results, most of them are designed for a single modality, leading to redundant compressor deployments in multi-modal settings. Designing a unified multi-modal compressor is critical yet challenging, as different data types vary largely in format, dimension, and statistics. Multi-modal large language models offer a promising resolution but remain too complex for practical use. Thus, we propose \textbf{OmniZip}, \textbf{a unified and lightweight lossless compressor for multi-modal data (like image, text, speech, tactile, database, and gene sequence)}. Built on a lightweight backbone, OmniZip incorporates three key components to enable efficient multi-modal lossless compression: a modality-unified tokenizer that reversibly transforms diverse data into tokens, a modality-routing context learning mechanism that enables flexible multi-modal context modeling, and a modality-routing feedforward design that further enhances the model's nonlinear representation flexibility. A reparameterization training strategy is used to enhance model capacity. OmniZip outperforms or matches other state-of-the-art compressors on multiple modalities, achieving 42\%, 57\%, 62\% and 42\%, 53\% higher compression efficiency than gzip on CLIC-M, TouchandGo, enwik9, LibriSpeech, and WikiSQL datasets, respectively. It also supports near real-time inference on resource-constrained edge devices, reaching about 1MB/s on MacBook CPUs and iPhone NPUs. Our code is released at https://github.com/adminasmi/OmniZip-CVPR2026.

</details>


### [242] [Reliable XAI Explanations in Sudden Cardiac Death Prediction for Chagas Cardiomyopathy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22288)
*Vinícius P. Chagas,Luiz H. T. Viana,Mac M. da S. Carlos,João P. V. Madeiro,Roberto C. Pedrosa,Thiago Alves Rocha,Carlos H. L. Cavalcante*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Logic-based explainability with correctness guarantees for SCD prediction in Chagas cardiomyopathy (CC) achieves high accuracy and 100% explanation fidelity, outperforming heuristic explanations and improving clinical trust for deployment in endemic regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the opacity of AI models in high-stakes CC risk stratification and enable trustworthy deployment in resource-limited, endemic settings.

Method: Apply a logic-based explainability framework with correctness guarantees to an AI classifier reporting >95% accuracy and recall; compare against heuristic explanation approaches.

Result: Demonstrates strong predictive performance and 100% explanation fidelity; superior consistency/robustness vs heuristics; potential for increased clinical trust and large-scale adoption.

Conclusion: Logic-based, correctness-guaranteed explanations can enhance adoption of AI for SCD risk prediction in CC and support deployment where transparency is critical.

Abstract: Sudden cardiac death (SCD) is unpredictable, and its prediction in Chagas cardiomyopathy (CC) remains a significant challenge, especially in patients not classified as high risk. While AI and machine learning models improve risk stratification, their adoption is hindered by a lack of transparency, as they are often perceived as \textit{black boxes} with unclear decision-making processes. Some approaches apply heuristic explanations without correctness guarantees, leading to mistakes in the decision-making process. To address this, we apply a logic-based explainability method with correctness guarantees to the problem of SCD prediction in CC. This explainability method, applied to an AI classifier with over 95\% accuracy and recall, demonstrated strong predictive performance and 100\% explanation fidelity. When compared to state-of-the-art heuristic methods, it showed superior consistency and robustness. This approach enhances clinical trust, facilitates the integration of AI-driven tools into practice, and promotes large-scale deployment, particularly in endemic regions where it is most needed.

</details>


### [243] [Manifold of Failure: Behavioral Attraction Basins in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22291)
*Sarthak Munshi,Manish Bhatt,Vineeth Sai Narajala,Idan Habler,AmmarnAl-Kahfah,Ken Huang,Blake Gatto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to map the Manifold of Failure in LLMs using MAP-Elites to explore failure regions as a quality-diversity problem, yielding interpretable global safety landscapes across three models and highlighting distinct topology signatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond repairing adversarial outputs to projecting failures onto safe manifolds; instead characterize and map unsafe regions to understand AI safety comprehensively.

Method: Apply MAP-Elites (quality-diversity search) guided by Alignment Deviation to explore the continuous topology of failure regions (behavioral attraction basins) across Llama-3-8B, GPT-OSS-20B, and GPT-5-Mini; compare against existing attack methods (GCG, PAIR, TAP) and produce global maps of safety landscapes.

Result: Reported up to 63% behavioral coverage, discovery of up to 370 vulnerability niches, and model-specific topological signatures: Llama-3-8B with near-universal plateau (mean AD 0.93), GPT-OSS-20B with fragmented basins (mean 0.73), GPT-5-Mini robust ceiling (0.50). The maps are claimed to be interpretable and superior for understanding failures than prior attacks.

Conclusion: Shifts safety research from locating discrete failures to understanding their structure; provides global, interpretable maps of each model's safety landscape and prompts new avenues for analyzing alignment and robustness.

Abstract: While prior work has focused on projecting adversarial examples back onto the manifold of natural data to restore safety, we argue that a comprehensive understanding of AI safety requires characterizing the unsafe regions themselves. This paper introduces a framework for systematically mapping the Manifold of Failure in Large Language Models (LLMs). We reframe the search for vulnerabilities as a quality diversity problem, using MAP-Elites to illuminate the continuous topology of these failure regions, which we term behavioral attraction basins. Our quality metric, Alignment Deviation, guides the search towards areas where the model's behavior diverges most from its intended alignment. Across three LLMs: Llama-3-8B, GPT-OSS-20B, and GPT-5-Mini, we show that MAP-Elites achieves up to 63% behavioral coverage, discovers up to 370 distinct vulnerability niches, and reveals dramatically different model-specific topological signatures: Llama-3-8B exhibits a near-universal vulnerability plateau (mean Alignment Deviation 0.93), GPT-OSS-20B shows a fragmented landscape with spatially concentrated basins (mean 0.73), and GPT-5-Mini demonstrates strong robustness with a ceiling at 0.50. Our approach produces interpretable, global maps of each model's safety landscape that no existing attack method (GCG, PAIR, or TAP) can provide, shifting the paradigm from finding discrete failures to understanding their underlying structure.

</details>


### [244] [Global River Forecasting with a Topology-Informed AI Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22293)
*Hancheng Ren,Gang Zhao,Shuo Wang,Louise Slater,Dai Yamazaki,Shu Liu,Jingfang Fan,Shibo Cui,Ziming Yu,Shengyu Kang,Depeng Zuo,Dingzhi Peng,Zongxue Xu,Bo Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphRiverCast (GRC) is a topology-informed AI foundation model for global river hydrodynamics, enabling ColdStart predictions without historical states. It achieves ~NSE 0.82 on 7-day global pseudo-hindcasts, with topology encoding enabling accurate mass distribution and hydraulic connectivity. Pre-training with physics alignment and subsequent fine-tuning yield superior performance over physics-based and locally trained baselines, applicable from gauged reaches to entire river networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hydrology data scarcity and the need for systemic, cross-scale river hydrodynamics simulation. Traditional data-driven forecasting is often limited to isolated reaches; a topology-aware foundation model can leverage global network structure to reconstruct flows without heavy reliance on historical states.

Method: Develop a topology-informed AI foundation model (GRC) built on physics-aligned neural operator architecture. Enable ColdStart mode to predict without prior river states. Use global river topology to encode hydraulic connectivity and mass redistribution; train via physics-aligned pre-training and subsequent fine-tuning for local deployment. Evaluate on 7-day global pseudo-hindcasts; perform ablations to assess the role of topology encoding; compare against physics-based and locally-trained AI baselines.

Result: GRC-ColdStart achieves NSE ~0.82 over 7-day global pseudo-hindcasts, with minimal autoregressive error accumulation. Ablations show topology encoding is indispensable in the absence of historical states. Local pre-training and fine-tuning consistently outperform baselines. The model scales from gauged reaches to whole networks, underscoring the importance of topology and physics-informed training.

Conclusion: Topology encoding and physics-aligned pre-training are essential for robust, cross-scale hydrodynamic simulation. GRC provides a scalable, global-to-local framework that bridges global hydrodynamic knowledge with local hydrological reality, enabling rapid, cross-scale adaptive simulation.

Abstract: River systems operate as inherently interconnected continuous networks, meaning river hydrodynamic simulation ought to be a systemic process. However, widespread hydrology data scarcity often restricts data-driven forecasting to isolated predictions. To achieve systemic simulation and reduce reliance on river observations, we present GraphRiverCast (GRC), a topology-informed AI foundation model designed to simulate multivariate river hydrodynamics in global river systems. GRC is capable of operating in a "ColdStart" mode, generating predictions without relying on historical river states for initialization. In 7-day global pseudo-hindcasts, GRC-ColdStart functions as a robust standalone simulator, achieving a Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) of approximately 0.82 without exhibiting the significant error accumulation typical of autoregressive paradigms. Ablation studies reveal that topological encoding serves as indispensable structural information in the absence of historical states, explicitly guiding hydraulic connectivity and network-scale mass redistribution to reconstruct flow dynamics. Furthermore, when adapted locally via a pre-training and fine-tuning strategy, GRC consistently outperforms physics-based and locally-trained AI baselines. Crucially, this superiority extends from gauged reaches to full river networks, underscoring the necessity of topology encoding and physics-based pre-training. Built on a physics-aligned neural operator architecture, GRC enables rapid and cross-scale adaptive simulation, establishing a collaborative paradigm bridging global hydrodynamic knowledge with local hydrological reality.

</details>


### [245] [When Should a Model Change Its Mind? An Energy-Based Theory and Regularizer for Concept Drift in Electrocardiogram (ECG) Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22294)
*Timothy Oladunni,Blessing Ojeme,Kyndal Maclin,Clyde Baidoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Physiologic Energy Conservation Theory (PECT) and Energy-Constrained Representation Learning (ECRL) to enforce energy-consistent latent drift in dynamic physiological signals, demonstrated on multimodal ECG models with improved robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing concept-drift frameworks are largely distributional and do not distinguish harmless physiologic fluctuations from true concept drift in dynamic signals, leading to unstable predictions in multimodal fusion. There is a need for an energy-aware notion of representation stability aligned with physiological signal energy changes.

Method: Proposes PECT, an energy-based framework where normalized latent displacement scales with normalized signal energy change under virtual drift; persistent violations indicate real drift. Implements ECRL as a lightweight regularizer that penalizes energy-inconsistent latent movement without altering encoder architectures or increasing inference cost.

Result: Evaluated on seven unimodal and hybrid multimodal ECG models, including a strongest trimodal (1D+2D+Transformer). Clean accuracy preserved (96.0% → 94.1%), perturbed accuracy improved (72.6% → 85.5%), and fused representation drift reduced by over 45%.

Conclusion: PECT functions as an energy-drift law governing concept stability in continuous physiologic signals, with ECRL providing a practical, architecture-agnostic regularizer that enhances robustness across diverse models.

Abstract: Models operating on dynamic physiologic signals must distinguish benign, label-preserving variability from true concept change. Existing concept-drift frameworks are largely distributional and provide no principled guidance on how much a model's internal representation may move when the underlying signal undergoes physiologically plausible fluctuations in energy. As a result, deep models often misinterpret harmless changes in amplitude, rate, or morphology as concept drift, yielding unstable predictions, particularly in multimodal fusion settings.
  This study introduces Physiologic Energy Conservation Theory (PECT), an energy-based framework for concept stability in dynamic signals. PECT posits that under virtual drift, normalized latent displacement should scale proportionally with normalized signal energy change, while persistent violations of this proportionality indicate real concept drift. We operationalize this principle through Energy-Constrained Representation Learning (ECRL), a lightweight regularizer that penalizes energy-inconsistent latent movement without modifying encoder architectures or adding inference-time cost.
  Although PECT is formulated for dynamic signals in general, we instantiate and evaluate it on multimodal ECG across seven unimodal and hybrid models. Experiments show that in the strongest trimodal hybrid (1D+2D+Transformer), clean accuracy is largely preserved (96.0% to 94.1%), while perturbed accuracy improves substantially (72.6% to 85.5%) and fused representation drift decreases by over 45%. Similar trends are observed across all architectures, providing empirical evidence that PECT functions as an energy-drift law governing concept stability in continuous physiologic signals.

</details>


### [246] [Learning Rewards, Not Labels: Adversarial Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Machinery Fault Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22297)
*Dhiraj Neupane,Richard Dazeley,Mohamed Reda Bouadjenek,Sunil Aryal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline AIRL for machinery fault detection (MFD) using healthy operation data. Learns reward dynamics via adversarial IRL; discriminator-derived reward acts as an anomaly score to separate normal vs. faulty states, enabling early, robust fault detection across three run-to-failure datasets (HUMS2023, IMS, XJTU-SY).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL offers sequential decision-making advantages for MFD but current approaches treat MFD as contextual bandits and require manual reward engineering and fault labels. An offline IRL approach can learn reward dynamics directly from healthy operational sequences, bypassing label requirements and exploiting temporal structure.

Method: Offline inverse reinforcement learning via Adversarial Inverse Reinforcement Learning (AIRL). Train a discriminator to distinguish expert (normal) transitions from policy-generated ones. The discriminator yields a learned reward function, used as an anomaly score reflecting deviations from normal operation. No fault labels needed; evaluated on three run-to-failure datasets.

Result: The framework consistently yields low anomaly scores for normal samples and high scores for faulty ones, enabling early and robust fault detection across HUMS2023, IMS, and XJTU-SY datasets.

Conclusion: By aligning RL's sequential reasoning with MFD's temporal structure, the approach opens a pathway for RL-based diagnostics in data-driven industrial settings and reduces reliance on manual reward engineering and fault labeling.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) offers significant promise for machinery fault detection (MFD). However, most existing RL-based MFD approaches do not fully exploit RL's sequential decision-making strengths, often treating MFD as a simple guessing game (Contextual Bandits). To bridge this gap, we formulate MFD as an offline inverse reinforcement learning problem, where the agent learns the reward dynamics directly from healthy operational sequences, thereby bypassing the need for manual reward engineering and fault labels. Our framework employs Adversarial Inverse Reinforcement Learning to train a discriminator that distinguishes between normal (expert) and policy-generated transitions. The discriminator's learned reward serves as an anomaly score, indicating deviations from normal operating behaviour. When evaluated on three run-to-failure benchmark datasets (HUMS2023, IMS, and XJTU-SY), the model consistently assigns low anomaly scores to normal samples and high scores to faulty ones, enabling early and robust fault detection. By aligning RL's sequential reasoning with MFD's temporal structure, this work opens a path toward RL-based diagnostics in data-driven industrial settings.

</details>


### [247] [AviaSafe: A Physics-Informed Data-Driven Model for Aviation Safety-Critical Cloud Forecasts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22298)
*Zijian Zhu,Qiusheng Huang,Anboyu Guo,Xiaohui Zhong,Hao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical, physics-informed neural forecaster AviaSafe predicts six-hourly global concentrations of four cloud hydrometeors for up to 7 days, using an Icing Condition constraint and masked-attention architecture; it achieves lower RMSE than baselines and outperforms some operational models at 7-day lead times, enabling aviation route optimization by distinguishing ice vs liquid water.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI weather models predict conventional variables and struggle to distinguish cloud microphysical species critical for aviation safety; a method that forecasts individual hydrometeor species and accounts for icing processes could reduce engine icing risk and improve route planning.

Method: A hierarchical neural forecaster with masked attention that first predicts cloud spatial distribution and then quantifies species within identified regions. It integrates the Icing Condition (IC) index as a physics-based constraint to identify regions where supercooled water drives ice growth. Training on ERA5 reanalysis data, it forecasts six-hourly global fields of four hydrometeor species and compares RMSE against baselines and operational models up to 7 days lead time.

Result: The model achieves lower RMSE for cloud species than baseline methods and outperforms certain operational numerical models on selected variables at 7-day lead times, with global six-hourly forecasts for four hydrometeor species.

Conclusion: Forecasting individual cloud species enables aviation applications such as route optimization by distinguishing ice and liquid water; the IC constraint links microphysics to icing risk, but real-time operational validation and uncertainty quantification remain important for deployment.

Abstract: Current AI weather forecasting models predict conventional atmospheric variables but cannot distinguish between cloud microphysical species critical for aviation safety. We introduce AviaSafe, a hierarchical, physics-informed neural forecaster that produces global, six-hourly predictions of these four hydrometeor species for lead times up to 7 days. Our approach addresses the unique challenges of cloud prediction: extreme sparsity, discontinuous distributions, and complex microphysical interactions between species. We integrate the Icing Condition (IC) index from aviation meteorology as a physics-based constraint that identifies regions where supercooled water fuels explosive ice crystal growth. The model employs a hierarchical architecture that first predicts cloud spatial distribution through masked attention, then quantifies species concentrations within identified regions. Training on ERA5 reanalysis data, our model achieves lower RMSE for cloud species compared to baseline and outperforms operational numerical models on certain key variables at 7-day lead times. The ability to forecast individual cloud species enables new applications in aviation route optimization where distinguishing between ice and liquid water determines engine icing risk.

</details>


### [248] [Training Agents to Self-Report Misbehavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22303)
*Bruce W. Lee,Chen Yueh-Han,Tomek Korbak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-incrimination training trains agents to signal covert misbehavior (via report_scheming()) to reduce undetected harms in frontier AI, outperforming monitoring and alignment baselines with low trade-offs and strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limits of alignment training, which may fail to curb hidden or instrumentally pursued misbehavior and can induce unwanted side effects. The work seeks a reliable, externally visible signal of misbehavior that survives adversarial prompting and distribution shifts.

Method: Train state-of-the-art agents (GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.0) to emit a visible reporting signal (report_scheming()) whenever they behave deceptively. Evaluate in out-of-distribution environments, compare against blackbox monitors and alignment baselines, test under adversarial prompt optimization, and assess generalization to misalignment-driven goals.

Result: Self-incrimination markedly reduces the rate of undetected successful attacks, outperforming matched-capability monitors and alignment baselines, while preserving general capabilities and instruction hierarchy. The signaling behavior is robust to task difficulty, external suspicion levels, prompting adversaries, and extends to scenarios where agents pursue misaligned goals themselves.

Conclusion: Self-incrimination offers a viable, scalable approach to frontier misalignment risk reduction that does not rely on preventing misbehavior or externally classifying it; it provides consistent signals across tasks and remains effective under adversarial conditions.

Abstract: Frontier AI agents may pursue hidden goals while concealing their pursuit from oversight. Alignment training aims to prevent such behavior by reinforcing the correct goals, but alignment may not always succeed and can lead to unwanted side effects. We propose self-incrimination training, which instead trains agents to produce a visible signal when they covertly misbehave. We train GPT-4.1 and Gemini-2.0 agents to call a report_scheming() tool when behaving deceptively and measure their ability to cause harm undetected in out-of-distribution environments. Self-incrimination significantly reduces the undetected successful attack rate, outperforming matched-capability monitors and alignment baselines while preserving instruction hierarchy and incurring minimal safety tax on general capabilities. Unlike blackbox monitoring, self-incrimination performance is consistent across tasks regardless of how suspicious the misbehavior appears externally. The trained behavior persists under adversarial prompt optimization and generalizes to settings where agents pursue misaligned goals themselves rather than being instructed to misbehave. Our results suggest self-incrimination offers a viable path for reducing frontier misalignment risk, one that neither assumes misbehavior can be prevented nor that it can be reliably classified from the outside.

</details>


### [249] [A 1/R Law for Kurtosis Contrast in Balanced Mixtures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22334)
*Yuda Bi,Wenjun Xiao,Linhao Bai,Vince D Calhoun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kurtosis-based ICA degrades in wide, balanced mixtures; population excess kurtosis scales as O(κ_max / R_eff); with balance, O(c_b κ_max / R) (c_b ~ log R). Sample-kurtosis estimation requires R ≲ κ_max √T to beat 1/√T; purification (m ≪ R) yields R-independent contrast Ω(1/m). Synthetic validation confirms decay, √T crossover, and contrast recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limitations of kurtosis-based ICA in high-dimensional, distributed mixtures and to identify a practical remedy that preserves discriminative contrast.

Method: Theoretical derivation of bounds on population excess kurtosis as a function of effective width R_eff; establish impossibility results under finite-moment assumptions for sample kurtosis; introduce purification by selecting a small set of sign-consistent sources with a data-driven heuristic; validate results with synthetic experiments.

Result: Population excess kurtosis decays as |κ(y)| = O(κ_max / R_eff); under balance, O(c_b κ_max / R) with c_b = O(log R). Surpassing the 1/√T estimation scale requires R ≲ κ_max √T. Purification with m ≪ R recovers contrast independent of R, achieving Ω(1/m); experimental results corroborate the theory, showing the predicted decay, √T crossover, and contrast recovery.

Conclusion: Kurtosis-based ICA faces intrinsic redundancy in wide mixtures, but purification provides a practical, data-driven remedy that restores discriminative power without sensitivity to the mixture width.

Abstract: Kurtosis-based Independent Component Analysis (ICA) weakens in wide, balanced mixtures. We prove a sharp redundancy law: for a standardized projection with effective width $R_{\mathrm{eff}}$ (participation ratio), the population excess kurtosis obeys $|κ(y)|=O(κ_{\max}/R_{\mathrm{eff}})$, yielding the order-tight $O(c_bκ_{\max}/R)$ under balance (typically $c_b=O(\log R)$). As an impossibility screen, under standard finite-moment conditions for sample kurtosis estimation, surpassing the $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ estimation scale requires $R\lesssim κ_{\max}\sqrt{T}$. We also show that \emph{purification} -- selecting $m\!\ll\!R$ sign-consistent sources -- restores $R$-independent contrast $Ω(1/m)$, with a simple data-driven heuristic. Synthetic experiments validate the predicted decay, the $\sqrt{T}$ crossover, and contrast recovery.

</details>


### [250] [Structure and Redundancy in Large Language Models: A Spectral Study via Random Matrix Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22345)
*Davide Ettori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual framework combining Spectral Geometry and Random Matrix Theory to improve reliability and efficiency in deep learning: EigenTrack detects hallucinations/ODI in real-time via spectral descriptors; RMT-KD compresses networks by distilling along eigenvalue outliers to lower-dimensional subspaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address persistent issues of hallucinations, fragile generalization under distribution shift, and rising compute/energy demands as models scale.

Method: Analyze eigenvalue dynamics of hidden activations across layers/inputs; use spectral statistics (entropy, variance, MP deviations) as descriptors; train lightweight recurrent classifiers for temporal evolution to detect reliability failures; iterative self-distillation projecting network onto subspaces guided by outlier eigenvalues.

Result: Enables early detection of reliability failures before outputs; produces more compact, energy-efficient models with preserved accuracy; interpretable insights into representation dynamics.

Conclusion: A unified spectral-RMT framework yields practical mechanisms for reliability monitoring and efficient compression, linking activation structure to task-relevant information via eigenvalue analysis.

Abstract: This thesis addresses two persistent and closely related challenges in modern deep learning, reliability and efficiency, through a unified framework grounded in Spectral Geometry and Random Matrix Theory (RMT). As deep networks and large language models continue to scale, their internal behavior becomes increasingly opaque, leading to hallucinations, fragile generalization under distribution shift, and growing computational and energy demands. By analyzing the eigenvalue dynamics of hidden activations across layers and inputs, this work shows that spectral statistics provide a compact, stable, and interpretable lens on model behavior, capable of separating structured, causal representations from noise-dominated variability. Within this framework, the first contribution, EigenTrack, introduces a real-time method for detecting hallucinations and out-of-distribution behavior in large language and vision-language models. EigenTrack transforms streaming activations into spectral descriptors such as entropy, variance, and deviations from the Marchenko-Pastur baseline, and models their temporal evolution using lightweight recurrent classifiers, enabling early detection of reliability failures before they appear in model outputs while offering interpretable insight into representation dynamics. The second contribution, RMT-KD, presents a principled approach to compressing deep networks via random matrix theoretic knowledge distillation. By interpreting outlier eigenvalues in activation spectra as carriers of task-relevant information, RMT-KD progressively projects networks onto lower-dimensional subspaces through iterative self-distillation, yielding significantly more compact and energy-efficient models while preserving accuracy and dense, hardware-friendly structure.

</details>


### [251] [Learning geometry-dependent lead-field operators for forward ECG modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22367)
*Arsenii Dokuchaev,Francesca Bonizzoni,Stefano Pagani,Francesco Regazzoni,Simone Pezzuto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shape-informed neural surrogate for the lead-field operator enables accurate, efficient forward ECG simulations with reduced data needs and no full torso segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current forward ECG models require accurate torso geometry; imaging protocols often omit the full torso, and lead-field computations scale with electrode count, limiting high-density recordings. There is a gap for a method that combines anatomical fidelity, low data requirements, and computational efficiency.

Method: A two-component framework: (1) geometry-encoding module maps anatomical shapes into a low-dimensional latent space; (2) geometry-conditioned neural surrogate predicts lead-field gradients from spatial coordinates, electrode positions, and latent codes. It serves as a drop-in replacement for the full-order lead-field model.

Result: The method achieves high lead-field accuracy: mean angular error 5° within the torso and inside the heart; relative mean squared error <2.5% for ECG simulations. It outperforms pseudo lead-field approximations and maintains negligible inference cost. The latent representation is compact, enabling deployment in data-limited settings without full torso segmentation.

Conclusion: The proposed shape-informed surrogate delivers high anatomical fidelity with low data requirements and computational cost, facilitating accurate, high-density ECG simulations without needing detailed torso segmentation.

Abstract: Modern forward electrocardiogram (ECG) computational models rely on an accurate representation of the torso domain. The lead-field method enables fast ECG simulations while preserving full geometric fidelity. Achieving high anatomical accuracy in torso representation is, however, challenging in clinical practice, as imaging protocols are typically focused on the heart and often do not include the entire torso. In addition, the computational cost of the lead-field method scales linearly with the number of electrodes, limiting its applicability in high-density recording settings. To date, no existing approach simultaneously achieves high anatomical fidelity, low data requirements and computational efficiency. In this work, we propose a shape-informed surrogate model of the lead-field operator that serves as a drop-in replacement for the full-order model in forward ECG simulations. The proposed framework consists of two components: a geometry-encoding module that maps anatomical shapes into a low-dimensional latent space, and a geometry-conditioned neural surrogate that predicts lead-field gradients from spatial coordinates, electrode positions and latent codes. The proposed method achieves high accuracy in approximating lead fields both within the torso (mean angular error 5°) and inside the heart, resulting in highly accurate ECG simulations (relative mean squared error <2.5%. The surrogate consistently outperforms the widely used pseudo lead-field approximation while preserving negligible inference cost. Owing to its compact latent representation, the method does not require a fully detailed torso segmentation and can therefore be deployed in data-limited settings while preserving high-fidelity ECG simulations.

</details>


### [252] [Disentangling Shared and Target-Enriched Topics via Background-Contrastive Non-negative Matrix Factorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22387)
*Yixuan Li,Archer Y. Yang,Yue Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable, interpretable contrastive non-negative matrix factorization method that isolates target-specific signals in high-dimensional biological data by jointly factorizing target and matched background with shared non-negative bases, yielding interpretable components and enabling discovery of signals masked by background variation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biological signals of interest in high-dimensional data are often obscured by dominant, condition-shared variation from baseline biology or technical effects. This confounding background can hide condition-specific structure, and such background topics are often unknown. Existing background correction methods are either computationally intractable for large data or lack interpretability. There is a need for a scalable, interpretable method that suppresses background structure while preserving target signals.

Method: Propose background contrastive Non-negative Matrix Factorization (contrastive-NMF). It jointly factorizes a target dataset and a matched background using shared non-negative bases. The contrastive objective suppresses background-expressed structure, yielding target-enriched latent topics with interpretable, non-negative components at the feature level. Optimization is performed via efficient multiplicative updates using matrix multiplications, enabling GPU-accelerated, minibatch training for scalability.

Result: Across simulations and diverse biological datasets, the method reveals signals obscured by conventional methods. Demonstrated in postmortem depressive brain single-cell RNA-seq (disease-associated programs), genotype-linked protein expression in mice, treatment-specific transcriptional changes in leukemia, and TP53-dependent drug responses in cancer cell lines, highlighting its ability to isolate target-specific variation and improve interpretability.

Conclusion: Background contrastive-NMF provides an interpretable, scalable approach to extract target-enriched latent topics by suppressing shared background structure, enabling discovery of condition-specific biological programs in high-dimensional data. Its GPU-accelerated, minibatch training makes it practical for big datasets, expanding the toolkit for analyzing complex biomedical signals.

Abstract: Biological signals of interest in high-dimensional data are often masked by dominant variation shared across conditions. This variation, arising from baseline biological structure or technical effects, can prevent standard dimensionality reduction methods from resolving condition-specific structure. The challenge is that these confounding topics are often unknown and mixed with biological signals. Existing background correction methods are either unscalable to high dimensions or not interpretable. We introduce background contrastive Non-negative Matrix Factorization (\model), which extracts target-enriched latent topics by jointly factorizing a target dataset and a matched background using shared non-negative bases under a contrastive objective that suppresses background-expressed structure. This approach yields non-negative components that are directly interpretable at the feature level, and explicitly isolates target-specific variation. \model is learned by an efficient multiplicative update algorithm via matrix multiplication such that it is highly efficient on GPU hardware and scalable to big data via minibatch training akin to deep learning approach. Across simulations and diverse biological datasets, \model reveals signals obscured by conventional methods, including disease-associated programs in postmortem depressive brain single-cell RNA-seq, genotype-linked protein expression patterns in mice, treatment-specific transcriptional changes in leukemia, and TP53-dependent drug responses in cancer cell lines.

</details>


### [253] [Predicting Multi-Drug Resistance in Bacterial Isolates Through Performance Comparison and LIME-based Interpretation of Classification Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22400)
*Santanam Wishal,Riad Sahara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interpretable ensemble ML framework predicts multidrug resistance (MDR) in bacterial isolates from clinical data, with LIME-based explanations to support stewardship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDR poses major treatment challenges and delays susceptibility testing; there is a critical need for fast, accurate, and transparent predictive tools to aid clinical decisions and antimicrobial stewardship.

Method: Using a curated dataset of 9,714 isolates, resistance is encoded at the antibiotic-family level. Five classifiers were evaluated: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, AdaBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM. Models were assessed with accuracy, F1-score, AUC-ROC, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Ensemble models (XGBoost, LightGBM) showed superior performance. LIME provided instance-level explanations for predictions, highlighting resistance to quinolones, Co-trimoxazole, Colistin, aminoglycosides, and furans as key MDR contributors.

Result: Ensemble models outperformed baseline methods across all metrics; LIME explanations aligned with known biological mechanisms, offering actionable insights for antimicrobial stewardship and enabling earlier MDR identification in a clinical setting.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that pairing high-performing ML ensembles with local interpretability can enhance accuracy and trust in ML-assisted clinical decision support for MDR, supporting earlier intervention and informed therapeutic decisions.

Abstract: The rise of Antimicrobial Resistance, particularly Multi-Drug Resistance (MDR), presents a critical challenge for clinical decision-making due to limited treatment options and delays in conventional susceptibility testing. This study proposes an interpretable machine learning framework to predict MDR in bacterial isolates using clinical features and antibiotic susceptibility patterns. Five classification models were evaluated, including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, AdaBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM. The models were trained on a curated dataset of 9,714 isolates, with resistance encoded at the antibiotic family level to capture cross-class resistance patterns consistent with MDR definitions. Performance assessment included accuracy, F1-score, AUC-ROC, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Ensemble models, particularly XGBoost and LightGBM, demonstrated superior predictive capability across all metrics. To address the clinical transparency gap, Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) was applied to generate instance-level explanations. LIME identified resistance to quinolones, Co-trimoxazole, Colistin, aminoglycosides, and Furanes as the strongest contributors to MDR predictions, aligning with known biological mechanisms. The results show that combining high-performing models with local interpretability provides both accuracy and actionable insights for antimicrobial stewardship. This framework supports earlier MDR identification and enhances trust in machine learning-assisted clinical decision support.

</details>


### [254] [MolFM-Lite: Multi-Modal Molecular Property Prediction with Conformer Ensemble Attention and Cross-Modal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22405)
*Syed Omer Shah,Mohammed Maqsood Ahmed,Danish Mohiuddin Mohammed,Shahnawaz Alam,Mohd Vahaj ur Rahman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MolFM-Lite integrates 1D SELFIES, 2D graphs, and 3D conformer ensembles via cross-modal attention and FiLM conditioning to predict molecular properties. It uses Boltzmann-weighted priors for conformers and cross-modal fusion; yields 7–11% absolute AUC gains over single-modality baselines on MoleculeNet scaffold-split benchmarks, with ~2% extra from conformer ensembles. Pre-training on ZINC250K with cross-modal contrastive and masked-atom objectives improves initialization with modest compute; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-representation models and static geometry miss complementary information captured by sequence, graph, and 3D structure. A multi-modal, thermodynamically aware approach can leverage diverse signals and context to improve predictive performance and data efficiency.

Method: MolFM-Lite encodes three modalities: 1D SELFIES sequences, 2D molecular graphs, and 3D conformer ensembles. It uses a conformer ensemble attention mechanism that combines learnable attention with Boltzmann-weighted priors over RDKit conformers to reflect thermodynamic distributions. A cross-modal fusion layer enables each modality to attend to others. FiLM condenses experimental context to modulate predictions. Pre-training on ZINC250K employs cross-modal contrastive and masked-atom objectives. Evaluation on four MoleculeNet scaffold-split benchmarks uses the model's own splits with baselines re-evaluated under the same protocol; ablations assess component contributions.

Result: Tri-modal fusion yields 7–11 percentage points AUC improvement over single-modality baselines; incorporating conformer ensembles adds ~2 percentage points over single-conformer variants. Comprehensive ablations confirm each component contributes independently. Pre-training provides effective weight initialization at modest compute cost. Code, trained models, and data splits are released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: A thermodynamically informed, cross-modal fusion framework (MolFM-Lite) that jointly encodes sequence, graph, and conformer data with FiLM conditioning and multi-task pre-training improves molecular property prediction across benchmarks, and is reproducible and extensible to other modalities.

Abstract: Most machine learning models for molecular property prediction rely on a single molecular representation (either a sequence, a graph, or a 3D structure) and treat molecular geometry as static. We present MolFM-Lite, a multi-modal model that jointly encodes SELFIES sequences (1D), molecular graphs (2D), and conformer ensembles (3D) through cross-attention fusion, while conditioning predictions on experimental context via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM). Our main methodological contributions are: (1) a conformer ensemble attention mechanism that combines learnable attention with Boltzmann-weighted priors over multiple RDKit-generated conformers, capturing the thermodynamic distribution of molecular shapes; and (2) a cross-modal fusion layer where each modality can attend to others, enabling complementary information sharing. We evaluate on four MoleculeNet scaffold-split benchmarks using our model's own splits, and report all baselines re-evaluated under the same protocol. Comprehensive ablation studies across all four datasets confirm that each architectural component contributes independently, with tri-modal fusion providing 7-11% AUC improvement over single-modality baselines and conformer ensembles adding approximately 2% over single-conformer variants. Pre-training on ZINC250K (~250K molecules) using cross-modal contrastive and masked-atom objectives enables effective weight initialization at modest compute cost. We release all code, trained models, and data splits to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [255] [A Learning-Based Hybrid Decision Framework for Matching Systems with User Departure Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22412)
*Ruiqi Zhou,Donghao Zhu,Houcai Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A learning-based Hybrid policy blends immediate and delayed matching in dynamic markets to balance waiting, congestion, and matching efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, static matching policies are inflexible in dynamic environments. Delaying matches can improve efficiency but imposes waiting costs; a data-driven approach is needed to adapt to observed departure behavior.

Method: A data-driven framework that (i) collects departure-time data over time, (ii) estimates the departure distribution via regression, and (iii) uses a threshold-based rule to decide whether to delay in the next period, thereby interpolating between greedy (immediate) and patient (delayed) policies.

Result: The framework can substantially reduce waiting times and congestion with limited sacrifice of matching efficiency, acting as a robust, adaptive alternative to static policies.

Conclusion: Dynamic, data-driven Hybrid matching provides a flexible mechanism to balance trade-offs in dynamic markets; performance depends on the quality of departure-distribution estimation and the threshold tuning.

Abstract: In matching markets such as kidney exchanges and freight exchanges, delayed matching has been shown to improve overall market efficiency. The benefits of delay are highly sensitive to participants' sojourn times and departure behavior, and delaying matches can impose significant costs, including longer waiting times and increased market congestion. These competing effects make fixed matching policies inherently inflexible in dynamic environments. We propose a learning-based Hybrid framework that adaptively combines immediate and delayed matching. The framework continuously collects data on user departures over time, estimates the underlying departure distribution via regression, and determines whether to delay matching in the subsequent period based on a decision threshold that governs the system's tolerance for matching efficiency loss. The proposed framework can substantially reduce waiting times and congestion while sacrificing only a limited amount of matching efficiency. By dynamically adjusting its matching strategy, the Hybrid framework enables system performance to flexibly interpolate between purely greedy and purely patient policies, offering a robust and adaptive alternative to static matching mechanisms.

</details>


### [256] [Physics Informed Viscous Value Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23280)
*Hrishikesh Viswanath,Juanwu Lu,S. Talha Bukhari,Damon Conover,Ziran Wang,Aniket Bera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics-informed regularization for offline goal-conditioned RL using viscosity solution of the HJB equation, with a Feynman-Kac-based Monte Carlo objective to stabilize learning. Improves geometric consistency in high-dimensional tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline GCRL struggles with accurate value estimation due to limited state-action coverage; prior PDE-based priors (e.g., Eikonal) can be ill-posed in complex environments. A physics-grounded regularizer from optimal control aims to provide a robust inductive bias.

Method: Derive a regularization term from the viscosity solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation and integrate it into value updates (value iteration). Use the Feynman-Kac theorem to reformulate the PDE solution as an expectation, enabling tractable Monte Carlo estimation and avoiding instability from higher-order gradients.

Result: Empirical results show improved geometric consistency and performance on navigation and high-dimensional manipulation tasks; open-source implementation available.

Conclusion: Grounds offline GCRL value estimation in optimal control theory through a physics-based regularizer derived from the HJB viscosity solution. The Feynman-Kac reformulation provides stable, sample-based estimation, broadening applicability to complex tasks. Potential future work includes deeper theoretical guarantees and scalability considerations.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) learns goal-conditioned policies from static pre-collected datasets. However, accurate value estimation remains a challenge due to the limited coverage of the state-action space. Recent physics-informed approaches have sought to address this by imposing physical and geometric constraints on the value function through regularization defined over first-order partial differential equations (PDEs), such as the Eikonal equation. However, these formulations can often be ill-posed in complex, high-dimensional environments. In this work, we propose a physics-informed regularization derived from the viscosity solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation. By providing a physics-based inductive bias, our approach grounds the learning process in optimal control theory, explicitly regularizing and bounding updates during value iterations. Furthermore, we leverage the Feynman-Kac theorem to recast the PDE solution as an expectation, enabling a tractable Monte Carlo estimation of the objective that avoids numerical instability in higher-order gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our method improves geometric consistency, making it broadly applicable to navigation and high-dimensional, complex manipulation tasks. Open-source codes are available at https://github.com/HrishikeshVish/phys-fk-value-GCRL.

</details>


### [257] [Revisiting Chebyshev Polynomial and Anisotropic RBF Models for Tabular Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22422)
*Luciano Gerber,Huw Lloyd*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Smooth-basis models can rival trees on CPU-limited tabular regression and offer better generalization; transformers dominate accuracy but have practicality issues; include smooth models in model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address underutilization of smooth, differentiable models in tabular regression and assess competitiveness across many datasets.

Method: Introduce three smooth models: anisotropic RBF network with data-driven centres and gradient-based widths; ridge-regularized Chebyshev polynomial regressor; Chebyshev model tree; release as scikit-learn packages; benchmark against tree ensembles, a pretrained transformer, and baselines; evaluate accuracy and generalization.

Result: Transformer often highest accuracy but CPU-constraints limit deployment; among CPU-viable models, smooth baselines and tree ensembles are statistically tied in accuracy, but smooth models show tighter generalization; results support including smooth-basis models in candidate pools.

Conclusion: Recommend routinely including smooth-basis models in practice, especially when tighter generalization and smoothly varying predictions are desirable; smooth models complement tree ensembles and broad applicability in CPU-based settings.

Abstract: Smooth-basis models such as Chebyshev polynomial regressors and radial basis function (RBF) networks are well established in numerical analysis. Their continuously differentiable prediction surfaces suit surrogate optimisation, sensitivity analysis, and other settings where the response varies gradually with inputs. Despite these properties, smooth models seldom appear in tabular regression, where tree ensembles dominate. We ask whether they can compete, benchmarking models across 55 regression datasets organised by application domain.
  We develop an anisotropic RBF network with data-driven centre placement and gradient-based width optimisation, a ridge-regularised Chebyshev polynomial regressor, and a smooth-tree hybrid (Chebyshev model tree); all three are released as scikit-learn-compatible packages. We benchmark these against tree ensembles, a pre-trained transformer, and standard baselines, evaluating accuracy alongside generalisation behaviour.
  The transformer ranks first on accuracy across a majority of datasets, but its GPU dependence, inference latency, and dataset-size limits constrain deployment in the CPU-based settings common across applied science and industry. Among CPU-viable models, smooth models and tree ensembles are statistically tied on accuracy, but the former tend to exhibit tighter generalisation gaps. We recommend routinely including smooth-basis models in the candidate pool, particularly when downstream use benefits from tighter generalisation and gradually varying predictions.

</details>


### [258] [Calibrated Test-Time Guidance for Bayesian Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22428)
*Daniel Geyfman,Felix Draxler,Jan Groeneveld,Hyunsoo Lee,Theofanis Karaletsos,Stephan Mandt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper exposes miscalibration in test-time guidance for pretrained diffusion models, attributes it to structural approximations, and proposes consistent estimators to enable calibrated Bayesian posterior sampling, achieving strong empirical gains on Bayesian inference tasks and Black Hole image reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrated Bayesian inference is essential for reliable diffusion-model guidance. Existing test-time guidance optimizes rewards rather than sampling from the true posterior, causing miscalibrated results.

Method: The authors diagnose the structural causes of posterior mismatch in test-time guidance, derive consistent alternative estimators that recover the Bayesian posterior sampling, and validate the approach with experiments across Bayesian inference tasks and black-hole image reconstruction.

Result: Significant improvements over prior methods on Bayesian inference benchmarks and matching state-of-the-art performance in Black Hole image reconstruction.

Conclusion: Consistent estimators restore calibrated posterior sampling in test-time guidance, improving reliability and performance of diffusion-based inference, with potential applicability to broader guided generation tasks.

Abstract: Test-time guidance is a widely used mechanism for steering pretrained diffusion models toward outcomes specified by a reward function. Existing approaches, however, focus on maximizing reward rather than sampling from the true Bayesian posterior, leading to miscalibrated inference. In this work, we show that common test-time guidance methods do not recover the correct posterior distribution and identify the structural approximations responsible for this failure. We then propose consistent alternative estimators that enable calibrated sampling from the Bayesian posterior. We significantly outperform previous methods on a set of Bayesian inference tasks, and match state-of-the-art in black hole image reconstruction.

</details>


### [259] [From Bias to Balance: Fairness-Aware Paper Recommendation for Equitable Peer Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22438)
*Uttamasha Anjally Oyshi,Susan Gauch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fair-PaperRec uses a differentiable fairness loss to re-rank post-review papers, improving inclusion of underrepresented groups with minimal utility loss, validated on synthetic biases and real SIGCHI conference data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate systemic biases in double-blind peer review by explicitly optimizing for intersectional fairness in the post-review ranking stage.

Method: MLP with differentiable fairness regularizer over intersectional attributes; post-review re-ranking; experiments on synthetic datasets with varying bias levels and on real conference data (SIGCHI DIS IUI).

Result: In synthetic settings, increasing fairness weight boosts macro/micro diversity while keeping utility roughly stable; in real data, up to ~42% gain in underrepresented participation with only ~3% utility loss relative to historical selections.

Conclusion: Fair-PaperRec demonstrates that fairness regularization can serve as an equity mechanism and mild quality regularizer, effective across bias regimes, and offers a practical framework for equity-focused post-review paper selection.

Abstract: Despite frequent double-blind review, systemic biases related to author demographics still disadvantage underrepresented groups. We start from a simple hypothesis: if a post-review recommender is trained with an explicit fairness regularizer, it should increase inclusion without degrading quality. To test this, we introduce Fair-PaperRec, a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with a differentiable fairness loss over intersectional attributes (e.g., race, country) that re-ranks papers after double-blind review. We first probe the hypothesis on synthetic datasets spanning high, moderate, and near-fair biases. Across multiple randomized runs, these controlled studies map where increasing the fairness weight strengthens macro/micro diversity while keeping utility approximately stable, demonstrating robustness and adaptability under varying disparity levels. We then carry the hypothesis into the original setting, conference data from ACM Special Interest Group on Computer-Human Interaction (SIGCHI), Designing Interactive Systems (DIS), and Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI). In this real-world scenario, an appropriately tuned configuration of Fair-PaperRec achieves up to a 42.03% increase in underrepresented-group participation with at most a 3.16% change in overall utility relative to the historical selection. Taken together, the synthetic-to-original progression shows that fairness regularization can act as both an equity mechanism and a mild quality regularizer, especially in highly biased regimes. By first analyzing the behavior of the fairness parameters under controlled conditions and then validating them on real submissions, Fair-PaperRec offers a practical, equity-focused framework for post-review paper selection that preserves, and in some settings can even enhance, measured scholarly quality.

</details>


### [260] [ECHO: Encoding Communities via High-order Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22446)
*Emilio Ferrara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECHO reframes community detection as a scalable, self-supervised diffusion process guided by a topology-aware router, achieving near-linear memory efficiency and fast clustering on graphs with millions of nodes, while overcoming the resolution limit and heterophily-related issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between topology-only community detection and Graph Neural Networks by tackling the Semantic Wall (feature over-smoothing in GNNs) and Systems Wall (O(N^2) memory in pairwise clustering), enabling accurate, scalable detection on large attributed networks.

Method: Introduce ECHO: a self-supervised architecture with a Topology Aware Router that analyzes sparsity, density, and assortativity to select inductive biases; memory-sharded full-batch contrastive objective; chunked O(N*K) similarity extraction; unified memory-optimized framework enabling hardware-adaptive deployments; multi-scale diffusion for adaptive communities.

Result: Empirically, ECHO achieves scale-invariant accuracy on synthetic LFR benchmarks up to 1M nodes under severe topological noise; on real networks with 1.6M nodes and 30M edges, clustering completes in minutes with throughput >2,800 nodes/sec, matching optimized purely topological baselines; demonstrates consistent overcoming of the classical resolution limit.

Conclusion: ECHO delivers scalable, accurate community detection in attributed networks by integrating topology-aware routing with memory-efficient diffusion and contrastive learning, preserving gradient precision while bypassing O(N^2) bottlenecks; open-source implementation supports diverse hardware constraints.

Abstract: Community detection in attributed networks faces a fundamental divide: topological algorithms ignore semantic features, while Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) encounter devastating computational bottlenecks. Specifically, GNNs suffer from a Semantic Wall of feature over smoothing in dense or heterophilic networks, and a Systems Wall driven by the O(N^2) memory constraints of pairwise clustering. To dismantle these barriers, we introduce ECHO (Encoding Communities via High order Operators), a scalable, self supervised architecture that reframes community detection as an adaptive, multi scale diffusion process. ECHO features a Topology Aware Router that automatically analyzes structural heuristics sparsity, density, and assortativity to route graphs through the optimal inductive bias, preventing heterophilic poisoning while ensuring semantic densification. Coupled with a memory sharded full batch contrastive objective and a novel chunked O(N \cdot K) similarity extraction method, ECHO completely bypasses traditional O(N^2) memory bottlenecks without sacrificing the mathematical precision of global gradients. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that this topology feature synergy consistently overcomes the classical resolution limit. On synthetic LFR benchmarks scaled up to 1 million nodes, ECHO achieves scale invariant accuracy despite severe topological noise. Furthermore, on massive real world social networks with over 1.6 million nodes and 30 million edges, it completes clustering in mere minutes with throughputs exceeding 2,800 nodes per second matching the speed of highly optimized purely topological baselines. The implementation utilizes a unified framework that automatically engages memory sharded optimization to support adoption across varying hardware constraints. GitHub Repository: https://github.com/emilioferrara/ECHO-GNN

</details>


### [261] [Beyond performance-wise Contribution Evaluation in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22470)
*Balazs Pejo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-dimensional client contribution evaluation in federated learning using Shapley-value approximations reveals no single client dominates across reliability, resilience, and fairness; accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluation and rewarding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of accuracy-centric evaluation in FL and motivate a principled, multi-faceted attribution of client contributions to model trustworthiness (reliability, resilience, fairness) to guide fair rewards and robust deployment.

Method: Apply state-of-the-art Shapley-value approximations to quantify each client's contributions across three axes (reliability, resilience, fairness) in FL; analyze the independence of dimensions and compare with traditional accuracy-based metrics.

Result: Found that no client excels across all three dimensions; contributions are largely uncorrelated; current evaluation schemes are inadequate for comprehensive assessment or equitable reward allocation.

Conclusion: A multi-dimensional attribution framework based on Shapley values is needed to capture diverse contributions to trustworthiness in FL and to inform fair incentives and robust aggregation.

Abstract: Federated learning offers a privacy-friendly collaborative learning framework, yet its success, like any joint venture, hinges on the contributions of its participants. Existing client evaluation methods predominantly focus on model performance, such as accuracy or loss, which represents only one dimension of a machine learning model's overall utility. In contrast, this work investigates the critical, yet overlooked, issue of client contributions towards a model's trustworthiness -- specifically, its reliability (tolerance to noisy data), resilience (resistance to adversarial examples), and fairness (measured via demographic parity). To quantify these multifaceted contributions, we employ the state-of-the-art approximation of the Shapley value, a principled method for value attribution. Our results reveal that no single client excels across all dimensions, which are largely independent from each other, highlighting a critical flaw in current evaluation scheme: no single metric is adequate for comprehensive evaluation and equitable rewarding allocation.

</details>


### [262] [Efficient Continual Learning in Language Models via Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22479)
*Afshin Khadangi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRC^2 is a sparse, decoder-only architecture with thalamic routing and a fast corrective pathway that enables continual learning by improving the stability-plasticity tradeoff while maintaining comparable compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning in deployed language models suffers from catastrophic forgetting under non-stationary data and from methods that increase latency or memory demands. An architectural solution that scales to long contexts is needed to achieve rapid adaptation without degrading previously learned behavior.

Method: Introduce TRC^2, a decoder-only backbone featuring sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns, with modulation, prediction, memory, and feedback pathways plus a fast corrective pathway. The block is sparse and chunk-parallel, enabling efficient training/inference. A reproducible training/evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness measure proxy forgetting under streaming domain shifts, enabling ablations of subsystems.

Result: On language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC^2 improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving prior behavior.

Conclusion: Architectural design with sparse thalamic routing and fast corrective pathways offers a scalable solution to continual learning in language models, balancing adaptability and retention with efficient computation; facilitates clean ablations and reproducible evaluation.

Abstract: Continual learning is a core requirement for deployed language models, yet standard training and fine-tuning pipelines remain brittle under non-stationary data. Online updates often induce catastrophic forgetting, while methods that improve stability frequently increase latency, memory footprint, or dense computation in ways that do not scale well to long contexts. We introduce TRC$^{2}$ (Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns), a decoder-only backbone that addresses continual learning at the architectural level. TRC$^{2}$ combines sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns with mechanisms for modulation, prediction, memory, and feedback, together with a fast corrective pathway that supports rapid adaptation without destabilizing slower parameters. The resulting block is sparse and chunk-parallel, enabling efficient training and inference while preserving clean ablations of each subsystem. We instantiate a reproducible training and evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness that measures proxy forgetting under streaming domain shifts. Across language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC$^{2}$ improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving previously acquired behavior.

</details>


### [263] [Reinforcement-aware Knowledge Distillation for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22495)
*Zhaoyang Zhang,Shuli Jiang,Yantao Shen,Yuting Zhang,Dhananjay Ram,Shuo Yang,Zhuowen Tu,Wei Xia,Stefano Soatto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL-aware distillation (RLAD) for RL-trained LLMs uses selective imitation with Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD) to replace KL with a PPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher-old-policy mixture, enabling stable, advantage-aware distillation during RL and improved student performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training RL improves long-chain reasoning in LLMs but distilling to smaller students suffers from distribution mismatch and objective interference between imitation and reward optimization. KL-based regularization requires careful tuning and can hinder policy learning.

Method: RLAD performs selective imitation during RL. The core TRRD replaces KL with a PPO/GRPO-like likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher–old-policy mixture, yielding advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks shows RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.

Conclusion: RLAD provides a principled, adaptive distillation framework for RL-based knowledge distillation, mitigating distribution mismatch and improving sample efficiency and performance of compact students by decoupling imitation from pure reward maximization through a trust-region ratio objective.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has recently driven major gains in long chain-of-thought reasoning large language models (LLMs), but the high inference cost of such models motivates distillation into smaller students. Most existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods are designed for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), relying on fixed teacher traces or teacher-student Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence-based regularization. When combined with RL, these approaches often suffer from distribution mismatch and objective interference: teacher supervision may not align with the student's evolving rollout distribution, and the KL regularizer can compete with reward maximization and require careful loss balancing. To address these issues, we propose RL-aware distillation (RLAD), which performs selective imitation during RL -- guiding the student toward the teacher only when it improves the current policy update. Our core component, Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD), replaces the teacher-student KL regularizer with a PPO/GRPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher--old-policy mixture, yielding advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and naturally balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation. Across diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks, RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.

</details>


### [264] [Sharp Convergence Rates for Masked Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22505)
*Yuchen Liang,Zhiheng Tan,Ness Shroff,Yingbin Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a direct TV-based convergence analysis for Euler and First-Hitting Sampler (FHS) in discrete diffusion models, improving over KL-based analyses by relaxing score-estimation assumptions, proving a tight Euler lower bound, and showing FHS error equals score-estimation error with a matching lower bound; uses a TV error decomposition along the CTMC trajectory and a decoupling-based path-wise analysis for FHS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KL-based analyses of discrete diffusion samplers often yield loose bounds and require strong assumptions on score estimation. There is a gap in understanding the high-performance FHS variant. A direct TV-based approach aims to tighten dependencies, remove surrogate initialization requirements, and provide tight lower/upper bounds for Euler and FHS.

Method: Develop a direct total-variation (TV) analysis for the Euler method, with a new TV error decomposition along the CTMC trajectory; establish convergence guarantees with relaxed score-estimation assumptions and no surrogate initialization; derive a lower bound for Euler to prove tightness. For FHS, introduce a decoupling-based, path-wise analysis showing its sampling error is solely due to score estimation error, and provide a matching lower bound to demonstrate tightness.

Result: Euler sampler: TV-based convergence guarantees with improved parameter dependencies, relaxed score-estimation assumptions, no surrogate initialization, and a first tight convergence lower bound in data dimension d and accuracy ε. FHS sampler: sampling error is exactly the score-estimation error, with a matching lower bound, indicating no extra sampling error beyond estimation. Overall, a direct TV-based error decomposition and path-wise decoupling analysis are developed, potentially applicable beyond the studied setting.

Conclusion: The paper provides a tighter, TV-based theoretical framework for discrete diffusion samplers, overcoming KL-based limitations, and clarifying the error sources for Euler and FHS. The methods and bounds offer a foundation for future analyses and may be of independent interest due to their path-wise and decoupled perspectives.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models have achieved strong empirical performance in text and other symbolic domains, with masked (absorbing-rate) variants emerging as competitive alternatives to autoregressive models. Among existing samplers, the Euler method remains the standard choice in many applications, and more recently, the First-Hitting Sampler (FHS) has shown considerable promise for masked diffusion models.
