Abstract: Recent advances in generic large models, such as GPT and DeepSeek, have motivated the introduction of universality to graph pre-training, aiming to learn rich and generalizable knowledge across diverse domains using graph representations to improve performance in various downstream applications. However, most existing methods face challenges in learning effective knowledge from generic graphs, primarily due to simplistic data alignment and limited training guidance. The issue of simplistic data alignment arises from the use of a straightforward unification for highly diverse graph data, which fails to align semantics and misleads pre-training models. The problem with limited training guidance lies in the arbitrary application of in-domain pre-training paradigms to cross-domain scenarios. While it is effective in enhancing discriminative representation in one data space, it struggles to capture effective knowledge from many graphs. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Latent sEmantic Distribution Alignment (LEDA) model for universal graph pre-training. Specifically, we first introduce a dimension projection unit to adaptively align diverse domain features into a shared semantic space with minimal information loss. Furthermore, we design a variational semantic inference module to obtain the shared latent distribution. The distribution is then adopted to guide the domain projection, aligning it with shared semantics across domains and ensuring cross-domain semantic learning. LEDA exhibits strong performance across a broad range of graphs and downstream tasks. Remarkably, in few-shot cross-domain settings, it significantly outperforms in-domain baselines and advanced universal pre-training models.

</details>


### [291] [Duel-Evolve: Reward-Free Test-Time Scaling via LLM Self-Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.21585)
*Sweta Karlekar,Carolina Zheng,Magnus Saebo,Nicolas Beltran-Velez,Shuyang Yu,John Bowlan,Michal Kucer,David Blei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Duel-Evolve replaces scalar rewards with pairwise LLM-derived preferences in an evolutionary optimizer, using Bayesian Bradley-Terry to estimate candidate quality and Double Thompson Sampling to allocate comparisons and select parents, achieving strong test-time improvements without external rewards or labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many tasks lack reliable scalar reward signals for test-time optimization. Pairwise comparisons are easier to obtain, can be elicited from the same LLM, and still guide search effectively. The goal is to optimize discrete output spaces without hand-crafted scoring functions or reward models.

Method: An evolutionary search algorithm that uses pairwise preferences (no external rewards). Candidate comparisons are modeled with Bayesian Bradley-Terry to estimate quality with uncertainty. Double Thompson Sampling allocates comparison budget toward plausible optima and selects high-quality parents to generate better candidates. No ground-truth labels or reward models are used.

Result: On MathBench, Duel-Evolve achieves ~20 percentage point improvement in accuracy over existing methods and baselines. On LiveCodeBench, it surpasses comparable iterative methods by >12 percentage points. The approach requires no reward model, no external labels during search, and no hand-crafted scoring function.

Conclusion: Pairwise self-preferences elicited from the LLM provide a strong optimization signal for test-time improvement over large discrete output spaces; Duel-Evolve demonstrates the viability of reward-free, pairwise-guided evolutionary search for LLM outputs.

Abstract: Many applications seek to optimize LLM outputs at test time by iteratively proposing, scoring, and refining candidates over a discrete output space. Existing methods use a calibrated scalar evaluator for the target objective to guide search, but for many tasks such scores are unavailable, too sparse, or unreliable. Pairwise comparisons, by contrast, are often easier to elicit, still provide useful signal on improvement directions, and can be obtained from the LLM itself without external supervision. Building on this observation, we introduce Duel-Evolve, an evolutionary optimization algorithm that replaces external scalar rewards with pairwise preferences elicited from the same LLM used to generate candidates. Duel-Evolve aggregates these noisy candidate comparisons via a Bayesian Bradley-Terry model, yielding uncertainty-aware estimates of candidate quality. These quality estimates guide allocation of the comparison budget toward plausible optima using Double Thompson Sampling, as well as selection of high-quality parents to generate improved candidates. We evaluate Duel-Evolve on MathBench, where it achieves 20 percentage points higher accuracy over existing methods and baselines, and on LiveCodeBench, where it improves over comparable iterative methods by over 12 percentage points. Notably, the method requires no reward model, no ground-truth labels during search, and no hand-crafted scoring function. Results show that pairwise self-preferences provide strong optimization signal for test-time improvement over large, discrete output spaces.

</details>


### [292] [Forecasting Antimicrobial Resistance Trends Using Machine Learning on WHO GLASS Surveillance Data: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Approach for Policy Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22673)
*Md Tanvir Hasan Turja*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-component framework forecasts AMR trends using GLASS data with six ML models; XGBoost yields best accuracy; prior-year resistance is the key predictor; a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline provides source-attributed policy answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AMR is a growing global health crisis. Forecasting population-level resistance trends from standardized GLASS data can guide policy, but few studies apply machine learning for this task.

Method: Benchmark six models (Naive, Linear Regression, Ridge, XGBoost, LightGBM, LSTM) on 5,909 GLASS observations across six WHO regions (2021–2023); evaluate with MAE and R^2; perform feature-importance analysis; develop a RAG system with ChromaDB and Phi-3 Mini for policy guidance; provide code/data publicly.

Result: XGBoost achieved the best test MAE of 7.07% and R^2 of 0.854, outperforming naive baseline by 83.1%. Prior-year resistance rate is the dominant predictor (50.5% importance). Regional MAE ranges 4.16% (Europe) to 10.14% (South-East Asia). A RAG pipeline with policy documents was implemented for source-attributed, hallucination-constrained policy answers.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective short-term AMR trend forecasting from GLASS data, with interpretable drivers and policy-support via RAG; code/data are publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a growing global crisis projected to cause 10 million deaths per year by 2050. While the WHO Global Antimicrobial Resistance and Use Surveillance System (GLASS) provides standardized surveillance data across 44 countries, few studies have applied machine learning to forecast population-level resistance trends from this data. This paper presents a two-component framework for AMR trend forecasting and evidence-grounded policy decision support. We benchmark six models -- Naive, Linear Regression, Ridge Regression, XGBoost, LightGBM, and LSTM -- on 5,909 WHO GLASS observations across six WHO regions (2021-2023). XGBoost achieved the best performance with a test MAE of 7.07% and R-squared of 0.854, outperforming the naive baseline by 83.1%. Feature importance analysis identified the prior-year resistance rate as the dominant predictor (50.5% importance), while regional MAE ranged from 4.16% (European Region) to 10.14% (South-East Asia Region). We additionally implemented a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline combining a ChromaDB vector store of WHO policy documents with a locally deployed Phi-3 Mini language model, producing source-attributed, hallucination-constrained policy answers. Code and data are available at https://github.com/TanvirTurja

</details>


### [293] [Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22681)
*Shuchen Zhu,Rizhen Hu,Mingze Wang,Mou Sun,Xue Wang,Kun Yuan,Zaiwen Wen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LITE introduces a Riemannian-geometry-inspired acceleration for adaptive optimizers in LLN pre-training, leveraging larger Hessian damping along flat directions to speed up convergence compared to Muon and SOAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-training LLMs is extremely resource-intensive; optimization is highly anisotropic with slow progress along flat directions. Existing matrix-based optimizers underutilize curvature information and tend toward isotropy, limiting efficiency.

Method: Develop a unified Riemannian ODE framework to reveal how preconditioners shape geometry and how momentum acts as Riemannian damping. Propose LITE, applying increased Hessian damping and larger learning rates along flat trajectories to accelerate training. Validate across Muon/SOAP, architectures (Dense, MoE), scales (130M–1.3B), datasets (C4, Pile), and schedulers (cosine, warmup-stable-decay).

Result: LITE significantly accelerates training with Muon and SOAP across diverse settings, confirmed both empirically and theoretically to enhance convergence along flat directions in anisotropic loss landscapes.

Conclusion: A principled, geometry-inspired acceleration scheme (LITE) improves efficiency of LLM pre-training by exploiting anisotropy in curvature; provides a generalized framework for faster convergence through tailored Hessian damping and learning-rate adjustments.

Abstract: Pre-training Large Language Models requires immense computational resources, making optimizer efficiency essential. The optimization landscape is highly anisotropic, with loss reduction driven predominantly by progress along flat directions. While matrix-based optimizers such as Muon and SOAP leverage fine-grained curvature information to outperform AdamW, their updates tend toward isotropy -- relatively conservative along flat directions yet potentially aggressive along sharp ones. To address this limitation, we first establish a unified Riemannian Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) framework that elucidates how common adaptive algorithms operate synergistically: the preconditioner induces a Riemannian geometry that mitigates ill-conditioning, while momentum serves as a Riemannian damping term that promotes convergence. Guided by these insights, we propose LITE, a generalized acceleration strategy that enhances training dynamics by applying larger Hessian damping coefficients and learning rates along flat trajectories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LITE significantly accelerates both Muon and SOAP across diverse architectures (Dense, MoE), parameter scales (130M--1.3B), datasets (C4, Pile), and learning-rate schedules (cosine, warmup-stable-decay). Theoretical analysis confirms that LITE facilitates faster convergence along flat directions in anisotropic landscapes, providing a principled approach to efficient LLM pre-training. The code is available at https://github.com/SHUCHENZHU/LITE.

</details>


### [294] [Switch-Hurdle: A MoE Encoder with AR Hurdle Decoder for Intermittent Demand Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22685)
*Fabian Muşat,Simona Căbuz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Switch-Hurdle combines a sparse Mixture-of-Experts encoder with a hurdle-based probabilistic decoder that separately models sale occurrence and quantity, yielding state-of-the-art intermittent-demand forecasts with scalable training on large sparse series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intermittent demand features long runs of zeros punctuated by sporadic non-zero values. Traditional continuous-demand models and many neural approaches underperform because they treat demand as a single process and scale poorly when forecasting many sparse series. A model that explicitly handles occurrence (zero vs non-zero) and magnitude, while remaining scalable, is highly desirable.

Method: Encoder uses sparse Top-1 MoE routing during the forward pass and remains approximately dense in the backward pass via a straight-through estimator (STE). The decoder is cross-attention autoregressive with a shared hurdle head, comprising two components: (i) a binary classifier estimating the probability of a sale and (ii) a conditional regression predicting the quantity given a sale. The model is trained end-to-end and evaluated on the M5 benchmark and a large proprietary retail dataset.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art prediction performance on the M5 benchmark and on a large proprietary retail dataset, with favorable scalability due to the MoE/Hurdle design and the split occurrence/magnitude modeling.

Conclusion: Switch-Hurdle demonstrates that decoupling demand occurrence from demand magnitude via a sparse MoE encoder and a hurdle-based decoder yields superior accuracy and scalable inference for intermittent demand forecasting; the approach is effective on standard benchmarks and large real-world data.

Abstract: Intermittent demand, a pattern characterized by long sequences of zero sales punctuated by sporadic, non-zero values, poses a persistent challenge in retail and supply chain forecasting. Both traditional methods, such as ARIMA, exponential smoothing, or Croston variants, as well as modern neural architectures such as DeepAR and Transformer-based models often underperform on such data, as they treat demand as a single continuous process or become computationally expensive when scaled across many sparse series. To address these limitations, we introduce Switch-Hurdle: a new framework that integrates a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) encoder with a Hurdle-based probabilistic decoder. The encoder uses a sparse Top-1 expert routing during the forward pass yet approximately dense in the backward pass via a straight-through estimator (STE). The decoder follows a cross-attention autoregressive design with a shared hurdle head that explicitly separates the forecasting task into two components: a binary classification component estimating the probability of a sale, and a conditional regression component, predicting the quantity given a sale. This structured separation enables the model to capture both occurrence and magnitude processes inherent to intermittent demand. Empirical results on the M5 benchmark and a large proprietary retail dataset show that Switch-Hurdle achieves state-of-the-art prediction performance while maintaining scalability.

</details>


### [295] [Enhancing Geometric Perception in VLMs via Translator-Guided Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22703)
*Hao Yu,Shuning Jia,Guanghao Li,Wenhao Jiang,Chun Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeoPerceive provides a diagram-DSL benchmark and an NL-to-DSL translator-guided RL framework (GeoDPO) to boost geometric perception in vision-language models, achieving strong in-domain and out-of-domain gains and improved downstream reasoning over SFT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs struggle with geometric reasoning due to limited perception of diagram elements. A specialized benchmark and a translation-guided RL method are proposed to isolate perception from reasoning and improve it.

Method: Geometric Perception Benchmark (GeoPerceive) with diagram instances paired with domain-specific language (DSL) representations and an automatic data-generation engine. NL-to-DSL translator trained on synthetic NL-DSL pairs to bridge natural language and DSL. Translator provides fine-grained DSL-level reward signals for reinforcement learning (GeoDPO). Evaluation on in-domain/out-of-domain datasets for geometric perception and downstream reasoning.

Result: GeoDPO yields substantial gains: +26.5% in-domain, +8.0% out-of-domain, and +39.0% on downstream reasoning. Supervised fine-tuning offers marginal or negative effects in some out-of-domain cases. Code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: GeoDPO improves geometric perception and generalization over SFT, demonstrating the value of DSL-guided RL. The GeoPerceive benchmark enables isolated evaluation of perception from reasoning, and the approach generalizes to downstream reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) often struggle with geometric reasoning due to their limited perception of fundamental diagram elements. To tackle this challenge, we introduce GeoPerceive, a benchmark comprising diagram instances paired with domain-specific language (DSL) representations, along with an efficient automatic data generation pipeline. This design enables the isolated evaluation of geometric perception independently from reasoning. To exploit the data provided by GeoPerceive for enhancing the geometric perception capabilities of VLMs, we propose GeoDPO, a translator-guided reinforcement learning (RL) framework. GeoDPO employs an NL-to-DSL translator, which is trained on synthetic pairs generated by the data engine of GeoPerceive, to bridge natural language and DSL. This translator facilitates the computation of fine-grained, DSL-level scores, which serve as reward signals in reinforcement learning. We assess GeoDPO on both in-domain and out-of-domain datasets, spanning tasks in geometric perception as well as downstream reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that, while supervised fine-tuning (SFT) offers only marginal improvements and may even impair performance in out-of-domain scenarios, GeoDPO achieves substantial gains: $+26.5\%$ on in-domain data, $+8.0\%$ on out-of-domain data, and $+39.0\%$ on downstream reasoning tasks. These findings underscore the superior performance and generalization ability of GeoDPO over SFT. All codes are released at https://github.com/Longin-Yu/GeoPerceive
  to ensure reproducibility.

</details>


### [296] [Set-based v.s. Distribution-based Representations of Epistemic Uncertainty: A Comparative Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22747)
*Kaizheng Wang,Yunjia Wang,Fabio Cuzzolin,David Moens,Hans Hallez,Siu Lun Chau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Controlled, like-for-like comparison of distribution-based and credal-set second-order uncertainty representations using the same predictive distributions from a shared neural network; evaluated on multiple uncertainty measures, benchmarks, models, and runs; finds feasible and informative comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify whether distribution-based and credal-set representations are comparable by removing predictive model variability and other confounds; assess practical impact of representation choice on uncertainty-aware tasks.

Method: Construct both representations from a single finite collection of predictive distributions derived from a shared neural network; evaluate across 3 uncertainty measures, 8 benchmarks (including selective prediction and OOD detection), 6 underlying predictive models, and 10 random runs per configuration.

Result: Demonstrates that principled, like-for-like comparison is feasible and informative; reveals insights into how second-order representation choice affects practical performance.

Conclusion: Supports the viability of cross-framework evaluation for epistemic uncertainty representations and highlights the importance of controlled experimental design to disentangle representational effects from predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Epistemic uncertainty in neural networks is commonly modeled using two second-order paradigms: distribution-based representations, which rely on posterior parameter distributions, and set-based representations based on credal sets (convex sets of probability distributions). These frameworks are often regarded as fundamentally non-comparable due to differing semantics, assumptions, and evaluation practices, leaving their relative merits unclear. Empirical comparisons are further confounded by variations in the underlying predictive models. To clarify this issue, we present a controlled comparative study enabling principled, like-for-like evaluation of the two paradigms. Both representations are constructed from the same finite collection of predictive distributions generated by a shared neural network, isolating representational effects from predictive accuracy. Our study evaluates each representation through the lens of 3 uncertainty measures across 8 benchmarks, including selective prediction and out-of-distribution detection, spanning 6 underlying predictive models and 10 independent runs per configuration. Our results show that meaningful comparison between these seemingly non-comparable frameworks is both feasible and informative, providing insights into how second-order representation choices impact practical uncertainty-aware performance.

</details>


### [297] [KMLP: A Scalable Hybrid Architecture for Web-Scale Tabular Data Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22777)
*Mingming Zhang,Pengfei Shi,Zhiqing Xiao,Feng Zhao,Guandong Sun,Yulin Kang,Ruizhe Gao,Ningtao Wang,Xing Fu,Weiqiang Wang,Junbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KMLP combines a feature-wise, learnable Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) front-end with a gMLP backbone to scale deep learning for web-scale tabular data, automatically modeling per-feature nonlinearities and high-order interactions, achieving state-of-the-art results especially at large data scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web-scale tabular data face scalability and heterogeneity challenges (anisotropy, heavy tails, non-stationarity) that hinder traditional models like Gradient Boosting and require manual feature engineering.

Method: A hybrid architecture where a shallow KAN front-end learns per-feature nonlinear transformations via learnable activations, feeding a gated MLP (gMLP) backbone that captures high-order interactions; evaluated on public benchmarks and a billions-sample industrial dataset.

Result: KMLP attains state-of-the-art performance, with relative advantages over GBDTs that grow with data scale, demonstrating scalable deep learning applicability to large-scale web tabular data.

Conclusion: KMLP provides a scalable, effective deep-learning paradigm for large-scale tabular data, potentially reducing manual feature engineering and improving performance as data size increases.

Abstract: Predictive modeling on web-scale tabular data with billions of instances and hundreds of heterogeneous numerical features faces significant scalability challenges. These features exhibit anisotropy, heavy-tailed distributions, and non-stationarity, creating bottlenecks for models like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees and requiring laborious manual feature engineering. We introduce KMLP, a hybrid deep architecture integrating a shallow Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) front-end with a Gated Multilayer Perceptron (gMLP) backbone. The KAN front-end uses learnable activation functions to automatically model complex non-linear transformations for each feature, while the gMLP backbone captures high-order interactions. Experiments on public benchmarks and an industrial dataset with billions of samples show KMLP achieves state-of-the-art performance, with advantages over baselines like GBDTs increasing at larger scales, validating KMLP as a scalable deep learning paradigm for large-scale web tabular data.

</details>


### [298] [Multi-agent imitation learning with function approximation: Linear Markov games and beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22810)
*Luca Viano,Till Freihaut,Emanuele Nevali,Volkan Cevher,Matthieu Geist,Giorgia Ramponi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical and interactive MAIL framework in linear Markov games; introduces feature-level concentrability and achieves d-dimensional sample complexity; demonstrates deep MAIL improves over BC in canonical games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the high state-action policy deviation concentrability in imitation learning by exploiting linear structure and feature representations, enabling tighter theory and scalable algorithms.

Method: Theoretical analysis of multi-agent imitation learning (MAIL) in linear Markov games; replace state-action concentrability with feature-level concentrability; develop a computationally efficient interactive MAIL algorithm with sample complexity depending on feature dimension d; extend to a deep MAIL interactive method.

Result: Feature-level concentrability can be substantially smaller than the state-action analogue; interactive MAIL achieves sample complexity that scales with the feature dimension d; deep MAIL materially outperforms behavioral cloning on Tic-Tac-Toe and Connect4.

Conclusion: The work provides a theoretically tighter, scalable MAIL framework via feature-level analysis and interactive learning; offers practical algorithms with competitive performance; applicability beyond linear settings and broader generalization remain potential directions.

Abstract: In this work, we present the first theoretical analysis of multi-agent imitation learning (MAIL) in linear Markov games where both the transition dynamics and each agent's reward function are linear in some given features. We demonstrate that by leveraging this structure, it is possible to replace the state-action level "all policy deviation concentrability coefficient" (Freihaut et al., arXiv:2510.09325) with a concentrability coefficient defined at the feature level which can be much smaller than the state-action analog when the features are informative about states' similarity. Furthermore, to circumvent the need for any concentrability coefficient, we turn to the interactive setting. We provide the first, computationally efficient, interactive MAIL algorithm for linear Markov games and show that its sample complexity depends only on the dimension of the feature map $d$. Building on these theoretical findings, we propose a deep MAIL interactive algorithm which clearly outperforms BC on games such as Tic-Tac-Toe and Connect4.

</details>


### [299] [Accelerating Local LLMs on Resource-Constrained Edge Devices via Distributed Prompt Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22812)
*Hiroki Matsutani,Naoki Matsuda,Naoto Sugiura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distributed prompt caching with partial matching and a Bloom-filter catalog to suppress unnecessary inter-device communication; yields substantial TTFT and TTLT reductions on edge hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge devices' local LLM inference is bottlenecked by latency and limited resources; cooperative caching aims to accelerate inference.

Method: Cooperative distributed prompt caching across low-end devices; partial matching to exploit prompt similarity; Bloom-filter-based catalog to check state availability and reduce communication.

Result: On Gemma-3 270M and MMLU tasks, Raspberry Pi Zero 2W shows TTFT decrease of 93.12% and TTLT decrease of 50.07% on average.

Conclusion: The approach effectively speeds up edge LLM inference by combining caching, partial matching, and communication suppression, though it introduces catalog management and potential Bloom filter false positives; performance gains observed on constrained hardware.

Abstract: Since local LLM inference on resource-constrained edge devices imposes a severe performance bottleneck, this paper proposes distributed prompt caching to enhance inference performance by cooperatively sharing intermediate processing states across multiple low-end edge devices. To fully utilize prompt similarity, our distributed caching mechanism also supports partial matching. As this approach introduces communication overhead associated with state sharing over a wireless network, we introduce a Bloom-filter-based data structure, referred to as a catalog, to determine whether a remote server possesses the desired internal states, thereby suppressing unnecessary communication. Experiments using the Gemma-3 270M model and the MMLU dataset on the Raspberry Pi Zero 2W platform demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces TTFT (Time to First Token) and TTLT (Time to Last Token) by 93.12% and 50.07% on average, respectively.

</details>


### [300] [Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22817)
*Shuo He,Lang Feng,Qi Wei,Xin Cheng,Lei Feng,Bo An*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HGPO introduces a Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization to improve stepwise advantage estimation in long-horizon agentic RL by addressing context inconsistency; it uses multiple hierarchical groups within a rollout to compute group-specific advantages with adaptive weighting, achieving better bias-variance trade-off without extra data and showing strong gains on ALFWorld and WebShop with large LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stepwise group-based policy optimization suffers from context inconsistency: steps in the same group may have different historical contexts, biasing advantage estimates and harming policy updates. A method to stabilize stepwise advantages without extra models or rollouts is needed for long-horizon agentic tasks.

Method: HGPO partitions a group of rollout trajectories into multiple hierarchical groups based on historical-context consistency. Each step is assigned to several groups; advantages are computed within each group for that step and then adaptively combined with weights. This yields a more robust, bias-variance trade-off in stepwise advantage estimation without additional data or models.

Result: Empirical evaluations on ALFWorld and WebShop using Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct show HGPO significantly outperforms existing agentic RL methods under the same computational constraints.

Conclusion: HGPO effectively mitigates context-induced bias in stepwise advantage estimation, offering a simple yet powerful approach to improve long-horizon agentic RL without extra data or models, with demonstrated gains on challenging tasks.

Abstract: Group-based reinforcement learning (RL), such as GRPO, has advanced the capabilities of large language models on long-horizon agentic tasks. To enable more fine-grained policy updates, recent research has increasingly shifted toward stepwise group-based policy optimization, which treats each step in a rollout trajectory independently while using a memory module to retain historical context. However, we find a key issue in estimating stepwise relative advantages, namely context inconsistency, where steps within the same group may differ in their historical contexts. Empirically, we reveal that this issue can lead to severely biased advantage estimation, thereby degrading policy optimization significantly. To address the issue, in this paper, we propose Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization (HGPO) for long-horizon agentic tasks. Specifically, within a group of rollout trajectories, HGPO assigns each step to multiple hierarchical groups according to the consistency of historical contexts. Then, for each step, HGPO computes distinct advantages within each group and aggregates them with an adaptive weighting scheme. In this way, HGPO can achieve a favorable bias-variance trade-off in stepwise advantage estimation, without extra models or rollouts. Evaluations on two challenging agentic tasks, ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, show that HGPO significantly outperforms existing agentic RL methods under the same computational constraints. Code is available at https://github.com/langfengQ/verl-agent/tree/master/recipe/hgpo.

</details>


### [301] [Hypernetwork-based approach for grid-independent functional data clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22823)
*Anirudh Thatipelli,Ali Siahkoohi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a grid-agnostic functional data clustering framework that encodes discretized function observations into a compact, grid-invariant weight space via a hypernetwork that predicts INR decoders; clusters in this learned weight space, yielding resolution-robust and generalizable clustering performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clustering functions often relies on discretization grids, causing results to depend on sampling density and preprocessing rather than the underlying functions. A need exists for a discretization-agnostic representation that preserves function structure for clustering.

Method: An auto-encoder where the encoder is a hypernetwork that maps coordinate-value pairs to the weight space of an implicit neural representation (INR) decoder. INRs provide compact, continuous-function representations. Clustering is performed in the INR weight space using standard algorithms. The approach is grid- and discretization-agnostic, with the hypernetwork amortizing weight prediction across the dataset.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world high-dimensional data show competitive clustering performance that is robust to changes in sampling resolution and generalizes to resolutions not seen during training.

Conclusion: The framework decouples clustering from discretization, enabling resolution-robust functional clustering. It leverages INRs for compact function representation and a hypernetwork to amortize encoding, yielding an effective, flexible clustering pipeline across arbitrary grids.

Abstract: Functional data clustering is concerned with grouping functions that share similar structure, yet most existing methods implicitly operate on sampled grids, causing cluster assignments to depend on resolution, sampling density, or preprocessing choices rather than on the underlying functions themselves. To address this limitation, we introduce a framework that maps discretized function observations -- at arbitrary resolution and on arbitrary grids -- into a fixed-dimensional vector space via an auto-encoding architecture. The encoder is a hypernetwork that maps coordinate-value pairs to the weight space of an implicit neural representation (INR), which serves as the decoder. Because INRs represent functions with very few parameters, this design yields compact representations that are decoupled from the sampling grid, while the hypernetwork amortizes weight prediction across the dataset. Clustering is then performed in this weight space using standard algorithms, making the approach agnostic to both the discretization and the choice of clustering method. By means of synthetic and real-world experiments in high-dimensional settings, we demonstrate competitive clustering performance that is robust to changes in sampling resolution -- including generalization to resolutions not seen during training.

</details>


### [302] [Moral Preferences of LLMs Under Directed Contextual Influence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22831)
*Phil Blandfort,Tushar Karayil,Urja Pawar,Robert Graham,Alex McKenzie,Dmitrii Krasheninnikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Directed contextual cues reshape moral decisions in trolley-style triage; pilot harness reveals steerability and limits of baseline neutrality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarking LLM moral judgments often assumes stable preferences; deployment prompts include contextual signals that may bias decisions; need to understand how these cues steer behavior.

Method: Pilot evaluation harness applying matched, direction-flipped contextual influences for each demographic factor; measures directional response; analyzes baseline vs influenced decisions; examines effects of reasoning and few-shot cues on steerability.

Result: Contextual influences significantly shift decisions even when superficially unrelated; baseline preferences poorly predict steerability; influences can backfire, with neutrality claims not preventing shifts; reasoning reduces average sensitivity but amplifies effects of biased few-shot examples.

Conclusion: Moral evaluations should be extended with controlled, direction-flipped contextual manipulations to better characterize model behavior and steerability.

Abstract: Moral benchmarks for LLMs typically use context-free prompts, implicitly assuming stable preferences. In deployment, however, prompts routinely include contextual signals such as user requests, cues on social norms, etc. that may steer decisions. We study how directed contextual influences reshape decisions in trolley-problem-style moral triage settings. We introduce a pilot evaluation harness for directed contextual influence in trolley-problem-style moral triage: for each demographic factor, we apply matched, direction-flipped contextual influences that differ only in which group they favor, enabling systematic measurement of directional response. We find that: (i) contextual influences often significantly shift decisions, even when only superficially relevant; (ii) baseline preferences are a poor predictor of directional steerability, as models can appear baseline-neutral yet exhibit systematic steerability asymmetry under influence; (iii) influences can backfire: models may explicitly claim neutrality or discount the contextual cue, yet their choices still shift, sometimes in the opposite direction; and (iv) reasoning reduces average sensitivity, but amplifies the effect of biased few-shot examples. Our findings motivate extending moral evaluations with controlled, direction-flipped context manipulations to better characterize model behavior.

</details>


### [303] [Decentralized Ranking Aggregation: Gossip Algorithms for Borda and Copeland Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22847)
*Anna Van Elst,Kerrian Le Caillec,Igor Colin,Stephan Clémençon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decentralized gossip-based consensus for ranking aggregation with convergence guarantees for Borda, Copeland, and median-based rules; validated theoretically and empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ranking aggregation has been studied in centralized settings, but in distributed networks data are dispersed across nodes. There is a need for reliable, scalable consensus on rankings using only local communications. Existing decentralized work largely targets arithmetic means; extending to consensus on rankings raises robustness and communication challenges.

Method: Random gossip communication among autonomous agents enabling local interactions to reach global ranking consensus. The paper provides rigorous convergence guarantees with explicit rate bounds for Borda and Copeland. It also delivers a decentralized implementation for the median rank rule and local Kemenization, with extensive empirical evaluations across network topologies and real/synthetic ranking datasets.

Result: Proven convergence with explicit rate bounds for Borda and Copeland rules; decentralized median/rank rules and Kemenization implemented. Empirical results show fast, reliable convergence to the correct ranking across diverse networks and datasets, confirming scalability and reduced communication costs.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that decentralized ranking consensus is feasible with provable guarantees via gossip-based methods. Raises questions on robustness to corrupted nodes and scalability, while showing practical performance gains for distributed systems such as P2P, IoT, and multi-agent networks.

Abstract: The concept of ranking aggregation plays a central role in preference analysis, and numerous algorithms for calculating median rankings, often originating in social choice theory, have been documented in the literature, offering theoretical guarantees in a centralized setting, i.e., when all the ranking data to be aggregated can be brought together in a single computing unit. For many technologies (e.g. peer-to-peer networks, IoT, multi-agent systems), extending the ability to calculate consensus rankings with guarantees in a decentralized setting, i.e., when preference data is initially distributed across a communicating network, remains a major methodological challenge. Indeed, in recent years, the literature on decentralized computation has mainly focused on computing or optimizing statistics such as arithmetic means using gossip algorithms. The purpose of this article is precisely to study how to achieve reliable consensus on collective rankings using classical rules (e.g. Borda, Copeland) in a decentralized setting, thereby raising new questions, robustness to corrupted nodes, and scalability through reduced communication costs in particular. The approach proposed and analyzed here relies on random gossip communication, allowing autonomous agents to compute global ranking consensus using only local interactions, without coordination or central authority.
  We provide rigorous convergence guarantees, including explicit rate bounds, for the Borda and Copeland consensus methods. Beyond these rules, we also provide a decentralized implementation of consensus according to the median rank rule and local Kemenization. Extensive empirical evaluations on various network topologies and real and synthetic ranking datasets demonstrate that our algorithms converge quickly and reliably to the correct ranking aggregation.

</details>


### [304] [MEDNA-DFM: A Dual-View FiLM-MoE Model for Explainable DNA Methylation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22850)
*Yi He,Yina Cao,Jixiu Zhai,Di Wang,Junxiao Kong,Tianchi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MEDNA-DFM, an explainable deep learning model for DNA methylation detection, paired with mechanism-inspired signal purification, achieving cross-species robustness by relying on conserved motifs; enables reliable motif extraction and a sequence-structure synergy hypothesis validated in silico.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the black-box nature of deep learning in methylation detection and enable biologically interpretable insights, while achieving robust cross-species generalization by exploiting conserved intrinsic motifs rather than phylogenetic proximity.

Method: Develop MEDNA-DFM, a high-performance DL architecture complemented by mechanism-inspired signal purification algorithms; validate on external independent datasets; extract motifs with higher reliability than prior work; perform in silico mutagenesis to test hypotheses.

Result: Model distinguishes methylation patterns robustly across diverse species; generalization driven by conserved intrinsic motifs (e.g., GC content) rather than phylogeny; Bray motifs extracted with higher reliability than prior studies; Drosophila 6mA case supports a sequence-structure synergy; ablation of core motif components degrades recognition.

Conclusion: Explainable DL can drive methodological innovation and biologically testable hypotheses, offering a powerful methylation predictor and advancing motif-based mechanistic understanding.

Abstract: Accurate computational identification of DNA methylation is essential for understanding epigenetic regulation. Although deep learning excels in this binary classification task, its "black-box" nature impedes biological insight. We address this by introducing a high-performance model MEDNA-DFM, alongside mechanism-inspired signal purification algorithms. Our investigation demonstrates that MEDNA-DFM effectively captures conserved methylation patterns, achieving robust distinction across diverse species. Validation on external independent datasets confirms that the model's generalization is driven by conserved intrinsic motifs (e.g., GC content) rather than phylogenetic proximity. Furthermore, applying our developed algorithms extracted motifs with significantly higher reliability than prior studies. Finally, empirical evidence from a Drosophila 6mA case study prompted us to propose a "sequence-structure synergy" hypothesis, suggesting that the GAGG core motif and an upstream A-tract element function cooperatively. We further validated this hypothesis via in silico mutagenesis, confirming that the ablation of either or both elements significantly degrades the model's recognition capabilities. This work provides a powerful tool for methylation prediction and demonstrates how explainable deep learning can drive both methodological innovation and the generation of biological hypotheses.

</details>


### [305] [Fair feature attribution for multi-output prediction: a Shapley-based perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22882)
*Umberto Biccari,Alain Ibáñez de Opakua,José María Mato,Óscar Millet,Roberto Morales,Enrique Zuazua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Shapley axioms to vector-valued predictors, proving a rigidity result that forces component-wise attribution under standard axioms; joint-output attributions must violate at least one axiom; empirical results show computational savings and consistency with component-wise SHAP for multi-output models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how to attribute feature importance in multi-output models within the Shapley framework and identify structural constraints on fairness-consistent explanations.

Method: Extend classical Shapley axioms (efficiency, symmetry, dummy, additivity) to vector-valued cooperative games; prove a rigidity theorem enforcing component-wise decomposition; analyze implications for joint-output attributions; validate with numerical experiments on a biomedical benchmark.

Result: Any attribution rule satisfying efficiency, symmetry, dummy, and additivity decomposes the attribution across outputs. Therefore, joint-output attribution rules must relax at least one of the axioms. Empirical results show potential computational savings in training/deployment for multi-output models and SHAP explanations remain consistent with the component-wise structure.

Conclusion: The work formalizes a structural constraint on Shapley-based explanations in multi-output settings, delineating the exact scope of fair, per-output attributions and indicating that joint attributions require axiom relaxation; practical SHAP explanations can be computed consistently in a component-wise fashion with observed computational benefits.

Abstract: In this article, we provide an axiomatic characterization of feature attribution for multi-output predictors within the Shapley framework. While SHAP explanations are routinely computed independently for each output coordinate, the theoretical necessity of this practice has remained unclear. By extending the classical Shapley axioms to vector-valued cooperative games, we establish a rigidity theorem showing that any attribution rule satisfying efficiency, symmetry, dummy player, and additivity must necessarily decompose component-wise across outputs. Consequently, any joint-output attribution rule must relax at least one of the classical Shapley axioms. This result identifies a previously unformalized structural constraint in Shapley-based interpretability, clarifying the precise scope of fairness-consistent explanations in multi-output learning. Numerical experiments on a biomedical benchmark illustrate that multi-output models can yield computational savings in training and deployment, while producing SHAP explanations that remain fully consistent with the component-wise structure imposed by the Shapley axioms.

</details>


### [306] [A Data-Driven Approach to Support Clinical Renal Replacement Therapy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22902)
*Alice Balboni,Luis Escobar,Andrea Manno,Fabrizio Rossi,Maria Cristina Ruffa,Gianluca Villa,Giordano D'Aloisio,Antonio Consolo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabularML with oversampling achieves strong, interpretable predictions of CRRT membrane fouling, outperforming LSTM and enabling counterfactual insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predict membrane fouling in CRRT to enable timely clinical decisions while ensuring model interpretability and actionable counterfactual explanations.

Method: Use ICU time-series data but frame as tabular with 16 selected features; apply ADASYN oversampling at 10% rebalancing; train Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM; compare to LSTM; perform feature selection to 5 features; apply Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis on the best model.

Result: Sensitivity 77.6% and specificity 96.3% at 10% rebalancing; robust across forecasting horizons; tabular models outperform LSTM; reduced feature set with minimal accuracy loss; counterfactuals identify minimal input changes to reverse fouling predictions.

Conclusion: Shows viability of interpretable ML with counterfactual analysis for predicting membrane fouling in CRRT; integration of prediction and counterfactuals has clinical value for guiding therapy and management.

Abstract: This study investigates a data-driven machine learning approach to predict membrane fouling in critically ill patients undergoing Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT). Using time-series data from an ICU, 16 clinically selected features were identified to train predictive models. To ensure interpretability and enable reliable counterfactual analysis, the researchers adopted a tabular data approach rather than modeling temporal dependencies directly. Given the imbalance between fouling and non-fouling cases, the ADASYN oversampling technique was applied to improve minority class representation. Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM models were tested, achieving balanced performance with 77.6% sensitivity and 96.3% specificity at a 10% rebalancing rate. Results remained robust across different forecasting horizons. Notably, the tabular approach outperformed LSTM recurrent neural networks, suggesting that explicit temporal modeling was not necessary for strong predictive performance. Feature selection further reduced the model to five key variables, improving simplicity and interpretability with minimal loss of accuracy. A Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis was applied to the best-performing model, successfully identifying minimal input changes capable of reversing fouling predictions. Overall, the findings support the viability of interpretable machine learning models for predicting membrane fouling during CRRT. The integration of prediction and counterfactual analysis offers practical clinical value, potentially guiding therapeutic adjustments to reduce fouling risk and improve patient management.

</details>


### [307] [NoRA: Breaking the Linear Ceiling of Low-Rank Adaptation via Manifold Expansion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22911)
*Hung-Hsuan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NoRA introduces a non-linear rank adaptation mechanism via SiLU gating and structural dropout in a weight-level adapter, enabling manifold expansion that overcomes LoRA's linear ceiling; empirically, NoRA at rank 64 matches or surpasses LoRA at higher ranks on SlimOrca and MathInstruct, with SVD analysis showing activation of the singular-value tail and reduced rank collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA achieves parameter-efficient fine-tuning but exhibits a linear scaling ceiling in complex reasoning tasks; simply increasing rank yields diminishing returns due to intrinsic linear constraints. A non-linear, rank-adaptive mechanism could expand the effective optimization manifold.

Method: NoRA is a weight-level parallel adapter that injects SiLU gating and structural dropout to induce non-linear interactions and manifold expansion. The approach aims to activate previously dormant regions of the parameter space and expand the effective rank without a proportional increase in parameters. Spectral analysis via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is used to diagnose tail activation of the singular-value spectrum.

Result: On SlimOrca, NoRA at rank 64 (PPL 3.89) outperforms LoRA at rank 512 (PPL 3.90), showing superior spectral efficiency. In mathematical reasoning, NoRA achieves perplexity 1.97 on MathInstruct, notably better than LoRA’s saturation at 2.07. SVD confirms NoRA activates the dormant tail of the singular value spectrum, mitigating rank-collapse observed in linear methods.

Conclusion: NoRA overcomes the linear scaling barrier of LoRA by non-linearly expanding the optimization manifold, yielding improved spectral efficiency and stronger performance on complex reasoning tasks. The SVD-based mechanism analysis supports the observed empirical gains. Further work could explore broader benchmarks, ablations on gating/dropout configurations, and trade-offs in computation or training dynamics.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) dominates parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). However, it faces a critical ``linear ceiling'' in complex reasoning tasks: simply increasing the rank yields diminishing returns due to intrinsic linear constraints. We introduce NoRA (Non-linear Rank Adaptation), a weight-level parallel adapter that injects SiLU gating and structural dropout to induce manifold expansion. On the SlimOrca benchmark, NoRA breaks this linear barrier: NoRA remarkably at rank 64 (PPL 3.89) outperforms LoRA at rank 512 (PPL 3.90), demonstrating superior spectral efficiency. This advantage generalizes to mathematical reasoning, where NoRA achieves a perplexity of 1.97 on MathInstruct, significantly surpassing LoRA's saturation point of 2.07. Mechanism analysis via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) confirms that NoRA activates the dormant tail of the singular value spectrum, effectively preventing the rank collapse observed in linear methods.

</details>


### [308] [Generalization Bounds of Stochastic Gradient Descent in Homogeneous Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22936)
*Wenquan Ma,Yang Sui,Jiaye Teng,Bohan Wang,Jing Xu,Jingqin Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shows that for homogeneous neural networks, algorithmic stability-based generalization bounds permit a slower stepsize decay, Ω(1/√t) instead of the conventional O(1/t), and extends to non-Lipschitz regimes, applicable to ReLU/LeakyReLU architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional stability-based generalization analyses for non-convex training commonly assume a stepsize decay of O(1/t), which can impede optimization. This work seeks to relax this decay under the homogeneous network regime, broadening applicability to practical settings.

Method: Derives generalization bounds via algorithmic stability within the homogeneous neural network framework, establishing a slower stepsize decay rate under mild assumptions; extends the results to non-Lipschitz regimes and demonstrates applicability to common architectures.

Result: Under homogeneous regimes, the stepsize can decay as Ω(1/√t) while preserving stability-based generalization bounds; the results extend to non-Lipschitz cases and apply to fully-connected and CNNs with ReLU/LeakyReLU activations.

Conclusion: Homogeneous neural networks admit slower learning-rate decay without sacrificing generalization guarantees, aligning theory more closely with practice and broadening the scope of stability-based analyses beyond Lipschitz assumptions.

Abstract: Algorithmic stability is among the most potent techniques in generalization analysis. However, its derivation usually requires a stepsize $η_t = \mathcal{O}(1/t)$ under non-convex training regimes, where $t$ denotes iterations. This rigid decay of the stepsize potentially impedes optimization and may not align with practical scenarios. In this paper, we derive the generalization bounds under the homogeneous neural network regimes, proving that this regime enables slower stepsize decay of order $Ω(1/\sqrt{t})$ under mild assumptions. We further extend the theoretical results from several aspects, e.g., non-Lipschitz regimes. This finding is broadly applicable, as homogeneous neural networks encompass fully-connected and convolutional neural networks with ReLU and LeakyReLU activations.

</details>


### [309] [MSINO: Curvature-Aware Sobolev Optimization for Manifold Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22937)
*Suresan Pareth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSINO is a curvature-aware Sobolev training framework for neural nets on Riemannian manifolds that replaces Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss, uses parallel transport and Laplace-Beltrami regularization, and provides geometry-dependent convergence guarantees including a Descent Lemma, Sobolev PL inequality, and a two-step Newton method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable stable, principled learning for neural networks defined on manifolds by incorporating intrinsic geometry into optimization, addressing limitations of Euclidean Sobolev training and exploiting curvature information to obtain convergence guarantees and robust performance in geometry-rich domains.

Method: Replace Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss, align gradients via parallel transport, and enforce Laplace-Beltrami smoothness. Derive geometry-dependent constants yielding (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak–Łojasiewicz inequality granting linear convergence for Riemannian gradient descent and SGD under explicit step-size bounds, and (iii) a two-step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature-controlled neighborhoods. Train-time guarantees explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics-informed learning, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3).

Result: The framework provides linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent/SGD via the Sobolev PL inequality, a two-step Newton method with local quadratic contraction, and explicit training-time guarantees that account for curvature and transported Jacobians; it unifies value- and gradient-based learning on manifolds and broadens applicability to manifolds and Lie groups.

Conclusion: MSINO provides a curvature-aware, geometrically grounded training paradigm for neural networks on manifolds, delivering convergence guarantees and a unifying framework for learning on curved spaces with practical applications in surface imaging, physics-informed learning, and robotics on Lie groups.

Abstract: We introduce Manifold Sobolev Informed Neural Optimization (MSINO), a curvature aware training framework for neural networks defined on Riemannian manifolds. The method replaces standard Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients using parallel transport and improves stability via a Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization term.
  Building on classical results in Riemannian optimization and Sobolev theory on manifolds, we derive geometry dependent constants that yield (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak Lojasiewicz inequality giving linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step size bounds, and (iii) a two step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature controlled neighborhoods.
  Unlike prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space, MSINO provides training time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics informed learning settings, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3). The framework unifies value and gradient based learning with curvature aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds.

</details>


### [310] [Scaling Laws of Global Weather Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22962)
*Yuejiang Yu,Langwen Huang,Alexandru Calotoiu,Torsten Hoefler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weather-model scaling laws show strong data scaling and a preference for wider architectures; under fixed compute, longer training beats bigger models, and scaling differs from language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how model size, dataset size, and compute budget constrain predictive accuracy in weather forecasting, enabling data- and compute-efficient model design.

Method: Empirical scaling analysis across multiple weather models, examining validation loss as a function of model size (N), dataset size (D), and compute budget (C); evaluation of training duration vs. model scale under fixed compute; analysis of model width vs. depth.

Result: Aurora shows strong data scaling: increasing training data by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast offers high parameter efficiency but limited hardware utilization. Under fixed compute, longer training yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Weather models favor increased width over depth, with scaling behavior distinct from language models. Implication: prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

Conclusion: Future weather models should emphasize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets, with compute allocations favoring longer training runs over simply increasing model size to maximize predictive performance.

Abstract: Data-driven models are revolutionizing weather forecasting. To optimize training efficiency and model performance, this paper analyzes empirical scaling laws within this domain. We investigate the relationship between model performance (validation loss) and three key factors: model size ($N$), dataset size ($D$), and compute budget ($C$). Across a range of models, we find that Aurora exhibits the strongest data-scaling behavior: increasing the training dataset by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast demonstrates the highest parameter efficiency, yet suffers from limited hardware utilization. Our compute-optimal analysis indicates that, under fixed compute budgets, allocating resources to longer training durations yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Furthermore, we analyze model shape and uncover scaling behaviors that differ fundamentally from those observed in language models: weather forecasting models consistently favor increased width over depth. These findings suggest that future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

</details>


### [311] [Exploratory Memory-Augmented LLM Agent via Hybrid On- and Off-Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23008)
*Zeyuan Liu,Jeonghye Kim,Xufang Luo,Dongsheng Li,Yuqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A memory-augmented, hybrid RL framework (EMPO^2) improves exploration and generalization for LLM agents by integrating memory for exploration with on- and off-policy updates; it outperforms GRPO on ScienceWorld and WebShop and shows rapid, mem-augmented adaptability to new tasks without parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning suffer from exploration bottlenecks, especially in environments requiring discovery of novel states; improved exploratory capabilities and generalization are needed.

Method: EMPO^2 combines memory-augmented exploration for discovery with a hybrid on-policy and off-policy RL optimization, enabling robust performance with and without memory.

Result: Empirical gains over GRPO: 128.6% on ScienceWorld and 11.3% on WebShop. In out-of-distribution tests, EMPO^2 adapts to new tasks after only a few trials using memory and without updating model parameters.

Conclusion: EMPO^2 appears to be a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents, addressing exploration and adaptation challenges.

Abstract: Exploration remains the key bottleneck for large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning. While prior methods exploit pretrained knowledge, they fail in environments requiring the discovery of novel states. We propose Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization (EMPO$^2$), a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on- and off-policy updates to make LLMs perform well with memory while also ensuring robustness without it. On ScienceWorld and WebShop, EMPO$^2$ achieves 128.6% and 11.3% improvements over GRPO, respectively. Moreover, in out-of-distribution tests, EMPO$^2$ demonstrates superior adaptability to new tasks, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates. These results highlight EMPO$^2$ as a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

</details>


### [312] [Learning Disease-Sensitive Latent Interaction Graphs From Noisy Cardiac Flow Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23035)
*Viraj Patel,Marko Grujic,Philipp Aigner,Theodor Abart,Marcus Granegger,Deblina Bhattacharjee,Katharine Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed latent relational graph model captures inter-vortex interactions in cardiac flow, linking graph entropy to disease severity and LVAD intervention across CFD and ultrasound data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current imaging methods struggle to capture relational structures of coherent flow features in cardiac blood flow; a relational, physics-informed approach could yield interpretable markers of disease and interventions.

Method: Model combines neural relational inference with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics to treat cardiac vortices as graph nodes; first validated on CFD simulations of aortic coarctation, then on ultrasound data of left ventricles under LVAD support.

Result: Latent graphs show stronger/more frequent vortex interactions as the aorta narrows; graph entropy correlates with coarctation severity (R^2=0.78, Spearman |ρ|=0.96); cross-modal generalization observed with ultrasound data; entropy and latent interactions serve as robust, interpretable disease/intervention markers.

Conclusion: A physics-informed latent relational framework yields clinically relevant, interpretable markers of disease severity and intervention level in cardiac flows, with demonstrated cross-modal transfer between CFD and ultrasound data.

Abstract: Cardiac blood flow patterns contain rich information about disease severity and clinical interventions, yet current imaging and computational methods fail to capture underlying relational structures of coherent flow features. We propose a physics-informed, latent relational framework to model cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph. Our model combines a neural relational inference architecture with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics, yielding a latent graph sensitive to disease severity and intervention level. We first apply this to computational fluid dynamics simulations of aortic coarctation. Learned latent graphs reveal that as the aortic radius narrows, vortex interactions become stronger and more frequent. This leads to a higher graph entropy, correlating monotonically with coarctation severity ($R^2=0.78$, Spearman $|ρ|=0.96$). We then extend this method to ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under varying levels of left ventricular assist device support. Again the latent graph representation captures the weakening of coherent vortical structures, thereby demonstrating cross-modal generalisation. Results show latent interaction graphs and entropy serve as robust and interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention.

</details>


### [313] [RhythmBERT: A Self-Supervised Language Model Based on Latent Representations of ECG Waveforms for Heart Disease Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23060)
*Xin Wang,Burcu Ozek,Aruna Mohan,Amirhossein Ravari,Or Zilbershot,Fatemeh Afghah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RhythmBERT is a generative ECG language model that encodes P/QRS/T segments into discrete tokens via autoencoder latent representations, augmented with continuous morphology embeddings, pretrained with masked prediction on ~800k ECGs, achieving competitive performance with single-lead input against 12-lead baselines and generalizing across atrial fibrillation and subtle ST-T changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of treating ECG as a generic time series and using contrastive or fixed-window generative methods that distort rhythm or misalign cycles. The authors aim to incorporate rhythm-level semantics and physiologic structure by modeling ECG as a language with segment-level tokens.

Method: Encode ECG segments (P, QRS, T) into symbolic tokens using autoencoder-based latent representations; maintain complementary continuous embeddings for morphology; construct a unified token-and-embedding representation; pretrain a RhythmBERT model with a masked prediction objective on ~800k unlabeled ECGs; evaluate on single-lead input against 12-lead baselines.

Result: RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines using only a single lead; demonstrates generalization across common and clinically challenging conditions, including atrial fibrillation, subtle ST-T abnormalities, and myocardial infarction.

Conclusion: Viewing ECG as a structured language with rhythm semantics enables a scalable, physiology-aligned approach to cardiac analysis; RhythmBERT shows effective integration of rhythm tokens and morphology, offering robust, label-efficient representations for ECG interpretation.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is crucial for diagnosing heart disease, but most self-supervised learning methods treat ECG as a generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Existing contrastive methods utilize augmentations that distort morphology, whereas generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation, which misaligns cardiac cycles. To address these limitations, we propose RhythmBERT, a generative ECG language model that considers ECG as a language paradigm by encoding P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations. These discrete tokens capture rhythm semantics, while complementary continuous embeddings retain fine-grained morphology, enabling a unified view of waveform structure and rhythm. RhythmBERT is pretrained on approximately 800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings with a masked prediction objective, allowing it to learn contextual representations in a label-efficient manner. Evaluations show that despite using only a single lead, RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines. This generalization extends from prevalent conditions such as atrial fibrillation to clinically challenging cases such as subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction. Our results suggest that considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

</details>


### [314] [Physics-informed neural particle flow for the Bayesian update step](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23089)
*Domonkos Csuzdi,Tamás Bécsi,Olivér Törő*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Physics-informed neural particle flow for Bayesian updates in high dimensions: an unsupervised, amortized approach that embeds a master PDE (linking log-homotopy trajectories to the continuity equation) into the loss to learn the transport velocity, improving stability, mode coverage, and reducing online cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional nonlinear Bayesian updates are computationally challenging. Existing log-homotopy particle flows yield stiff dynamics; deep learning methods often treat the update as a black-box or rely on asymptotic relaxation, neglecting finite-horizon transport geometry.

Method: Derive a master PDE by coupling the log-homotopy prior–posterior density trajectory with the continuity equation for density evolution. Embed this PDE as a physical constraint in the loss and train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field (amortized inference) in an unsupervised manner. The neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating stiffness and reducing online cost, without ground-truth posterior samples.

Result: Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario shows improved mode coverage and robustness compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: The physics-informed neural particle flow provides a stable, unsupervised, amortized framework for finite-horizon Bayesian updates, leveraging the PDE constraint to learn transport velocities and achieve better posterior coverage with lower online complexity.

Abstract: The Bayesian update step poses significant computational challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation. While log-homotopy particle flow filters offer an alternative to stochastic sampling, existing formulations usually yield stiff differential equations. Conversely, existing deep learning approximations typically treat the update as a black-box task or rely on asymptotic relaxation, neglecting the exact geometric structure of the finite-horizon probability transport. In this work, we propose a physics-informed neural particle flow, which is an amortized inference framework. To construct the flow, we couple the log-homotopy trajectory of the prior to posterior density function with the continuity equation describing the density evolution. This derivation yields a governing partial differential equation (PDE), referred to as the master PDE. By embedding this PDE as a physical constraint into the loss function, we train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field. This approach enables purely unsupervised training, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples. We demonstrate that the neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating the numerical stiffness inherent to analytic flows and reducing online computational complexity. Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario confirms better mode coverage and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [315] [PRAC: Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression and Memory-Efficient Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23111)
*Yanyi Li,Yimu Zhang,Cong Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRAC: a two-subspace activation compression for LLMs using SVD-based principal subspace and a random orthogonal-tail subspace, yielding unbiased gradient estimates with low variance and strong memory savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activations are the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training; current compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations and often lead to slow convergence or limited compression.

Method: Decompose activations into a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. Apply a precise scaling factor to ensure the gradient estimator is unbiased with minimum variance under certain conditions.

Result: Experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks show up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

Conclusion: PRAC demonstrates that leveraging the spectral structure of activations enables effective memory compression by producing unbiased, low-variance gradient estimates, yielding practical memory savings in training large language models.

Abstract: Activations have become the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. However, existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, resulting in slow convergence or limited compression. To address this, we bridge the relationship between the algorithm's fast convergence and the requirements for subspace projection, and show that an effective compression should yield an unbiased estimate of the original activation with low variance. We propose Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression (PRAC), which novelly decomposes activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. By introducing a precise scaling factor, we prove that PRAC yields an unbiased gradient estimator with minimum variance under certain conditions. Extensive experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks demonstrate that PRAC achieves up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

</details>


### [316] [Learning Physical Operators using Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23113)
*Vignesh Gopakumar,Ander Gray,Dan Giles,Lorenzo Zanisi,Matt J. Kusner,Timo Betcke,Stanislas Pamela,Marc Peter Deisenroth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed modular neural operator framework using operator splitting and mixture-of-experts to learn nonlinear operators and fixed linear operators, cast as a neural ODE for continuous-time Navier–Stokes surrogates with improved generalization and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address generalization gaps of neural operators across unseen physics and time discretizations; enforce PDE structure and provide parameter-efficient, interpretable surrogates for fluid dynamics.

Method: Decompose PDEs via operator splitting; train separate neural operators for nonlinear terms while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions; use a mixture-of-experts to assemble operators; formulate as a neural ODE with learned operators on the RHS to enable continuous-time predictions via standard ODE solvers and implicit PDE constraints.

Result: Applied to incompressible and compressible Navier–Stokes; achieving better convergence and superior generalization to unseen physics; parameter-efficient and capable of temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons; provides interpretable components that can be verified against known physics.

Conclusion: The modular, physics-informed operator framework effectively encodes operator structure, improving generalization, interpretability, and data efficiency in neural surrogates for PDEs, with potential applicability to other PDEs beyond fluid dynamics.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as promising surrogate models for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), but struggle to generalise beyond training distributions and are often constrained to a fixed temporal discretisation. This work introduces a physics-informed training framework that addresses these limitations by decomposing PDEs using operator splitting methods, training separate neural operators to learn individual non-linear physical operators while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions. This modular mixture-of-experts architecture enables generalisation to novel physical regimes by explicitly encoding the underlying operator structure. We formulate the modelling task as a neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) where these learned operators constitute the right-hand side, enabling continuous-in-time predictions through standard ODE solvers and implicitly enforcing PDE constraints. Demonstrated on incompressible and compressible Navier-Stokes equations, our approach achieves better convergence and superior performance when generalising to unseen physics. The method remains parameter-efficient, enabling temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons, and provides interpretable components whose behaviour can be verified against known physics.

</details>
