### [317] [Regularized Online RLHF with Generalized Bilinear Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23116)
*Junghyun Lee,Minju Hong,Kwang-Sung Jun,Chulhee Yun,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study of contextual online RLHF with general preferences using GBPM; proves dual gap bound is controlled by squared estimation error under strong convexity and skew-symmetric GBPM; provides two regret bounds for simple algorithms exploiting low-rank structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend online RLHF analysis to general strongly convex regularizers and general preferences (beyond reverse KL), achieving statistically efficient guarantees in high dimensions.

Method: Model preferences with Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) using low-rank skew-symmetric matrices; establish dual gap bound from strong convexity and skew-symmetry; propose Greedy Sampling and Explore-Then-Commit leveraging feature diversity and low-rank structure.

Result: Two regret guarantees: (1) Greedy Sampling: polylog regret tilde{O}(η d^4 (log T)^2) free of e^{O(η)}; (2) Explore-Then-Commit: tilde{O}(sqrt{η r T}) regret, poly(d)-free; first statistically efficient high-dim RLHF guarantee.

Conclusion: Shows dual gap is bounded by squared estimation error under GBPM; simple algorithms achieve efficient regret in high-dimensional online RLHF with general preferences, highlighting the advantages of low-rank skew-symmetric modeling.

Abstract: We consider the problem of contextual online RLHF with general preferences, where the goal is to identify the Nash Equilibrium. We adopt the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) to capture potentially intransitive preferences via low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices. We investigate general preference learning with any strongly convex regularizer (where $η^{-1}$ is the regularization strength), generalizing beyond prior works limited to reverse KL-regularization. Central to our analysis is proving that the dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of the estimation error - a result derived solely from strong convexity and the skew-symmetricity of GBPM.Building on this insight and a feature diversity assumption, we establish two regret bounds via two simple algorithms: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, $e^{O(η)}$-free regret $\tilde{O}(ηd^4 (\log T)^2)$. (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves $\mathrm{poly}(d)$-free regret $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{ηr T})$ by exploiting the low-rank structure; this is the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high-dimensions.

</details>


### [318] [Bound to Disagree: Generalization Bounds via Certifiable Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23128)
*Mathieu Bazinet,Valentina Zantedeschi,Pascal Germain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes disagreement-based certificates to bound the true risk gap between predictors, using a surrogate model with tight generalization guarantees evaluated on unlabeled data; demonstrates this with surrogate frameworks based on sample compression, model compression, and PAC-Bayes, without modifying the target model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization bounds for deep learning are often vacuous or intractable. There is a need for computable, model-agnostic certificates that bound the true risk gap between any two predictors and can be evaluated without altering the target training procedure.

Method: Introduce disagreement-based certificates that bound the true risk difference between any two predictors. Bound the predictor of interest using a surrogate model with strong generalization guarantees. Evaluate the disagreement bound using unlabeled data. Implement surrogate models via three frameworks: (i) sample compression, (ii) model compression, (iii) PAC-Bayes.

Result: Empirically show that the certificates are tight. Demonstrate the approach's versatility by training surrogate models using the three frameworks, with the guarantees not requiring changes to the target model or training procedure.

Conclusion: The approach provides noninvasive, tight generalization certificates for the risk gap between predictors, leveraging unlabeled data and surrogate models across multiple theoretical frameworks, without modifying the target model.

Abstract: Generalization bounds for deep learning models are typically vacuous, not computable or restricted to specific model classes. In this paper, we tackle these issues by providing new disagreement-based certificates for the gap between the true risk of any two predictors. We then bound the true risk of the predictor of interest via a surrogate model that enjoys tight generalization guarantees, and evaluating our disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset. We empirically demonstrate the tightness of the obtained certificates and showcase the versatility of the approach by training surrogate models leveraging three different frameworks: sample compression, model compression and PAC-Bayes theory. Importantly, such guarantees are achieved without modifying the target model, nor adapting the training procedure to the generalization framework.

</details>


### [319] [DyGnROLE: Modeling Asymmetry in Dynamic Graphs with Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23135)
*Tyler Bonnet,Marek Rei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DyGnROLE separates source and destination roles with role-aware embeddings and TCLP pretraining, yielding strong gains on future edge classification in dynamic directed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directed dynamic graphs exhibit asymmetrical source/destination roles and temporal dynamics; existing methods largely rely on shared parameters and lack systematic role-aware modeling; low-label regimes necessitate self-supervised pretraining to learn robust role semantics.

Method: Transformer-based architecture with separate embedding vocabularies for source and destination nodes, role-semantic positional encodings, and Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP) pretraining using the full interaction history.

Result: Outperforms diverse state-of-the-art baselines on future edge classification, demonstrating the effectiveness of role-aware modeling in dynamic graphs.

Conclusion: Role-aware modeling with explicit source/destination disentanglement and TCLP pretraining improves dynamic graph learning, especially in low-label settings.

Abstract: Real-world dynamic graphs are often directed, with source and destination nodes exhibiting asymmetrical behavioral patterns and temporal dynamics. However, existing dynamic graph architectures largely rely on shared parameters for processing source and destination nodes, with limited or no systematic role-aware modeling. We propose DyGnROLE (Dynamic Graph Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding), a transformer-based architecture that explicitly disentangles source and destination representations. By using separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings, the model captures the distinct structural and temporal contexts unique to each role. Critical to the effectiveness of these specialized embeddings in low-label regimes is a self-supervised pretraining objective we introduce: Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP). The pretraining uses the full unlabeled interaction history to encode informative structural biases, enabling the model to learn role-specific representations without requiring annotated data. Evaluation on future edge classification demonstrates that DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, establishing role-aware modeling as an effective strategy for dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [320] [Prediction of Diffusion Coefficients in Mixtures with Tensor Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23142)
*Zeno Romero,Kerstin Münnemann,Hans Hasse,Fabian Jirasek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid tensor completion with Bayesian SEGWE priors for temperature-dependent diffusion at infinite dilution; extrapolates across 268–378 K; accuracy surpasses traditional models, enhanced by active learning data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Experimental diffusion data are sparse and multi-temperature predictions are challenging; matrix completion methods are typically single-temperature and data-limited, necessitating data-efficient, temperature-aware approaches.

Method: A Tucker-decomposition–based tensor completion model (TCM) trained on diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution for binary systems at 298, 313, and 333 K. SEGWE model serves as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. The model linearly extrapolates to 268–378 K. Active learning (AL) expands the dataset by acquiring new PFG NMR diffusion data (298, 313, 333 K) for 19 solute–solvent systems, further improving predictions.

Result: TCM delivers markedly improved predictions across all studied temperatures compared with established models. Incorporating AL data yields substantial accuracy gains.

Conclusion: Integrating data-efficient ML with adaptive experiments enables reliable, temperature-spanning predictions of diffusion at infinite dilution in binary mixtures.

Abstract: Predicting diffusion coefficients in mixtures is crucial for many applications, as experimental data remain scarce, and machine learning (ML) offers promising alternatives to established semi-empirical models. Among ML models, matrix completion methods (MCMs) have proven effective in predicting thermophysical properties, including diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures. However, MCMs are restricted to single-temperature predictions, and their accuracy depends strongly on the availability of high-quality experimental data for each temperature of interest. In this work, we address this challenge by presenting a hybrid tensor completion method (TCM) for predicting temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary mixtures. The TCM employs a Tucker decomposition and is jointly trained on experimental data for diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary systems at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Predictions from the semi-empirical SEGWE model serve as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. The TCM then extrapolates linearly to any temperature between 268 K and 378 K, achieving markedly improved prediction accuracy compared to established models across all studied temperatures. To further enhance predictive performance, the experimental database was expanded using active learning (AL) strategies for targeted acquisition of new diffusion data by pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR measurements. Diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in 19 solute + solvent systems were measured at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Incorporating these results yields a substantial improvement in the TCM's predictive accuracy. These findings highlight the potential of combining data-efficient ML methods with adaptive experimentation to advance predictive modeling of transport properties.

</details>


### [321] [Partial recovery of meter-scale surface weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23146)
*Jonathan Giezendanner,Qidong Yang,Eric Schmitt,Anirban Chandra,Daniel Salles Civitarese,Johannes Jakubik,Jeremy Vila,Detlef Hohl,Campbell Watson,Sherrie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Meter-scale near-surface weather fields across the contiguous US can be inferred by conditioning a coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface measurements and high-resolution Earth observations, achieving 10 m resolution and improving over ERA5 in wind, temperature, and dewpoint accuracy while revealing physically meaningful structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in current weather analyses that misses meter-scale variability driven by land cover and topography. Determine whether such variability is recoverable/predictable from surface features and large-scale forcing, and extend continental-scale weather modeling to fine scales.

Method: Condition a coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface station data and high-resolution Earth observation data to infer spatially continuous near-surface fields at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States, then compare against ERA5.

Result: Inferred 10 m fields show a 29% reduction in wind error and 6% reduction in temperature and dewpoint error relative to ERA5; explain more spatial variance at fixed times; exhibit interpretable patterns such as urban heat islands, ET-driven humidity contrasts, and land-cover dependent wind differences.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference and shows how conditioning coarse dynamical models on fine-scale static features can reveal previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

Abstract: Near-surface atmospheric conditions can differ sharply over tens to hundreds of meters due to land cover and topography, yet this variability is absent from current weather analyses and forecasts. It is unclear whether such meter-scale variability reflects irreducibly chaotic dynamics or contains a component predictable from surface characteristics and large-scale atmospheric forcing. Here we show that a substantial, physically coherent component of meter-scale near-surface weather is statistically recoverable from existing observations. By conditioning coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface station measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, we infer spatially continuous fields of near-surface wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States. Relative to ERA5, the inferred fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature and dewpoint error by 6%, while explaining substantially more spatial variance at fixed time steps. They also exhibit physically interpretable structure, including urban heat islands, evapotranspiration-driven humidity contrasts, and wind speed differences across land cover types. Our findings expand the frontier of weather modeling by demonstrating a computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference. More broadly, they illustrate how conditioning coarse dynamical models on static fine-scale features can reveal previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

</details>


### [322] [Benchmarking Temporal Web3 Intelligence: Lessons from the FinSurvival 2025 Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23159)
*Oshani Seneviratne,Fernando Spadea,Adrien Pavao,Aaron Micah Green,Kristin P. Bennett*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Web3 survival benchmarks: 16 tasks from 21.8M Aave v3 transactions; domain-aware temporal features beat generic models; advocates future Web3 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of shared, reproducible benchmarks that capture real-world temporal dynamics (censoring, non-stationarity) in temporal Web3 data, hindering progress and cross-domain method transfer.

Method: Benchmark design for the FinSurvival Challenge 2025, operationalizing 16 survival prediction tasks on 21.8 million Aave v3 transactions, with emphasis on domain-aware temporal feature construction and analysis of winning solutions.

Result: Domain-aware temporal features significantly outperformed generic modeling approaches; detailed benchmark design and winning strategies highlighted; extended horizon temporal dynamics demonstrated.

Conclusion: Web3 provides a high-fidelity sandbox for studying temporal challenges (churn, risk, evolution) and motivates next-generation, transferable temporal benchmarks for the wider Web.

Abstract: Temporal Web analytics increasingly relies on large-scale, longitudinal data to understand how users, content, and systems evolve over time. A rapidly growing frontier is the \emph{Temporal Web3}: decentralized platforms whose behavior is recorded as immutable, time-stamped event streams. Despite the richness of this data, the field lacks shared, reproducible benchmarks that capture real-world temporal dynamics, specifically censoring and non-stationarity, across extended horizons. This absence slows methodological progress and limits the transfer of techniques between Web3 and broader Web domains. In this paper, we present the \textit{FinSurvival Challenge 2025} as a case study in benchmarking \emph{temporal Web3 intelligence}. Using 21.8 million transaction records from the Aave v3 protocol, the challenge operationalized 16 survival prediction tasks to model user behavior transitions.We detail the benchmark design and the winning solutions, highlighting how domain-aware temporal feature construction significantly outperformed generic modeling approaches. Furthermore, we distill lessons for next-generation temporal benchmarks, arguing that Web3 systems provide a high-fidelity sandbox for studying temporal challenges, such as churn, risk, and evolution that are fundamental to the wider Web.

</details>


### [323] [MetaOthello: A Controlled Study of Multiple World Models in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23164)
*Aviral Chawla,Galen Hall,Juniper Lovato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaOthello introduces a controlled suite of Othello variants to study how transformers organize multiple world models within a shared representation space. Findings show predominantly shared board-state representations across variants with causal transfer, cross-variant linear probes being effective; isomorphic variants differ by an orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers; partially overlapping rules reveal layered specialization (early: game-agnostic, middle: game identity, late: specialization).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address mechanistic interpretability of how a single transformer can learn and organize multiple, potentially conflicting world models, advancing beyond studies of isolated game scenarios.

Method: Create MetaOthello: a suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but different rules/tokenizations; train small GPTs on mixed-variant data; analyze internal representations using linear probing, cross-variant interventions, and layer-wise analyses; examine token remappings and orthogonal transformations across layers.

Result: Transformers trained on mixed-variant data converge to a mostly shared board-state representation that transfers across variants. Linear probes from one variant can intervene on another's internal state with effectiveness near that of matched probes. Isomorphic variants with token remapping show representations are equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers. When rules partially overlap, early layers are game-agnostic, the middle layer identifies the game, and the later layers specialize.

Conclusion: MetaOthello provides a concrete framework to study the organization of multiple world models in transformers, showing that shared representations can support cross-variant transfer and that layer-specific roles emerge for different degrees of rule overlap.

Abstract: Foundation models must handle multiple generative processes, yet mechanistic interpretability largely studies capabilities in isolation; it remains unclear how a single transformer organizes multiple, potentially conflicting "world models". Previous experiments on Othello playing neural-networks test world-model learning but focus on a single game with a single set of rules. We introduce MetaOthello, a controlled suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but different rules or tokenizations, and train small GPTs on mixed-variant data to study how multiple world models are organized in a shared representation space. We find that transformers trained on mixed-game data do not partition their capacity into isolated sub-models; instead, they converge on a mostly shared board-state representation that transfers causally across variants. Linear probes trained on one variant can intervene on another's internal state with effectiveness approaching that of matched probes. For isomorphic games with token remapping, representations are equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers. When rules partially overlap, early layers maintain game-agnostic representations while a middle layer identifies game identity, and later layers specialize. MetaOthello offers a path toward understanding not just whether transformers learn world models, but how they organize many at once.

</details>


### [324] [Induction Meets Biology: Mechanisms of Repeat Detection in Protein Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23179)
*Gal Kesten-Pomeranz,Yaniv Nikankin,Anja Reusch,Tomer Tsaban,Ora Schueler-Furman,Yonatan Belinkov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLMs detect both exact and approximate protein repeats via a two-stage mechanism: (1) build representations with general positional attention and amino-acid similarity neurons; (2) induction heads align tokens across repeats to drive predictions. Approximate repeats use the same mechanism, effectively subsuming exact repeats. The work highlights a hybrid of language-model pattern matching and specialized biological knowledge, advancing understanding of PLMs on evolutionary-biological tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein repeats are prevalent and structurally/functional important; understanding how PLMs internally detect repeats clarifies model interpretability and the interplay between language-based priors and biology-specific knowledge.

Method: Probing PLMs’ masked-token prediction behavior to identify intermediate representations: general positional heads, specialized neurons encoding amino-acid similarity, and induction heads that attend to aligned repeated segments across the sequence. Comparative analysis of exact vs. approximate repeats to reveal mechanism.


Result: Two-stage mechanism established: (i) representation-building stage using both general and biology-specific components; (ii) alignment stage via induction heads that link aligned repeats across segments. The mechanism for approximate repeats subsumes that of exact repeats, indicating robustness of the learned process.

Conclusion: PLMs solve the biological task of repeat detection by combining language-based pattern matching with specialized biological knowledge, offering a basis to study more complex evolutionary processes in PLMs and informing interpretability of PLMs on biosequence tasks.

Abstract: Protein sequences are abundant in repeating segments, both as exact copies and as approximate segments with mutations. These repeats are important for protein structure and function, motivating decades of algorithmic work on repeat identification. Recent work has shown that protein language models (PLMs) identify repeats, by examining their behavior in masked-token prediction. To elucidate their internal mechanisms, we investigate how PLMs detect both exact and approximate repeats. We find that the mechanism for approximate repeats functionally subsumes that of exact repeats. We then characterize this mechanism, revealing two main stages: PLMs first build feature representations using both general positional attention heads and biologically specialized components, such as neurons that encode amino-acid similarity. Then, induction heads attend to aligned tokens across repeated segments, promoting the correct answer. Our results reveal how PLMs solve this biological task by combining language-based pattern matching with specialized biological knowledge, thereby establishing a basis for studying more complex evolutionary processes in PLMs.

</details>


### [325] [Closing the gap on tabular data with Fourier and Implicit Categorical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23182)
*Marius Dragoi,Florin Gogianu,Elena Burceanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feature preprocessing to identify discretized-target correlated features plus Learned Fourier regularization boosts DL on tabular data, achieving parity or superiority over XGBoost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular data remain challenging for deep learning while tree-based methods excel, partly due to their ability to exploit non-linear interactions from categorical features. DL models tend to apply uniform numerical processing and smooth solutions, limiting their ability to leverage such patterns.

Method: Use statistical feature processing to identify features strongly correlated with the target after discretization. Mitigate the bias of deep models toward overly smooth solutions using Learned Fourier. Train DL models with these preprocessing steps and compare to XGBoost on a comprehensive tabular benchmark.

Result: Deep learning models equipped with the proposed feature preprocessing and Learned Fourier approach achieve performance that closely matches or surpasses XGBoost on a broad tabular data benchmark.

Conclusion: A practical pipeline that reduces the tabular-data performance gap for DL by combining statistical discretization-driven feature selection with frequency-domain regularization, enabling DL models to approach or exceed tree-based methods on tabular tasks.

Abstract: While Deep Learning has demonstrated impressive results in applications on various data types, it continues to lag behind tree-based methods when applied to tabular data, often referred to as the last "unconquered castle" for neural networks. We hypothesize that a significant advantage of tree-based methods lies in their intrinsic capability to model and exploit non-linear interactions induced by features with categorical characteristics. In contrast, neural-based methods exhibit biases toward uniform numerical processing of features and smooth solutions, making it challenging for them to effectively leverage such patterns. We address this performance gap by using statistical-based feature processing techniques to identify features that are strongly correlated with the target once discretized. We further mitigate the bias of deep models for overly-smooth solutions, a bias that does not align with the inherent properties of the data, using Learned Fourier. We show that our proposed feature preprocessing significantly boosts the performance of deep learning models and enables them to achieve a performance that closely matches or surpasses XGBoost on a comprehensive tabular data benchmark.

</details>


### [326] [Efficient Real-Time Adaptation of ROMs for Unsteady Flows Using Data Assimilation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23188)
*Ismaël Zighed,Andrea Nóvoa,Luca Magri,Taraneh Sayadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficient, probabilistic ROM retraining across parameter regimes using a VAE+transformer architecture with ensemble Kalman filtering; latent-manifold distortions guide lightweight adaptation by retraining only the autoencoder, achieving full-retraining accuracy with sparse data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for fast, data-efficient adaptation of parameterized reduced-order models to unseen dynamical regimes with sparse observations, while providing uncertainty quantification and state reconstruction.

Method: An encode–process–decode architecture: a variational autoencoder reduces dimensionality; a transformer evolves latent states with attention to capture temporal dynamics and parameter effects (e.g., Reynolds number). The ROM is parameterized by Reynolds number; probabilistic sampling yields trajectory ensembles. Data assimilation via ensemble Kalman filtering (EnKF) uses sparse observations to reconstruct full trajectories. Crucially, out-of-sample adaptation can focus on retraining only the autoencoder (latent-manifold corrections) for real-time efficiency.

Result: The approach achieves accuracy comparable to full retraining but at a fraction of the computational cost, with probabilistic trajectories and uncertainty quantified through the VAE. Ensemble assimilation from sparse data improves state estimates. Latent-manifold distortions are identified as the dominant source of out-of-sample error, supporting lightweight autoencoder retraining rather than full latent-dynamics retraining.

Conclusion: For the studied dynamical system, the main prediction error under parameter variation arises from changes in the latent manifold shape rather than latent dynamics. This justifies restricting retraining to the autoencoder, enabling scalable, real-time adaptation with sparse measurements and probabilistic forecasts via EnKF. The framework is applicable to parameterized PDEs where sparse data and uncertainty quantification are essential.

Abstract: We propose an efficient retraining strategy for a parameterized Reduced Order Model (ROM) that attains accuracy comparable to full retraining while requiring only a fraction of the computational time and relying solely on sparse observations of the full system. The architecture employs an encode-process-decode structure: a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to perform dimensionality reduction, and a transformer network to evolve the latent states and model the dynamics. The ROM is parameterized by an external control variable, the Reynolds number in the Navier-Stokes setting, with the transformer exploiting attention mechanisms to capture both temporal dependencies and parameter effects. The probabilistic VAE enables stochastic sampling of trajectory ensembles, providing predictive means and uncertainty quantification through the first two moments. After initial training on a limited set of dynamical regimes, the model is adapted to out-of-sample parameter regions using only sparse data. Its probabilistic formulation naturally supports ensemble generation, which we employ within an ensemble Kalman filtering framework to assimilate data and reconstruct full-state trajectories from minimal observations. We further show that, for the dynamical system considered, the dominant source of error in out-of-sample forecasts stems from distortions of the latent manifold rather than changes in the latent dynamics. Consequently, retraining can be limited to the autoencoder, allowing for a lightweight, computationally efficient, real-time adaptation procedure with very sparse fine-tuning data.

</details>


### [327] [Tell Me What To Learn: Generalizing Neural Memory to be Controllable in Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23201)
*Max S. Bennett,Thomas P. Zollo,Richard Zemel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a generalized neural memory system that uses natural language instructions to guide flexible, selective updates from heterogeneous information streams, addressing fixed-objective memory limitations in non-stationary tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning systems operate in non-stationary environments requiring continual adaptation. Existing continual fine-tuning and in-context learning are costly; fixed-objective neural memory yields limited control over what is remembered.

Method: A generalized neural memory framework where updates are guided by learning instructions expressed in natural language, enabling selective integration from diverse information sources.

Result: Conceptual framework introducing instruction-guided memory updates; suggests improved adaptability and reduced forgetting, though empirical validation is not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: Enables adaptive agents to selectively learn from heterogeneous sources, extending neural memory to settings where fixed-objective updates are insufficient (e.g., healthcare, customer service).

Abstract: Modern machine learning models are deployed in diverse, non-stationary environments where they must continually adapt to new tasks and evolving knowledge. Continual fine-tuning and in-context learning are costly and brittle, whereas neural memory methods promise lightweight updates with minimal forgetting. However, existing neural memory models typically assume a single fixed objective and homogeneous information streams, leaving users with no control over what the model remembers or ignores over time. To address this challenge, we propose a generalized neural memory system that performs flexible updates based on learning instructions specified in natural language. Our approach enables adaptive agents to learn selectively from heterogeneous information sources, supporting settings, such as healthcare and customer service, where fixed-objective memory updates are insufficient.

</details>


### [328] [Takeuchi's Information Criteria as Generalization Measures for DNNs Close to NTK Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23219)
*Hiroki Naganuma,Taiji Suzuki,Rio Yokota,Masahiro Nomura,Kohta Ishikawa,Ikuro Sato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Takeuchi's information criterion (TIC) can explain generalization gaps for DNNs near the NTK regime but not far from it; TIC-based estimates correlate with generalization gap and enable efficient hyperparameter trial pruning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether TIC, a classical generalization measure, remains reliable for statistically singular models like DNNs, and to identify conditions (NTK regime) where it is effective.

Method: Theoretically analyze TIC under the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime; conduct large-scale empirical study with 5,000+ DNN models across 12 architectures (including VGG-16) on four datasets; apply several TIC approximations with different computational costs; assess correlation between TIC and generalization gap; evaluate TIC for hyperparameter trial pruning.

Result: Estimated TIC values correlate with the generalization gap under conditions close to the NTK regime; this correlation diminishes both theoretically and empirically outside the NTK regime; TIC demonstrates better effectiveness for pruning hyperparameter trials than existing methods.

Conclusion: TIC is applicable as a generalization measure mainly near the NTK regime, offering practical utility for model selection and hyperparameter optimization while highlighting its limitations outside this regime.

Abstract: Generalization measures have been studied extensively in the machine learning community to better characterize generalization gaps. However, establishing a reliable generalization measure for statistically singular models such as deep neural networks (DNNs) is difficult due to their complex nature. This study focuses on Takeuchi's information criterion (TIC) to investigate the conditions under which this classical measure can effectively explain the generalization gaps of DNNs. Importantly, the developed theory indicates the applicability of TIC near the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. In a series of experiments, we trained more than 5,000 DNN models with 12 architectures, including large models (e.g., VGG-16), on four datasets, and estimated the corresponding TIC values to examine the relationship between the generalization gap and the TIC estimates. We applied several TIC approximation methods with feasible computational costs and assessed the accuracy trade-off. Our experimental results indicate that the estimated TIC values correlate well with the generalization gap under conditions close to the NTK regime. However, we show both theoretically and empirically that outside the NTK regime such correlation disappears. Finally, we demonstrate that TIC provides better trial pruning ability than existing methods for hyperparameter optimization.

</details>


### [329] [Conformalized Neural Networks for Federated Uncertainty Quantification under Dual Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23296)
*Quang-Huy Nguyen,Jiaqi Wang,Wei-Shinn Ku*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedWQ-CP is a practical conformal prediction approach for federated learning that calibrates locally on each agent and aggregates a global quantile threshold in a single round, achieving agent-level and global coverage while minimizing communication and set sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for reliable uncertainty quantification in FL with joint data- and model-heterogeneity; conventional UQ methods struggle with coverage reliability across heterogeneous agents; conformal prediction offers distribution-free UQ but its application to heterogeneous FL is underexplored.

Method: On each agent: compute conformity scores on calibration data, derive a local quantile threshold; transmit only the threshold and calibration size to server. Server aggregates thresholds via weighted average to obtain a global threshold. Free of iterated calibration, one round. Applies to classification and regression; aims to preserve agent-wise and global coverage with small prediction sets/intervals.

Result: Seven public datasets experiments show FedWQ-CP maintains both agent-wise and global coverage; achieves smallest prediction sets or intervals, with simple communication overhead.

Conclusion: FedWQ-CP effectively balances coverage reliability and efficiency under dual heterogeneity in FL, offering a simple, scalable CP-based UQ mechanism with one-shot calibration and robust performance.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) faces challenges in uncertainty quantification (UQ). Without reliable UQ, FL systems risk deploying overconfident models at under-resourced agents, leading to silent local failures despite seemingly satisfactory global performance. Existing federated UQ approaches often address data heterogeneity or model heterogeneity in isolation, overlooking their joint effect on coverage reliability across agents. Conformal prediction is a widely used distribution-free UQ framework, yet its applications in heterogeneous FL settings remains underexplored. We provide FedWQ-CP, a simple yet effective approach that balances empirical coverage performance with efficiency at both global and agent levels under the dual heterogeneity. FedWQ-CP performs agent-server calibration in a single communication round. On each agent, conformity scores are computed on calibration data and a local quantile threshold is derived. Each agent then transmits only its quantile threshold and calibration sample size to the server. The server simply aggregates these thresholds through a weighted average to produce a global threshold. Experimental results on seven public datasets for both classification and regression demonstrate that FedWQ-CP empirically maintains agent-wise and global coverage while producing the smallest prediction sets or intervals.

</details>


### [330] [Inferential Mechanics Part 1: Causal Mechanistic Theories of Machine Learning in Chemical Biology with Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23303)
*Ilya Balabin,Thomas M. Kaiser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A three-paper series proposes a formal causal framework for ML in chemical biology, introducing the concept of 'focus' to reveal hidden mechanisms; Part 1 provides foundational structure and initial proof on Akt inhibitors. Parts 2 and 3 (announced) will address chemical similarity and extensive experimental validation, respectively, aiming to establish 'inferential mechanics' as a non-reductionist modeling framework for Nature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning in the natural sciences often treats predictions as black boxes without respecting underlying causal structure. This work seeks a unified, formal causal framework that integrates chemical biology, probability, and causality to correct causal flaws in ML and improve mechanistic understanding.

Method: Develop a formal causal framework for phenomena in chemical biology and extend it to ML via the novel concept of 'focus', enabling ML to narrow to a hidden underpinning mechanism. Provide initial proof on a family of Akt inhibitors to illustrate the approach.

Result: Proposes a foundational causal framework and the new 'focus' concept; presents initial proof on Akt inhibitors; outlines subsequent papers that will formalize chemical similarity (Part 2) and provide extensive experimental evidence (Part 3).

Conclusion: Aims to establish a new mathematical framework, termed 'inferential mechanics', for modeling natural mechanisms without reductionism, specifically within chemical biology, and to integrate this with ML for more causally faithful predictions.

Abstract: Machine learning techniques are now routinely encountered in research laboratories across the globe. Impressive progress has been made through ML and AI techniques with regards to large data set processing. This progress has increased the ability of the experimenter to digest data and make novel predictions regarding phenomena of interest. However, machine learning predictors generated from data sets taken from the natural sciences are often treated as black boxes which are used broadly and generally without detailed consideration of the causal structure of the data set of interest. Work has been attempted to bring causality into discussions of machine learning models of natural phenomena; however, a firm and unified theoretical treatment is lacking. This series of three papers explores the union of chemical theory, biological theory, probability theory and causality that will correct current causal flaws of machine learning in the natural sciences. This paper, Part 1 of the series, provides the formal framework of the foundational causal structure of phenomena in chemical biology and is extended to machine learning through the novel concept of focus, defined here as the ability of a machine learning algorithm to narrow down to a hidden underpinning mechanism in large data sets. Initial proof of these principles on a family of Akt inhibitors is also provided. The second paper containing Part 2 will provide a formal exploration of chemical similarity, and Part 3 will present extensive experimental evidence of how hidden causal structures weaken all machine learning in chemical biology. This series serves to establish for chemical biology a new kind of mathematical framework for modeling mechanisms in Nature without the need for the tools of reductionism: inferential mechanics.

</details>


### [331] [A Proper Scoring Rule for Virtual Staining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23305)
*Samuel Tonks,Steve Hood,Ryan Musso,Ceridwen Hopely,Steve Titus,Minh Doan,Iain Styles,Alexander Krull*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Information Gain (IG) as a cell-wise, strictly proper scoring rule for evaluating predicted posteriors in generative virtual staining, addressing limitations of marginal-distribution-focused metrics; demonstrates its usefulness on HTS data with diffusion and GAN models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: True posterior is unavailable; current evaluation protocols only assess data marginal distribution; need a metric that evaluates predicted posteriors per cell and is interpretable and comparable across models.

Method: Define IG as a scoring rule; apply to diffusion- and GAN-based VS models on an extensive HTS dataset; compute IG and compare with other metrics.

Result: IG reveals substantial performance differences that others miss; demonstrates IG's ability to reveal nuance in predicted posteriors and provide interpretability.

Conclusion: IG provides a principled, interpretable framework for evaluating predicted posteriors in VS models; facilitates cross-model and feature comparison; potentially preferable to marginal-distribution metrics.

Abstract: Generative virtual staining (VS) models for high-throughput screening (HTS) can provide an estimated posterior distribution of possible biological feature values for each input and cell. However, when evaluating a VS model, the true posterior is unavailable. Existing evaluation protocols only check the accuracy of the marginal distribution over the dataset rather than the predicted posteriors. We introduce information gain (IG) as a cell-wise evaluation framework that enables direct assessment of predicted posteriors. IG is a strictly proper scoring rule and comes with a sound theoretical motivation allowing for interpretability, and for comparing results across models and features. We evaluate diffusion- and GAN-based models on an extensive HTS dataset using IG and other metrics and show that IG can reveal substantial performance differences other metrics cannot.

</details>


### [332] [ParamMem: Augmenting Language Agents with Parametric Reflective Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23320)
*Tianjun Yao,Yongqiang Chen,Yujia Zheng,Pan Li,Zhiqiang Shen,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces ParamMem and ParamAgent to enhance reflective diversity in language agents; parametric memory encodes cross-sample reflection patterns, enabling diverse reflection generation via temperature-controlled sampling; empirical gains across code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-hop QA; evidence of sample efficiency and cross-model transfer, enabling self-improvement without stronger external models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-reflection can lead to repetitive outputs that degrade reasoning. Prior attempts to diversify reflections show promise, and there is a strong observed link between reflective diversity and task success, motivating a memory-based solution to capture diverse reflection signals.

Method: Develop ParamMem, a parametric memory module that encodes cross-sample reflection patterns into model parameters; use temperature-controlled sampling to generate diverse reflections; extend with ParamAgent integrating episodic and cross-sample memory; evaluate across code generation, math reasoning, and multi-hop QA against SOTA baselines.

Result: ParamMem demonstrated strong improvements over baselines, is sample-efficient, and supports weak-to-strong transfer across model scales; ParamAgent achieves consistent gains across tasks; shows self-improvement without relying on stronger external models.

Conclusion: ParamMem is a promising component for language agents, enabling diverse reflection generation and cross-task transfer, and can underpin scalable self-improvement without external model dependencies.

Abstract: Self-reflection enables language agents to iteratively refine solutions, yet often produces repetitive outputs that limit reasoning performance. Recent studies have attempted to address this limitation through various approaches, among which increasing reflective diversity has shown promise. Our empirical analysis reveals a strong positive correlation between reflective diversity and task success, further motivating the need for diverse reflection signals. We introduce ParamMem, a parametric memory module that encodes cross-sample reflection patterns into model parameters, enabling diverse reflection generation through temperature-controlled sampling. Building on this module, we propose ParamAgent, a reflection-based agent framework that integrates parametric memory with episodic and cross-sample memory. Extensive experiments on code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-hop question answering demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Further analysis reveals that ParamMem is sample-efficient, enables weak-to-strong transfer across model scales, and supports self-improvement without reliance on stronger external model, highlighting the potential of ParamMem as an effective component for enhancing language agents.

</details>


### [333] [Differentiable Zero-One Loss via Hypersimplex Projections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23336)
*Camilo Gomez,Pengyang Wang,Liansheng Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Soft-Binary-Argmax, a differentiable, order-preserving projection onto the hypersimplex that serves as a smooth surrogate for the zero-one loss. It provides an efficiently computable Jacobian and can be integrated into binary and multiclass models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The zero-one loss is non-differentiable and hampers gradient-based optimization. The authors aim to inject structured optimization priors via a differentiable projection to improve generalization and align training with task objectives, particularly under large-batch regimes where standard training struggles.

Method: Formulates a constrained optimization problem to project predictions onto the n,k-hypersimplex, yielding Soft-Binary-Argmax as a smooth, order-preserving operator. Derives its mathematical properties, provides an efficient Jacobian, and demonstrates integration into binary and multiclass learning systems.

Result: Empirically improves generalization in large-batch training by enforcing geometric consistency constraints on output logits, narrowing the performance gap between large- and small-batch regimes.

Conclusion: Soft-Binary-Argmax offers a differentiable, geometry-aware surrogate for the zero-one loss, enabling end-to-end optimization with structured priors and improved robustness to large-batch training in both binary and multiclass settings.

Abstract: Recent advances in machine learning have emphasized the integration of structured optimization components into end-to-end differentiable models, enabling richer inductive biases and tighter alignment with task-specific objectives. In this work, we introduce a novel differentiable approximation to the zero-one loss-long considered the gold standard for classification performance, yet incompatible with gradient-based optimization due to its non-differentiability. Our method constructs a smooth, order-preserving projection onto the n,k-dimensional hypersimplex through a constrained optimization framework, leading to a new operator we term Soft-Binary-Argmax. After deriving its mathematical properties, we show how its Jacobian can be efficiently computed and integrated into binary and multiclass learning systems. Empirically, our approach achieves significant improvements in generalization under large-batch training by imposing geometric consistency constraints on the output logits, thereby narrowing the performance gap traditionally observed in large-batch training.

</details>


### [334] [Mean Estimation from Coarse Data: Characterizations and Efficient Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23341)
*Alkis Kalavasis,Anay Mehrotra,Manolis Zampetakis,Felix Zhou,Ziyu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Characterization of when the Gaussian mean is identifiable from coarse data under convex partitions, and a polynomial-time estimator when identifiable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coarse observations arise in measurement rounding and sensor limits; identifiability is needed to recover the mean; previous work shows convexity matters and that non-convexity leads to NP-hardness; open questions remain about when identifiability holds and whether efficient algorithms exist.

Method: Theoretically analyze the identifiability conditions under convex partitions for Gaussian mean estimation; derive algorithmic approaches and prove computational efficiency under identifiability; connect geometry of partitions to estimation feasibility.

Result: Complete resolution of the two open questions: (1) provides necessary and sufficient conditions for identifiability under convex partitions; (2) develops a computationally efficient estimator (polynomial-time) under identifiability and convexity assumptions.

Conclusion: Under convex coarse observations, the mean is identifiable exactly under the stated geometric conditions, and when identifiable, efficient (polynomial-time) mean estimation is possible; this substantiates a clear boundary between identifiability and computational tractability for coarse Gaussian data.

Abstract: Coarse data arise when learners observe only partial information about samples; namely, a set containing the sample rather than its exact value. This occurs naturally through measurement rounding, sensor limitations, and lag in economic systems. We study Gaussian mean estimation from coarse data, where each true sample $x$ is drawn from a $d$-dimensional Gaussian distribution with identity covariance, but is revealed only through the set of a partition containing $x$. When the coarse samples, roughly speaking, have ``low'' information, the mean cannot be uniquely recovered from observed samples (i.e., the problem is not identifiable). Recent work by Fotakis, Kalavasis, Kontonis, and Tzamos [FKKT21] established that sample-efficient mean estimation is possible when the unknown mean is identifiable and the partition consists of only convex sets. Moreover, they showed that without convexity, mean estimation becomes NP-hard. However, two fundamental questions remained open: (1) When is the mean identifiable under convex partitions? (2) Is computationally efficient estimation possible under identifiability and convex partitions? This work resolves both questions. [...]

</details>


### [335] [FlashOptim: Optimizers for Memory Efficient Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23349)
*Jose Javier Gonzalez Ortiz,Abhay Gupta,Chris Renard,Davis Blalock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashOptim reduces per-parameter memory in mixed-precision training by over 50% using two techniques: tight-bound master weight quantization and companded 8-bit optimizer state, with 16-bit gradients, enabling memory-efficient training across SGD, AdamW, and Lion without quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard mixed-precision training incurs high memory from storing parameters, gradients, and optimizer state. Even with 4-byte values per quantity, large models (e.g., billions of parameters) are memory-prohibitive on modest accelerators; reducing memory per parameter without compromising accuracy is crucial for enabling wider research.

Method: Two main techniques: (1) tight-bound master weight splitting to bound quantization error; (2) companding functions to reduce error in 8-bit optimizer state quantization. Use these with 16-bit gradients. Reported memory reduction: AdamW memory from 16 bytes/param to 7 bytes/param, 5 bytes with gradient release. Also halved checkpoint sizes. Evaluations on SGD, AdamW, Lion across standard vision and language benchmarks including Llama-3.1-8B finetuning.

Result: No measurable quality degradation across a suite of standard benchmarks on vision and language tasks, including Llama-3.1-8B finetuning, when applying FlashOptim to SGD, AdamW, and Lion.

Conclusion: FlashOptim offers a practical, API-compatible memory-saving approach that reduces per-parameter memory by >50% while preserving model quality, applicable across common optimizers and leading to smaller checkpoint sizes.

Abstract: Standard mixed-precision training of neural networks requires many bytes of accelerator memory for each model parameter. These bytes reflect not just the parameter itself, but also its gradient and one or more optimizer state variables. With each of these values typically requiring 4 bytes, training even a 7 billion parameter model can be impractical for researchers with less than 100GB of accelerator memory.
  We introduce FlashOptim, a suite of optimizations that reduces per-parameter memory by over 50% while preserving model quality and API compatibility. Our approach introduces two key techniques. First, we improve master weight splitting by finding and exploiting a tight bound on its quantization error. Second, we design companding functions that greatly reduce the error in 8-bit optimizer state quantization. Together with 16-bit gradients, these techniques reduce AdamW memory from 16 bytes to 7 bytes per parameter, or 5 bytes with gradient release. They also cut model checkpoint sizes by more than half.
  Experiments with FlashOptim applied to SGD, AdamW, and Lion show no measurable quality degradation on any task from a collection of standard vision and language benchmarks, including Llama-3.1-8B finetuning.

</details>


### [336] [SOTAlign: Semi-Supervised Alignment of Unimodal Vision and Language Models via Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23353)
*Simon Roschmann,Paul Krzakala,Sonia Mazelet,Quentin Bouniot,Zeynep Akata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOTAlign is a two-stage semi-supervised framework that aligns pretrained unimodal encoders with limited image-text pairs and abundant unpaired data. It first uses a linear teacher to recover coarse shared geometry, then refines the alignment with an optimal-transport-based divergence that transfers relational structure, outperforming supervised and semi-supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve meaningful cross-modal alignment with substantially reduced supervision, leveraging unpaired data and relational structure to support the Platonic Representation Hypothesis.

Method: Stage 1: Train a linear teacher to recover coarse shared geometry from a small set of paired image-text samples. Stage 2: Refine alignment on unpaired data using an optimal-transport-based divergence that preserves relational structure and avoids over-constraining the target space. The approach uses lightweight alignment layers on frozen pretrained unimodal encoders and learns joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs.

Result: Significant improvement over both supervised and semi-supervised baselines. The method learns robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs by effectively leveraging unpaired images and text, outperforming existing semi-supervised methods.

Conclusion: SOTAlign demonstrates that meaningful cross-modal alignment can be achieved with substantially less supervision by combining a coarse geometry stage with OT-based relational refinement, supporting the Platonic Representation Hypothesis and broad applicability to multimodal alignment with limited paired data.

Abstract: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis posits that neural networks trained on different modalities converge toward a shared statistical model of the world. Recent work exploits this convergence by aligning frozen pretrained vision and language models with lightweight alignment layers, but typically relies on contrastive losses and millions of paired samples. In this work, we ask whether meaningful alignment can be achieved with substantially less supervision. We introduce a semi-supervised setting in which pretrained unimodal encoders are aligned using a small number of image-text pairs together with large amounts of unpaired data. To address this challenge, we propose SOTAlign, a two-stage framework that first recovers a coarse shared geometry from limited paired data using a linear teacher, then refines the alignment on unpaired samples via an optimal-transport-based divergence that transfers relational structure without overconstraining the target space. Unlike existing semi-supervised methods, SOTAlign effectively leverages unpaired images and text, learning robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs, and significantly outperforming supervised and semi-supervised baselines.

</details>


### [337] [A Dataset is Worth 1 MB](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23358)
*Elad Kimchi Shoshani,Leeyam Gabay,Yedid Hoshen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLADA eliminates pixel transmission by sending only class labels for a pruned subset of a large unlabeled reference dataset, enabling task transfer with payloads under 1 MB across diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce communication costs and accommodate heterogeneous client hardware by avoiding pixel-level data transfer; enable local model training using task-specific labels derived from a shared reference dataset.

Method: Preload a large unlabeled reference dataset; for a new task, prune the reference to retain images whose labels are most semantically relevant to the target task; transmit only the class labels for those images; allow agents to train their own models locally using these labels as supervision.

Result: Experiments on 10 diverse datasets show successful task transfer with payloads under 1 MB while preserving high accuracy.

Conclusion: PLADA offers a scalable, low-bandwidth dataset-serving paradigm by substituting pixel data with semantically relevant class-label signals, addressing distribution mismatch via pruning.

Abstract: A dataset server must often distribute the same large payload to many clients, incurring massive communication costs. Since clients frequently operate on diverse hardware and software frameworks, transmitting a pre-trained model is often infeasible; instead, agents require raw data to train their own task-specific models locally. While dataset distillation attempts to compress training signals, current methods struggle to scale to high-resolution data and rarely achieve sufficiently small files. In this paper, we propose Pseudo-Labels as Data (PLADA), a method that completely eliminates pixel transmission. We assume agents are preloaded with a large, generic, unlabeled reference dataset (e.g., ImageNet-1K, ImageNet-21K) and communicate a new task by transmitting only the class labels for specific images. To address the distribution mismatch between the reference and target datasets, we introduce a pruning mechanism that filters the reference dataset to retain only the labels of the most semantically relevant images for the target task. This selection process simultaneously maximizes training efficiency and minimizes transmission payload. Experiments on 10 diverse datasets demonstrate that our approach can transfer task knowledge with a payload of less than 1 MB while retaining high classification accuracy, offering a promising solution for efficient dataset serving.

</details>


### [338] [Model Agreement via Anchoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23360)
*Eric Eaton,Surbhi Goel,Marcel Hussing,Michael Kearns,Aaron Roth,Sikata Bela Sengupta,Jessica Sorrell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Anchoring-based bounds on independent model disagreement; shows zero disagreement as stack size, iterations, architecture size, or tree depth increase, across four training paradigms; extends from 1D squared loss to multi-dimensional, strongly convex losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and bound how much two independently trained models disagree, and to provide a simple, general method that can drive this disagreement toward zero using standard training settings; with practical relevance to stability and ensemble design.

Method: Develops anchoring-to-average analysis to bound expected squared prediction differences; proves bounds for four algorithms: stacked aggregation, gradient boosting, neural architecture search, and depth-limited regression trees; begins with 1D regression with squared loss and extends to multi-dimensional cases with strongly convex losses.

Result: Disagreement is driven to zero as the controlling parameter grows (number of models k, iterations k, architecture size n, tree depth d) for each setting; the bounds hold under the strongly convex loss generalization.

Conclusion: The anchoring framework generalizes to multi-dimensional settings and provides a unifying method to bound and potentially reduce model disagreement across common training paradigms, linking training complexity to prediction agreement.

Abstract: Numerous lines of aim to control $\textit{model disagreement}$ -- the extent to which two machine learning models disagree in their predictions. We adopt a simple and standard notion of model disagreement in real-valued prediction problems, namely the expected squared difference in predictions between two models trained on independent samples, without any coordination of the training processes. We would like to be able to drive disagreement to zero with some natural parameter(s) of the training procedure using analyses that can be applied to existing training methodologies.
  We develop a simple general technique for proving bounds on independent model disagreement based on $\textit{anchoring}$ to the average of two models within the analysis. We then apply this technique to prove disagreement bounds for four commonly used machine learning algorithms: (1) stacked aggregation over an arbitrary model class (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of models $k$ being stacked) (2) gradient boosting (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of iterations $k$) (3) neural network training with architecture search (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the size $n$ of the architecture being optimized over) and (4) regression tree training over all regression trees of fixed depth (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the depth $d$ of the tree architecture). For clarity, we work out our initial bounds in the setting of one-dimensional regression with squared error loss -- but then show that all of our results generalize to multi-dimensional regression with any strongly convex loss.

</details>
